The backend's built snapshot at fresh launch contains only currently-running apps (the backend starts from .empty, not from disk), so an app X that is not running at relaunch is absent from liveSession and is therefore DROPPED from session.apps by mergedSession. start() then calls persistSessionSnapshot() unconditionally (AppStore.swift:217), overwriting the on-disk session with one that no longer contains X — permanently losing X's saved boost/volume/mute/targetDeviceUID. reapplyRestoredAudioState (AppStore.swift:820-846) cannot help because X is already gone from session.apps. This directly violates BOOST-04 acceptance #2 ('An app not running at relaunch keeps its saved boost'). | Fix: In mergedSession, after merging live apps, append any cached apps whose logicalID is not present in liveSession (carrying them as monitorOnly/retained entries) so saved settings survive until the app is rediscovered. Mirror the backend's isStillRunning-style retention, or keep a separate 'remembered customizations' store keyed by logicalID that is reattached on rediscovery rather than living only in the volatile session snapshot.
• [HIGH/correctness] refresh() assigns the backend snapshot directly with no cache reconciliation, dropping cached-only per-app boost/state (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:242) — refresh() does session = try await backend.refresh() (AppStore.swift:242) and assigns the backend snapshot DIRECTLY, unlike start() which routes through mergedSession. backend.refresh -> buildSnapshot(merging: previousSnapshot) (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:48-51, 999-1014) only preserves boost the backend already held in its own previous snapshot. Any per-app state that exists in AppStore.session but not in the backend snapshot (e.g. an app whose boost was reinjected into the AppStore session but never accepted by the backend because backend.setVolumeBoost threw appNotFound for a not-yet-running app in reapplyRestoredAudioState, AppStore.swift:833) is lost when refresh overwrites session, and persistSessionSnapshot() (AppStore.swift:245) then writes the loss to disk. Refresh is a prominent button (MainWindowView, MenuBarMixerView) and is also reachable via the waves://refresh URL command (AppStore.swift:284-285). togglePinned (AppStore.swift:596) and handleDeviceChange (AppStore.swift:799) similarly do session = await backend.currentSnapshot() with no reconciliation, so the same class of loss occurs on pin/unpin and device change. | Fix: In refresh(), reconcile like bootstrap: session = mergedSession(with: try await backend.refresh(), cached: session) (after fixing mergedSession to retain cached-only apps), and re-run reapplyRestoredAudioState() for newly-appearing customized apps before persistSessionSnapshot(). Apply the same reconciliation in togglePinned and handleDeviceChange.
• [MEDIUM/edge-case] Per-device boost is not restored on device switch when auto-restore-device is disabled (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:793) — restoreDeviceVolumePresets (AppStore.swift:848) — the only place a saved per-device boost is re-applied on a device switch — is called exclusively from handleDeviceChange (AppStore.swift:803). handleDeviceChange begins with guard preferences.autoRestoreDevice else { return } (AppStore.swift:793), so if autoRestoreDevice is OFF while enablePerDeviceVolumePresets is ON, a device change never reaches the per-device restore branch (AppStore.swift:802-804). These are two independent Settings toggles, both defaulting true (UserPreferences.swift:9,12), so the OFF/ON combination is a reachable, legitimate configuration. selectOutputDevice (AppStore.swift:759) relies on the device-change event funneling through handleDeviceChange, so manual switches are affected too. This violates BOOST-04 acceptance #3. | Fix: Decouple the per-device-preset restore from the autoRestoreDevice guard. Split handleDeviceChange into a route-restore part gated by autoRestoreDevice and a preset-restore part gated only by enablePerDeviceVolumePresets, so the enablePerDeviceVolumePresets block runs on device change regardless of autoRestoreDevice.
• [LOW/inconsistency] AudioApp clamps volumeBoost to [0.0,10.0] instead of the documented user-facing range [1.0,4.0] (Sources/WavesAudioCore/Models/AudioApp.swift:88) — AudioApp's memberwise init clamps volumeBoost with max(0.0, min(10.0, volumeBoost)) (AudioApp.swift:88). init(from:) (AudioApp.swift:118-165) decodes volumeBoost defaulting to 1.0 when absent and then routes through the memberwise init, so a persisted value is clamped to [0.0,10.0], NOT to the user-facing [1.0,4.0]. Every code path that SETS a boost clamps to [1.0,4.0] (AppStore.setVolumeBoost:548, backend.setVolumeBoost:118, controller.apply:1653 / setVolumeBoost:1661), and PresetEntry/AppVolumeSettings clamp to [1.0,4.0] on decode. But a tampered/corrupted session file (loaded at AppStore.swift:83) can carry a per-app boost in [0.0,1.0) or (4.0,10.0] straight into session.apps, which BoostMenu renders verbatim as Text("\(Int(app.volumeBoost))x") (MixerRowView.swift:376) — showing non-selectable labels like '0x', '7x', or '10x'. The backend re-clamps to [1.0,4.0] on the next apply and cycleBoost self-heals, so audio is safe, but the stale UI label persists until the boost is re-set. | Fix: Clamp volumeBoost to [1.0,4.0] in AudioApp's memberwise init (change max(0.0, min(10.0, volumeBoost)) to max(1.0, min(4.0, volumeBoost))), matching PresetEntry and AppVolumeSettings; since init(from:) routes through the memberwise init, this also clamps on decode.

Feature: Per-app managed routing via Core Audio process taps
User Story: As a macOS user, I want Waves to take over a specific app's audio with a Core Audio process tap, so that I can control that one app's playback independently from system volume without installing a kernel/audio driver.
Expected Behavior:
• applyRoute resolves the app's audible Core Audio process objects from its bundle family PIDs and/or its own PID via kAudioHardwarePropertyTranslatePIDToProcessObject (resolveProcessObjectIDs).
• createController builds a private CATapDescription (stereoMixdownOfProcesses) with mutedWhenTapped behavior, creates a process tap (AudioHardwareCreateProcessTap), reads its UID, then creates a private aggregate device whose main/clock/sub-device is the chosen output device and whose tap list includes the tap UID.
• A PerAppTapController starts an IO proc (AudioDeviceCreateIOProcIDWithBlock) on the aggregate device, configures input+output stream usage, and starts the device; success marks the app routingState=.managed.
• The aggregate routes to app.targetDeviceUID when set (per-app output device) or to the current system default output otherwise (currentDefaultOutputDeviceUID).
• If the app already has an active controller matching the same process object IDs, the existing controller is reused and just re-applied instead of rebuilt (applyRoute fast path).
• Waves' own private aggregate devices (UID prefix com.waves.aggregate.) and aggregates named 'waves' are filtered out of user-selectable output devices.
• When the chosen targetDeviceUID is no longer available, createController throws managedRouteUnavailable with a message telling the user to pick another output device instead of silently falling back.
Acceptance Criteria:
• Given a 14.2+ system with capture granted and a running audible app, when the user adjusts that app, then a process tap + private aggregate device + running IO proc are created and the app shows routingState=.managed.
• Given an app with no resolvable Core Audio process objects, when applyRoute runs, then it throws managedRouteUnavailable and the app is marked .error with a note (not silently 'managed').
• Given an app pinned to a targetDeviceUID that has been unplugged, when a route is (re)built, then it throws managedRouteUnavailable referencing the Output Device menu rather than routing to the default device.
• Waves' own aggregate devices never appear in availableOutputDevices().
Impl Notes: Full real Core Audio tap+aggregate+IOProc pipeline, not a stub. Honest failure handling: partial-create leaks are cleaned up (tap destroyed if UID read or aggregate creation fails; controller disposed if start() fails). One latent concern: resolveProcessObjectIDs unions the WHOLE bundle family's process objects into a single stereoMixdown tap, so a multi-process app (e.g. browser helpers) is tapped as a group — correct for grouping but means the tap set can drift as helper PIDs come/go, forcing rebuilds. translateProcessID throws on any non-noErr status, so one bad PID in the set aborts resolution rather than skipping that PID (the compactMap can still throw out of the closure).
Errors Found: • [MEDIUM/edge-case] One untranslatable sibling PID aborts whole process-object resolution for multi-process apps (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:546) — resolveProcessObjectIDs maps the bundle family's candidatePIDs with `try candidatePIDs.compactMap { ... try translateProcessID(forPID:) ... }` (line 546-552). translateProcessID (line 843-867) routes every OSStatus through withStatusCheck (line 1335-1338), which throws BackendError.managedRouteUnavailable on ANY non-noErr status (e.g. kAudioHardwareBadObjectError for a PID that has no Core Audio process object yet — common for transient helper/renderer processes in a browser family). Because the compactMap closure is `throws`, that single failure propagates out of the whole resolution, so the route is aborted even though other PIDs in the family resolve cleanly. The .pid-only fallback at line 559 is never reached because the throw escapes before line 555. | Fix: Make per-PID translation non-throwing inside the map (wrap translateProcessID in `try?`, or a do/catch that logs and returns nil) so a failing PID is skipped. Only throw managedRouteUnavailable when the resolved set is empty (the existing empty-check at 555/563 already covers that).

Feature: Real-time per-app volume and mute on tapped audio
User Story: As a user, I want to set a managed app's volume and mute it independently, so that I can quiet or silence one app while everything else keeps playing.
Expected Behavior:
• setDesiredVolume clamps to [0,1] (NaN/inf falls back to 1.0), stores desiredVolume, re-applies the route, and sets appliedVolume to 0 when muted else the target.
• setMuted stores isMuted, re-applies, and zeroes peak/rms + appliedVolume when muted.
• The IO render proc reads a lock-protected TapRenderState; if inactive, muted, or volume==0 it writes silence (zeroOutput) for that cycle.
• Otherwise it memcpy's tapped input to output and scales output in place via TapDSP.scale by effectiveVolume = volume * volumeBoost, with TapDSP clamping float to [-1,1] and saturating int16/int32 instead of overflowing.
• Volume/mute updates are pushed to the realtime thread asynchronously on the controller's callback queue (apply -> writeVolumeAndMute) so the caller never blocks the audio thread; values are clamped (volume [0,1], boost [1,4]) before write.
• On any apply error the app is marked routingState=.error, notes/backendStatus.lastError carry error.localizedDescription, and isRouteRecoveryHealthy=false.
Acceptance Criteria:
• Setting volume 0.5 on a managed app halves its output samples (TapDSP.scale gain 0.5 -> linear), verified by TapDSPTests float32AttenuationScalesLinearly.
• Muting a managed app produces silence on its aggregate output and zeroes its meters.
• Passing NaN/inf volume does not crash and results in desiredVolume defaulting to 1.0.
• A failed re-apply leaves the app in .error with a user-visible note and clears isRouteRecoveryHealthy.
Impl Notes: Core mixer behavior is fully wired and unit-tested at the DSP layer. Note: setMuted on success path does NOT recompute appliedVolume from the re-applied state in the same way as desiredVolume edits, but it does set appliedVolume = isMuted ? 0 : desiredVolume, which is consistent. Minor UX gap: setDesiredVolume's success path sets appliedVolume but does not reset peak/rms; muting resets them, unmuting relies on the next level poll to repopulate.
Errors Found:
• [LOW/correctness] NaN/inf volume sets desiredVolume=1.0 but skips re-apply and leaves appliedVolume/routingState stale (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:59) — In setDesiredVolume the `guard volume.isFinite else { ...; snapshot.apps[index].desiredVolume = 1.0; return }` branch (lines 59-63) returns immediately: it does NOT call applyRoute and does NOT update appliedVolume or routingState. If the app is currently managed at e.g. 0.3, the live tap keeps scaling by 0.3 while the model now reports desiredVolume=1.0 and appliedVolume=0.3. This is a real divergence from the story's stated behavior, but downgraded from medium to low because the only way a non-finite value reaches this method is a programmatic/edge caller (the UI slider is bounded 0...1), so it has near-zero practical reach and the AC 'does not crash and defaults to 1.0' is still met. | Fix: Treat NaN/inf as target = 1.0 and fall through to the normal apply path instead of returning early: `let target: Float = volume.isFinite ? max(0,min(1,volume)) : 1.0`, store it, then run the same applyRoute + appliedVolume/routingState update block.
• [LOW/inconsistency] Muting leaves the snapshot's peak/rms stale after the next poll (model-level only; no UI effect) (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1393) — setMuted zeroes snapshot.apps[i].peakLevel/rmsLevel on success (lines 95-96), but the render proc short-circuits to zeroOutput when isMuted!=0 (line 1695-1698) and never calls writeLevels, so the controller's stateBox keeps the last pre-mute peak/rms. updateAudioLevels() (line 1393-1402) then copies controller.getCurrentLevels() back into the snapshot with no isMuted guard, repopulating the muted app's peak/rms ~0.25s later, and audioLevels() (line 1098) re-exposes them. HOWEVER, the first tester's 'meters are re-shown to the UI' claim is a MISREAD: every meter/indicator surface gates on !app.isMuted — showsLevelMeter (MixerRowView.swift:174), the 'Playing audio' subtitle (MixerRowView.swift:187), liveApps (AppStore.swift:120), and activityRank (AppStore.swift:1308). So a muted app never renders a meter regardless of the stale value. This is a latent model/data inconsistency with no user-visible effect, hence low (not medium). The genuinely user-visible variant is the volume-0 case captured under TAP-04. | Fix: In updateAudioLevels, skip/zero muted apps (`if snapshot.apps[index].isMuted { peak = 0; rms = 0 }`), or have the controller zero its stateBox levels when it becomes muted (writeLevels(0,0) in the muted branch / when muted transitions true).

Feature: Per-app volume boost (1x-4x) with clip protection
User Story: As a user with a too-quiet app, I want to boost its volume up to 4x above unity, so that I can hear it without raising every other app or my system volume.
Expected Behavior:
• setVolumeBoost clamps the boost to [1.0, 4.0], stores volumeBoost, updates the live controller (setVolumeBoost), and re-applies the route.
• In the render proc, effectiveVolume = volume * volumeBoost is applied via TapDSP.scale, so boost multiplies the gain on the tapped buffer.
• TapDSP.scale clamps boosted float samples to [-1,1] and saturates int16/int32 at their min/max so boost cannot emit out-of-range / overflowed samples that clip harshly.
• Presets persist and re-apply volumeBoost per app (applyPreset / saveCurrentPreset include volumeBoost).
• AudioApp clamps stored volumeBoost to [0,10] at the model layer, while the backend/controller enforce the user-facing [1,4] range.
Acceptance Criteria:
• Boost 4x on a 0.5-volume app saturates to full-scale rather than wrapping (TapDSPTests float32BoostClampsToValidRange, int16/int32 saturation tests).
• Requesting boost 6x is clamped to 4x; requesting 0.5x is clamped to 1x.
• A saved preset restores the same boost value when re-applied.
Impl Notes: Boost is real (applied in the render path) and clip-protected, matching the README's '2x/3x/4x boost' claim. Redundancy concern: setVolumeBoost both calls controller.setVolumeBoost(clampedBoost) AND re-runs applyRoute (which calls controller.apply with app.volumeBoost) — two writes to the realtime state for one user action. setVolumeBoost reads currentState synchronously then writes asynchronously, so a concurrent volume change between read and the queued write could momentarily clobber volume/mute with a slightly stale value (cosmetic, self-corrects on next apply). int16/int32 boost rounds via .rounded() which is fine; unknown sample format silently no-ops scaling (boost has no effect, audio passes through at unity).
Errors Found: • [LOW/logic] setVolumeBoost double-writes realtime state (controller.setVolumeBoost + applyRoute) for one action (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:122) — setVolumeBoost calls controller.setVolumeBoost(clampedBoost) (line 123) — which reads currentState synchronously and queues an async writeVolumeAndMute — then immediately calls applyRoute (line 128), which on the fast path (line 367-369) calls controller.apply(volume:, volumeBoost: app.volumeBoost, muted:) queuing a SECOND async write with the same boost (snapshot.apps[index].volumeBoost was already set to clampedBoost at line 119, so apply carries the correct value). Both target the same realtime state for one user action; the first write is fully redundant and, because setVolumeBoost snapshots volume/muted at read time and writes them back later, an interleaved volume/mute change between its read and its queued write can momentarily clobber the newer value (self-correcting on the subsequent apply). Confirmed; cosmetic, so low. | Fix: Drop the explicit controller.setVolumeBoost(clampedBoost) call and rely solely on applyRoute (which already passes the updated app.volumeBoost into controller.apply).

Feature: Live per-app peak/RMS level metering from the tap render path
User Story: As a user, I want to see live audio levels for each managed app, so that I can tell which app is actually making sound and confirm my routing is working.
Expected Behavior:
• The render proc computes peak + sum-of-squares over the original (pre-scale) input via TapDSP.levels, normalizes int formats to [0,1], and writes peak/RMS into the shared state using a non-blocking try-lock (writeLevels skips the update if contended so the realtime thread never blocks).
• A backend level-update task polls controllers every 0.25s and copies each active controller's getCurrentLevels() into the matching app's peakLevel/rmsLevel.
• audioLevels() returns levels keyed by logicalID only for apps in .managed or .live state, intended for visibility-gated UI polling.
• RMS is sqrt(sum/sampleCount) and is defined as 0 (not NaN) when sampleCount is 0.
• Muting an app or disposing its controller zeroes its meters.
Acceptance Criteria:
• A known float buffer [1,-1,1,-1] yields peak 1.0 and RMS 1.0 (TapDSPTests levelsComputesPeakAndRMSForKnownFloatInput).
• Silence yields peak 0 and RMS 0; zero samples yield RMS 0 not NaN (levelsOfSilence / rmsOfZeroSamplesIsZeroNotNaN).
• int16 full-scale samples normalize to ~1.0 peak (int16LevelsNormalizeToUnitRange).
• audioLevels() omits apps that are not managed/live.
Impl Notes: Meters are derived from real tapped audio and the realtime path is correctly non-blocking (try-lock on writeLevels, sync read on the poll side). Subtlety: levels are computed from the INPUT buffer before volume/boost scaling, so meters reflect source loudness, not post-fader output — reasonable but worth noting for UX expectations. The 0.25s poll cadence runs whenever any controller exists (it is not itself visibility-gated inside the backend; the comment says callers should gate). audioLevels() includes .live apps, but .live apps have no controller, so their peak/rms stay 0 until they become managed.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/correctness] A managed app set to 0% volume (unmuted) keeps a frozen non-zero meter and false 'Playing audio' indicator (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1396) — When a managed app's volume is set to 0 (not muted), the render proc short-circuits at `if volume == 0.0 { zeroOutput; return }` (line 1702-1704) and never reaches writeLevels (line 1937), so the controller's stateBox keeps the last non-zero peak/rms. setDesiredVolume's success path (lines 65-74) does NOT zero peak/rms (only setMuted does). updateAudioLevels (line 1393-1402) then copies the stale levels back and audioLevels()/store.liveLevels keep returning them. Crucially, unlike the muted case, NO UI surface gates on volume==0: showsLevelMeter (MixerRowView.swift:174) and the 'Playing audio' subtitle (MixerRowView.swift:187) gate only on !isMuted, and liveApps (AppStore.swift:120) / activityRank (AppStore.swift:1308) require only `!app.isMuted && max(peakLevel,rmsLevel) > 0.001`. So a silent 0%-volume managed app renders a frozen cyan level meter, shows 'Playing audio', and stays sorted in the Live section indefinitely. This is the genuinely user-visible form of the stale-meter bug the first tester reported only for the (fully UI-gated, inert) muted case. | Fix: In updateAudioLevels, zero peak/rms when the app is effectively silent (snapshot.apps[index].isMuted || snapshot.apps[index].appliedVolume == 0), or have the render proc write zero levels (writeLevels(0,0)) in the muted and volume==0 short-circuit branches so getCurrentLevels() refreshes to 0.
• [LOW/inconsistency] Level poller does not refresh meters for muted apps (model-level stale, no UI effect) (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1396) — Same root cause as TAP-02's second issue: updateAudioLevels (line 1393-1402) copies controller.getCurrentLevels() with no isMuted guard, and the muted render path (line 1695-1698) never updates the stateBox, so a muted app's snapshot peak/rms is repopulated with the frozen pre-mute value and audioLevels() re-exposes it. As established, all meter/indicator surfaces gate on !app.isMuted (MixerRowView.swift:174/187, AppStore.swift:120/1308), so the muted case is not actually shown to the user — hence low (not medium as first reported). The user-visible defect is the volume-0 case above. | Fix: Zero peak/rms for muted apps in updateAudioLevels, or zero the controller's stateBox levels when it becomes muted.

Feature: Capability and capture-permission gating with diagnostics
User Story: As a user on an unsupported OS or without audio-capture permission, I want Waves to honestly tell me per-app control can't take effect and how to fix it, so that I'm not misled into thinking routing works when it silently does nothing.
Expected Behavior:
• supportsPerAppRouting requires macOS 14.2+; on older OS applyRoute/createController throw unsupportedOperation and every app is forced to monitorOnly with note 'Per-app route requires macOS 14.2+' and compatibility .planned.
• refreshCaptureAuthorization probes real TCC capture by creating and immediately destroying a private global process tap (no IO proc, no audio captured): noErr => .authorized, any failure => .notGranted, pre-14.2 => .unsupported; the result caches on captureAuthorization.
• buildSnapshot re-probes capture authorization every refresh so backendStatus.hasRequiredPermissions reflects actual capture grant, and isRouteRecoveryHealthy requires support AND authorized AND no route errors.
• diagnosticsReport surfaces checks for audio component (OS support), capture permission (passed/failed/warning with System Settings guidance), accessibility permission, route recovery health, and support-matrix coverage.
• When capture is not granted, the diagnostics detail directs the user to System Settings > Privacy & Security > Microphone.
Acceptance Criteria:
• On macOS < 14.2, no taps are created; all apps are monitorOnly/planned and diagnostics report per-app routing unsupported.
• With capture denied, refreshCaptureAuthorization returns .notGranted and the capture diagnostics check is .failed with actionable text.
• backendStatus.hasRequiredPermissions is true only when captureAuthorization == .authorized.
• The capability probe creates no lasting tap (it destroys the probe tap immediately).
Impl Notes: The capture probe is a genuinely clever and honest TCC check (create+destroy a global tap) rather than guessing from UI state, and it is re-run on every snapshot build so stale 'authorized' can't persist. Limitation: Core Audio exposes no dedicated 'denied' code, so undetermined vs. denied are both collapsed to .notGranted after a probe — a fresh, never-prompted state can show .undetermined only until the first probe runs, after which a not-yet-granted user reads as .notGranted/.failed. The probe creating a real (if instantly destroyed) tap is what can trigger the system permission prompt, which is intended but means merely opening diagnostics may prompt the user.

Feature: Route resilience: retry/backoff, recovery, device-change auto-restore, and lifecycle cleanup
User Story: As a user, I want my per-app routes to survive transient failures, output-device changes, and app quits, so that my volume/mute settings keep working without me manually re-establishing them and without orphaned audio devices piling up.
Expected Behavior:
• createControllerWithRetry retries tap/aggregate creation up to 3 times with exponential backoff (100/400/1600ms), re-resolving process object IDs between attempts; after exhausting retries it throws managedRouteUnavailable with plain-language guidance.
• A reentrancy guard disposes any controller installed for the same app during an await suspension so live taps/aggregates are not orphaned (no leak / double audio).
• recoverRoutes() tears down all controllers, rebuilds the snapshot, clears lastError, and reattaches routes for previously managed/active logical IDs; reattachRoutes sets isRouteRecoveryHealthy based on whether any route recovered.
• A default-output-device change listener fires handleDeviceChange -> autoRestoreDevice(), which disposes controllers, rebuilds the snapshot on the new device, reattaches managed routes, and yields a deviceChangeEvents signal so the UI/store can refresh and restore per-device presets even if restoration failed.
• releaseControllers(forBundleID:pid:) promptly disposes a quit app's controller (destroying its IO proc, aggregate device, and process tap) and resets that app to monitorOnly with zeroed levels, instead of leaking until the next manual refresh.
• disposeControllers(keeping:) garbage-collects controllers for apps no longer present in the snapshot; dispose() is idempotent (didDispose guard) and fully tears down IO proc + aggregate + tap, draining the callback queue.
• stop() removes the device-change listener, cancels the level task, disposes all controllers, and finishes the deviceChangeEvents stream; the listener registration is guarded against double-install.
Acceptance Criteria:
• A transient tap-creation failure that succeeds on attempt 2 yields a working managed route (logged 'on attempt 2').
• After 3 failed attempts the app is marked .error with a message pointing at Privacy & Security, and no partial tap/aggregate is left running.
• Switching the default output device disposes old aggregates, rebuilds routes on the new device, and emits exactly one deviceChangeEvents value.
• Quitting a managed app releases its tap/aggregate (no lingering 'Waves-<app>' aggregate device) and resets it to monitorOnly.
• recoverRoutes on a healthy session re-establishes all previously managed routes and sets isRouteRecoveryHealthy=true.
Impl Notes: This is unusually thorough lifecycle handling: explicit leak avoidance on the reentrancy race (line 382), idempotent dispose, retry with PID re-resolution, and device-change auto-restore with an event stream. Concerns: (1) reattachRoutes sets isRouteRecoveryHealthy=true if ANY route recovers, so a session where some apps fail can still report 'healthy' while individual apps are .error. (2) autoRestoreDevice/recoverRoutes dispose then rebuild ALL controllers on any default-device change, causing a brief audio gap for every managed app even if only one app cared about the device. (3) The device-change listener only watches kAudioHardwarePropertyDefaultOutputDevice, so hot-unplugging a per-app pinned (targetDeviceUID) device that is NOT the default won't auto-trigger recovery — that route stays .managed in state until the next manual refresh/apply, where createController then fails with 'device unavailable'. (4) deviceChangeListenerToken is a simple 0/1 flag, fine here but not a real token. These are edge-case correctness/UX gaps, not blockers, so the feature is marked implemented.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/edge-case] Hot-unplugging a per-app pinned (non-default) output device does not trigger auto-restore (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1410) — addDeviceChangeListener registers exactly one listener, on kAudioHardwarePropertyDefaultOutputDevice (line 1410-1427; verified this is the only AudioObjectAddPropertyListenerBlock call in the file). If an app is pinned to a specific targetDeviceUID that is NOT the system default and that device is unplugged, no listener fires, handleDeviceChange/autoRestoreDevice are never invoked, and the controller keeps an aggregate whose main/sub device just vanished. The app stays routingState=.managed in the snapshot (and in buildSnapshot's merge at line 1044-1047 it is re-marked .managed as long as controller.isActive reports true) even though its aggregate is broken, until a manual refresh/re-apply — at which point createController throws 'The chosen output device ... is unavailable.' (line 438). Meters freeze/go silent with no error surfaced in the meantime. | Fix: Also register a listener on kAudioHardwarePropertyDevices and, on change, check each managed app whose targetDeviceUID is no longer in availableOutputDevices(): dispose its controller and mark it .error (or attempt reattach on the default) so the UI reflects the broken route immediately.
• [LOW/inconsistency] isRouteRecoveryHealthy reports healthy if ANY route recovers, masking partially-failed sessions (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1366) — reattachRoutes sets snapshot.backendStatus.isRouteRecoveryHealthy = recoveredAnyRoute (line 1366), where recoveredAnyRoute (line 1358) becomes true as soon as a single app reattaches. In a recover/auto-restore where some apps reattach and others throw and are marked .error (line 1360-1362), the session-level health still reads true and the diagnostics 'Route recovery' check (diagnosticsReport line 264-270) shows .passed even though individual apps are in .error and lastError is set. Confirmed; low. | Fix: Set isRouteRecoveryHealthy = recoveredAnyRoute && (lastError == nil) — require that no app in the reattach set ended in .error — or compute it as 'all attempted routes recovered'.
• [LOW/error-handling] Mid-retry re-resolve can escape createControllerWithRetry, bypassing the friendly exhausted message (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:412) — Inside the retry loop's catch block, the between-attempt re-resolution `let refreshedProcessObjectIDs = try resolveProcessObjectIDs(for: app)` (line 412) is not wrapped in its own do/catch. If the app quits or loses all audible process objects between attempts, resolveProcessObjectIDs throws (line 563) and that error propagates straight out of createControllerWithRetry, skipping the remaining attempts and the final managedRouteUnavailable 'check that audio capture is allowed in System Settings › Privacy & Security' message (line 424-426). Confirmed; low. | Fix: Wrap the re-resolve in `if let refreshed = try? resolveProcessObjectIDs(for: app) { currentProcessObjectIDs = refreshed }` so a transient resolution failure between attempts is tolerated and the loop continues to the final friendly error.

Feature: Per-app route-state chip
User Story: As a user managing per-app audio, I want each app row to clearly label its routing state (Live, Recent, Managed, Ready/monitored, or Error), so that I can tell at a glance whether Waves is actually controlling that app's audio or just observing it.
Expected Behavior:
• RoutingState has five cases: live, recent, managed, monitorOnly (displayName "Ready"), error (AudioApp.swift:200-221).
• In a full mixer row, RoutingStateIndicator renders an SF Symbol + text label + tinted capsule: managed=green checkmark.circle.fill "Managed", live=accent waveform "Live", recent=secondary clock.fill "Recent", error=red exclamationmark.triangle.fill "Error" (MixerRowView.swift:407-481).
• The monitorOnly ("Ready") chip is intentionally NOT drawn in the full row — RoutingStateIndicator returns nothing when state == .monitorOnly (MixerRowView.swift:413).
• Compact rows (menu bar) render RoutingStateDot, an icon-only indicator that DOES include monitorOnly (open checkmark.circle, secondary color) (MixerRowView.swift:278,483-511).
• Each chip/dot carries a help tooltip and a VoiceOver accessibilityLabel describing the state, so route state is conveyed without relying on color alone (MixerRowView.swift:435-436,467-480,491-492).
• Under Increase Contrast the chip gains a solid stroked outline and higher background opacity (MixerRowView.swift:430-434,442-443).
• The chip/dot is hidden entirely for excluded apps, since Waves isn't controlling them (MixerRowView.swift:46-50,273-279).
• routingState is assigned by the backend: managed when a tap controller is active, live when the app is currently audible, monitorOnly otherwise (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1036-1055,1178).
Acceptance Criteria:
• A managed app shows a green "Managed" chip with a checkmark icon in the main window row.
• A currently-audible unmanaged app shows an accent "Live" chip with a waveform icon.
• A monitor-only app shows NO chip in the main window row, but shows an open-circle dot in the compact menu-bar row.
• Each indicator exposes a non-color VoiceOver label (e.g. "Managed route is active.") and a matching tooltip.
• Excluded apps show no route-state chip/dot at all.
• With Increase Contrast enabled, chips render with a solid outline.
Impl Notes: Wired end-to-end and rendered consistently. Two UX inconsistencies worth noting: (1) the "Ready"/monitorOnly state has a real displayName, help text, and an icon defined, but the full-row RoutingStateIndicator suppresses it (line 413), so in the primary window a monitored app is visually indistinguishable from a recent one (both show no chip) — the honest visible/monitored/managed distinction the docs promise is only partially expressed in the main window. (2) The compact dot does render monitorOnly, so the two surfaces disagree on whether "Ready" is shown. The README/DESIGN explicitly promise distinguishable visible/monitored/managed states; this is a minor gap against that intent.
Errors Found:
• [LOW/dead-code] The "Recent" (clock.fill) chip is unreachable in normal operation (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MixerRowView.swift:399-405) — Re-verified. discoverRunningApps assigns only .live or .monitorOnly (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1178). The buildSnapshot per-index loop (1036-1054) only ever produces .managed, .monitorOnly, or leaves .live; the retention loop (1017-1034) sets .managed or .monitorOnly. setDesiredVolume/setMuted/etc. produce .managed or .error. The ONLY source of .recent is the AudioApp init/decoder default (AudioApp.swift:48,135), which can only surface a persisted snapshot transiently before backend.start() rebuilds. So a running/discovered app is never assigned .recent in steady state, making the Recent chip effectively dead UI. Confirmed. | Fix: Either assign .recent in discovery for apps that were recently audible (e.g. audible within the last N seconds via a remembered audible-PID window), or remove the .recent case and its UI branches if the state is not intended to be produced at runtime.
• [LOW/inconsistency] Main window and menu bar disagree on whether monitor-only ("Ready") is shown (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MixerRowView.swift:413) — Re-verified. RoutingStateIndicator (full row) returns nothing when state == .monitorOnly (MixerRowView.swift:413), so a monitored app shows no chip in the main window and is visually identical to a (never-reached) recent app. RoutingStateDot (compact menu-bar row, used at MixerRowView.swift:278) DOES render .monitorOnly as an open checkmark.circle (503-504). The two surfaces express the visible/monitored/managed distinction differently for the same app. Confirmed — though this matches ROUTE-01's own documented expectedBehavior (the doc says the suppression and the dot are intentional), so it is a UX inconsistency against README/DESIGN intent rather than a code-vs-doc mismatch. | Fix: Decide on one policy: either render a subtle "Ready" chip in the full row too (it already has displayName/icon/help defined), or drop .monitorOnly from RoutingStateDot for parity and document that monitorOnly is intentionally chip-less in both surfaces.

Feature: Errored route surfacing with inline failure reason
User Story: As a user whose per-app route failed to set up, I want the row to show an Error state with the specific failure reason, so that I understand why volume/mute didn't take effect and what to do next.
Expected Behavior:
• Every route-applying backend action (setDesiredVolume, setMuted, setVolumeBoost, applyPreset, setOutputDevice) sets routingState=.error and stores error.localizedDescription into app.notes when applyRoute throws (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:76-77,102-103,134-135,176-177,736-737,1360-1361).
• In the full mixer row, when routingState==.error and notes are present, the failure text is rendered beneath the row in caption/secondary styling, indented under the slider (MixerRowView.swift:111-116).
• The error chip itself shows a red exclamation-triangle with "Error" and tooltip "Route setup failed." (MixerRowView.swift:402-403,462-463,476-477).
• Errored apps are reachable via the "Needs attention" VoiceOver rotor, which filters apps where routingState==.error (MainWindowView.swift:393-397).
• An app's error note is cleared (notes=nil) and state returns to .managed on the next successful apply or recovery (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:71-72,729-733).
• Successful applies also clear the global backendStatus.lastError and mark route recovery healthy again (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:73-74,99-100).
Acceptance Criteria:
• When a route apply fails, the affected row shows a red "Error" chip and the failure's localized description below it.
• The VoiceOver "Needs attention" rotor lists exactly the apps in error state.
• After a subsequent successful volume/mute change, the error chip and note disappear and the chip returns to "Managed".
• A failed action also surfaces a toast (e.g. "Mute toggle failed") via the store catch blocks.
Impl Notes: Solid and honest: the raw localizedDescription is exposed verbatim, which is great for diagnostics but can be terse/technical for non-technical users (e.g. "managedRouteUnavailable(...)" wording from BackendError). The inline note is only shown in the full MixerRowView, not the compact menu-bar row, so a menu-bar-only user sees the red error dot but no reason text. Error apps also are not pulled into a dedicated visible "needs attention" section in the on-screen list (only the accessibility rotor groups them); a sighted user must scan rows to find the red chip.
Errors Found:
• [HIGH/correctness] A failed volume change does not leave a persistent Error chip/note (unlike mute/boost) (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:443-460) — Re-verified. On a failed volume apply the backend's setDesiredVolume sets the app to .error with notes (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:76-77) before throwing. The store's scheduleVolumeApply catch (AppStore.swift:443-460) shows the "Volume change failed" toast, then calls self.session = try await self.backend.refresh() (453). refresh() rebuilds via buildSnapshot whose merge (999-1014) does NOT carry over previous.routingState/notes, and whose per-index loop resets a no-controller, non-.live app back to .monitorOnly with notes=nil (1051-1054), discarding the .error the backend just set. By contrast setMuted/setVolumeBoost catch blocks use backend.currentSnapshot() + mergeAppState (AppStore.swift:535-536,581-582); mergeAppState (1417-1436) copies the single backend app verbatim, preserving .error + notes. Confirmed and the inconsistency with mute/boost is real. | Fix: In the volume-failure path, mirror setMuted/setVolumeBoost: replace backend.refresh() with backend.currentSnapshot() + mergeAppState(from:appID:) so the .error state and notes set by the backend are preserved and rendered.
• [HIGH/correctness] Any plain refresh silently clears Error chips/notes without a successful re-apply (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1048-1054) — Re-verified. buildSnapshot's merge (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:999-1014) carries over volume/mute/pin/boost/targetDevice but NOT routingState or notes, and the per-index loop (1051-1054) resets any non-controller, non-.live app to .monitorOnly with notes=nil. buildSnapshot runs on every backend.refresh() (store.refresh() AppStore.swift:242, the Cmd-R toolbar at MainWindowView.swift:39) and on every autoRestoreDevice() (device-change). So after a real route failure has correctly shown an Error chip, the next refresh (which can be user- or system-triggered with no successful apply) clears the chip and inline reason. Note buildSnapshot also recomputes isRouteRecoveryHealthy = !hasRouteErrors (1089), so the global RouteHealthBadge also flips back to green after the same refresh. Confirmed. | Fix: Preserve previous.routingState==.error and previous.notes across buildSnapshot for apps rediscovered with no active controller, OR re-probe/re-apply the route during rebuild so the error clears only when the route actually recovers.
• [MEDIUM/accessibility] "Needs attention" rotor under-reports errored apps in non-Running filters (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MainWindowView.swift:393-397) — Re-verified. The rotor filters the view-local `apps` array for routingState==.error (MainWindowView.swift:393-397). `apps` is bound to filteredApps (line 20), which equals scopedApps when not searching; scopedApps is store.visibleApps only for .running, but store.liveApps for .frontmost, store.pinnedApps for .pinned, and store.recentApps for .recent (MainWindowView.swift:102-113). An errored app is not .live, may not be pinned, and recentApps is empty when showRecentApps is off. So on any tab other than Running the rotor omits errored apps outside that scoped subset, defeating the accessibility affordance for VoiceOver users. Confirmed. | Fix: Build the rotor from store.visibleApps.filter { $0.routingState == .error } (all visible apps) rather than the view-scoped `apps`, so it always enumerates every errored app regardless of the active filter. (Severity raised to medium: this is the only programmatic affordance that groups errored apps, so the gap fully blocks VoiceOver users from reaching them in 3 of 4 tabs.)
• [LOW/ux] Errored apps have no on-screen failure reason in the menu bar surface (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MixerRowView.swift:483-511) — Re-verified. The inline failure note renders only in the full MixerRowView when routingState==.error (MixerRowView.swift:111-116). CompactMixerRow (menu bar) shows only RoutingStateDot, a red exclamation-triangle for .error with no notes text (483-511). Additionally the menu bar lists only Pinned/Live/Recent CompactSections (MenuBarMixerView.swift:42-50); a non-live errored app falls into recentApps only if showRecentApps is on (recentApps = visibleApps minus live), so with showRecentApps off an errored, unpinned, non-audible app does not appear at all — a menu-bar-only user gets no indication of the failure. Confirmed. | Fix: Surface the error reason in the compact row (tooltip/secondary line on the dot) and/or always include errored apps in a menu-bar "Needs attention" section independent of showRecentApps.

Feature: Global route-health badge, status pill, and manual recovery
User Story: As a user, I want a single overall routing-health indicator and a one-click way to recover routes, so that I can confirm Waves is healthy and fix transient failures without restarting the app.
Expected Behavior:
• RouteHealthBadge in the output summary shows one of three states: "Ready" (green checkmark) when backendStatus.isRouteRecoveryHealthy, "Needs attention" (red triangle) when not healthy and lastError != nil, else "Limited" (secondary) (MainWindowView.swift:479-520).
• isRouteRecoveryHealthy is computed as supportsPerAppRouting && capture authorized && no app is in .error state (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1061,1089).
• A toolbar button "Recover managed routes" calls store.recoverRoutes(), which asks the backend to reattach routes, refreshes diagnostics, persists, and shows a success/failure toast (MainWindowView.swift:48-55; AppStore.swift:1181-1202).
• On recovery failure the store falls back to the current snapshot and surfaces a "Recovery failed" error toast (AppStore.swift:1194-1200).
• The badge text/color/icon never relies on color alone — it pairs an SF Symbol and text label, plus a help/accessibility label "Routing status: <title>" (MainWindowView.swift:483-490,517-519).
Acceptance Criteria:
• With the audio component installed, permission granted, and no errored apps, the badge reads "Ready" in green.
• If any app is in error state (or lastError is set), the badge reads "Needs attention" in red.
• If per-app routing is unsupported/permission missing with no specific error, the badge reads "Limited".
• Clicking "Recover managed routes" reattaches routes and shows a "Routes recovered" toast on success or "Recovery failed" on error.
• The badge conveys its state via icon+text, not color alone.
Impl Notes: Works and is honest. Edge case: the "Limited" branch only renders when isRouteRecoveryHealthy is false AND lastError is nil — but when per-app routing is unsupported (pre-14.2), no per-app apply runs so lastError typically stays nil, correctly yielding "Limited". However on a healthy supported system the badge says "Ready" even if zero apps are managed, which slightly overstates that routing is doing anything. The badge reflects backend recovery health, not whether any specific route is active. Recovery success toast claims routes were reattached even if there were none to reattach.
Errors Found:
• [HIGH/correctness] Partial recovery/restore reports route health as "Ready" despite apps left in Error (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1366) — Re-verified. reattachRoutes sets snapshot.backendStatus.isRouteRecoveryHealthy = recoveredAnyRoute (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1366), and recoveredAnyRoute is true if at least one route succeeds (1358), even when other apps fail and are set to .error (1360-1362). reattachRoutes runs AFTER buildSnapshot in both recoverRoutes (214-217) and autoRestoreDevice (231-235), overwriting the correct health (supportsPerAppRouting && authorized && !hasRouteErrors, line 1089). RouteHealthBadge reads isRouteRecoveryHealthy directly (MainWindowView.swift:494), so with one success and one failure the badge shows "Ready" green while an app remains errored. Confirmed. | Fix: Compute health as no-errors, not any-success: isRouteRecoveryHealthy = !snapshot.apps.contains { $0.routingState == .error } (still gated on supportsPerAppRouting && authorized), rather than = recoveredAnyRoute.
• [MEDIUM/correctness] A single successful per-app apply flips the GLOBAL route-health badge to Ready while another app stays errored (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:73-74) — NEW (missed by first tester). On any successful single-app apply, the backend unconditionally sets the GLOBAL snapshot.backendStatus.isRouteRecoveryHealthy = true and lastError = nil: setDesiredVolume (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:73-74), setMuted (99-100), setVolumeBoost (131-132); setOutputDevice clears lastError on success (734). These never consider whether OTHER apps are in .error. The store merges the new backendStatus via mergeAppState (AppStore.swift:429,525,571) — mergeAppState copies session.backendStatus = backendSession.backendStatus (1429). RouteHealthBadge reads isRouteRecoveryHealthy/lastError directly (MainWindowView.swift:494,497). So if app A is in .error and the user merely nudges app B's volume successfully, the global badge flips to "Ready" green and the diagnostics export flag flips to healthy, even though app A is still errored. Distinct from the reattach bug above (different code paths, normal everyday apply path). | Fix: Do not set isRouteRecoveryHealthy=true / lastError=nil unconditionally on per-app success. Recompute global health from the whole snapshot (e.g. isRouteRecoveryHealthy = supportsPerAppRouting && authorized && !snapshot.apps.contains { $0.routingState == .error }) at the end of each apply, and only clear lastError when no app remains errored.
• [LOW/ux] Recovery success toast claims routes were reattached even when there were none (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1189-1193) — Re-verified. recoverRoutes calls reattachRoutes only when managedLogicalIDs is non-empty (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:216), but the store unconditionally shows "Routes recovered — Managed routing paths were reattached." on success (AppStore.swift:1189-1193). With zero managed apps nothing is reattached, yet the toast asserts it was. Confirmed. | Fix: Return a count of reattached routes from recoverRoutes() and tailor the toast (e.g. "No managed routes to recover" vs "Reattached N routes").

Feature: Live-audio detection: subtitle, level meter, and live grouping
User Story: As a user, I want apps that are actively playing audio to be visibly marked as live (label, animated level meter, and grouping), so that I can quickly find and adjust whatever is making sound right now.
Expected Behavior:
• The backend marks an app .live when its process family is in the set of currently-audible PIDs (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1176-1178).
• The row subtitle reads "Playing audio" when the app is .live, or when it's .managed, unmuted, and has nonzero peak/RMS level; otherwise "Frontmost app" or "Running app" (MixerRowView.swift:182-205).
• A quiet cyan level-meter capsule is overlaid on managed/live, unmuted, non-excluded rows, width scaled by the live RMS/peak from the visibility-gated poll (MixerRowView.swift:122-134,173-180).
• Live levels are polled every 300ms only while a mixer surface is visible (reference-counted begin/endLiveLevels); the poll pulls levels for managed/live apps only (AppStore.swift:616-638; WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1096-1102).
• store.liveApps groups apps that are .live OR (.managed && unmuted && audible), feeding the Live menu-bar section, the "Frontmost/Live" filter, the output-summary "…playing" summary, and the "Playing apps" rotor (AppStore.swift:117-122; MainWindowView.swift:109,389-391,469-476; MenuBarMixerView.swift:46).
• The menu-bar icon switches to speaker.wave.3.fill when any app is live (AppStore.swift:149-152).
Acceptance Criteria:
• An app that is currently producing audio shows the "Playing audio" subtitle and a moving cyan level meter.
• Muted or excluded apps never show the level meter even if managed.
• The Live section / Frontmost filter lists exactly the apps detected as live or audibly managed.
• Level polling stops (CPU idle) when no mixer surface is on screen and resumes when one opens.
• The menu-bar icon reflects a live state with the 3-wave speaker glyph.
Impl Notes: Well-built and performance-conscious (visibility-gated polling, no-op redraw guards). One correctness nuance: the "Playing audio" subtitle for a managed app depends on peakLevel/rmsLevel stored in the snapshot, while the overlay meter reads store.liveLevels[logicalID]; these can briefly disagree (subtitle says playing while meter is at 0, or vice versa) between the 300ms poll and a snapshot refresh. For a purely .live (unmanaged) app, audioLevels() does include it but the snapshot peak/rms are 0 (set at discovery, line 1190-1191), so a live-but-unmanaged app may show "Playing audio" yet a zero-width meter until tapped.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/logic] Menu-bar icon never shows the live 3-wave glyph when any app is muted (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:146-152) — Re-verified. menuBarIconName returns speaker.slash.fill whenever ANY visible app isMuted, checked BEFORE the live check (AppStore.swift:146-152). Muting one background app permanently overrides the live indicator, so even with audio actively playing in another app the icon shows the mute glyph instead of speaker.wave.3.fill. Confirmed. | Fix: Reorder so live takes precedence, or distinguish "all muted" from "some muted" (e.g. show speaker.wave.3.fill when liveApps is non-empty; fall back to slash only when nothing is live or all live apps are themselves muted).
• [MEDIUM/edge-case] Live-but-unmanaged apps show "Playing audio" with a permanently zero-width level meter (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MixerRowView.swift:177-180) — Re-verified. showsLevelMeter is true for .live apps (MixerRowView.swift:174) and the subtitle reads "Playing audio" for .live apps (185-186). But a purely .live (untapped) app has peakLevel/rmsLevel == 0 at discovery (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1190-1191), updateAudioLevels only writes levels for apps with an active controller (1393-1394), and audioLevels() returns the stored 0/0 for live apps (1098-1099). So store.liveLevels yields meterLevel 0 (MixerRowView.swift:177-180) and the capsule renders at width 0 and never moves until the app is actually tapped/managed. Confirmed. | Fix: Either suppress the meter for live-unmanaged apps (show only when real levels exist), or source a lightweight level for live-unmanaged apps so the meter reflects actual audio, keeping subtitle and meter consistent.
• [LOW/ux] Subtitle "Playing audio" and the overlay meter can disagree due to two independent level sources (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MixerRowView.swift:182-205) — Re-verified as plausible. For a managed app the "Playing audio" subtitle derives from app.peakLevel/app.rmsLevel in the snapshot (MixerRowView.swift:187), updated by the backend's 250ms task (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1375) and merged on snapshot reads. The overlay meter reads store.liveLevels[logicalID], updated by the store's separate 300ms poll (AppStore.swift:621). The two update on different cadences and merge points, so transiently the subtitle can say "Playing audio" while the meter sits at 0 (or vice versa). This is a brief transient rather than a steady-state defect, hence likely. | Fix: Drive both the subtitle and the meter from a single level source (e.g. compute the "Playing audio" predicate from store.liveLevels too), so they cannot diverge.

Feature: Route-state lifecycle transitions on exclusion, termination, device change, and OS support
User Story: As a user, I want route states to update correctly when I exclude an app, when an app quits, when I switch output devices, or when my macOS is too old for per-app routing, so that the displayed status always matches reality and never falsely claims control.
Expected Behavior:
• Excluding an app tears down its controllers and resets it to monitorOnly with appliedVolume nil, unmuted, in both store and backend; re-including restores normal discovery (AppStore.swift:686-720; WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1104-1124).
• When an app terminates, its tap is released and managed/live/active rows are immediately demoted to monitorOnly with levels zeroed (AppStore.swift:899-938; WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1117-1123).
• On output-device change with auto-restore on, the backend re-establishes managed routes before emitting the event and the store reads the fresh snapshot (no double-restore) (AppStore.swift:792-818,820-846).
• If a route is rebuilt for a managed app on device change/output retarget and fails, it goes to .error with notes; if it never had a managed route, only the preference is recorded (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:710-741).
• When per-app routing is unsupported (pre-macOS 14.2), every app is forced to monitorOnly with note "Per-app route requires macOS 14.2+" and compatibility .planned (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1036-1042).
• Retained-but-not-rediscovered apps that still have an active controller keep .managed; otherwise drop to monitorOnly (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1016-1034).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Excluding a managed app immediately changes its chip away from Managed and stops tapping its audio.
• Quitting a managed app demotes its row to monitored and zeroes its meter without waiting for a full refresh.
• Switching output devices with auto-restore keeps previously-managed apps managed (or marks them Error if reattach fails) rather than silently dropping control.
• On macOS older than 14.2, all apps show monitor-only with a "requires macOS 14.2+" note and never claim Managed.
• Re-including a previously excluded app makes it eligible for management again.
Impl Notes: Transitions are carefully handled across all four lifecycle paths and consistently zero out levels/appliedVolume to avoid stale 'managed' claims. Note: the pre-14.2 note "Per-app route requires macOS 14.2+" is set on the app but is only rendered in the row when routingState==.error (MixerRowView.swift:111) — since these apps are monitorOnly, that explanatory note is never shown to the user in the row (it does reach diagnostics export). That's a small UX gap against DESIGN.md's promise that disabled/limited controls explain why control is limited. The slider/mute help text does generically say 'Move the slider… to start per-app control', which doesn't mention the OS requirement.
Errors Found:
• [HIGH/correctness] Backend auto-restores routes on device change even when the user disabled auto-restore, and the UI then goes stale (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:792-818) — Re-verified. The backend's handleDeviceChange unconditionally calls autoRestoreDevice() before yielding the event (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1462-1473) — it consults no user preference; autoRestoreDevice disposes all controllers and rebuilds/reattaches managed routes (223-238). The store's handleDeviceChange is gated by guard preferences.autoRestoreDevice else { return } (AppStore.swift:792-793). So with auto-restore OFF: (1) the backend still tears down and rebuilds managed routes against the user's intent, and (2) the store ignores the event and never reads the new snapshot, leaving session.currentDevice and all per-app states stale (wrong device name, stale managed/error chips) until some other refresh. Confirmed. | Fix: Pass the autoRestoreDevice preference into the backend so it skips reattach when disabled; at minimum always refresh the store's session/device on the device-change event and only skip the route-reattach behavior — never leave the UI stale when auto-restore is off.
• [LOW/inconsistency] Choosing an output device for a live-but-unmanaged app forces it into managed routing (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:725-732) — Re-verified. setOutputDevice treats wasManaged = (routingState == .managed || routingState == .live) (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:726) and, when true, calls applyRoute which creates a tap/controller and sets the app to .managed (729-733). A .live app has never had a managed route (no controller exists at discovery), yet picking a device for it silently enrolls it in managed routing rather than merely recording the preference. Note the doc's own ROUTE-05 expectedBehavior says "if it never had a managed route, only the preference is recorded" — so this is a genuine code-vs-intent mismatch. Confirmed. | Fix: Define wasManaged strictly by an existing managed route (controllers[id]?.isActive == true || routingState == .managed); for purely .live apps just record targetDeviceUID and defer route creation until the user actually manages the app.
• [LOW/ux] Pre-14.2 "requires macOS 14.2+" note is never shown in the row (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MixerRowView.swift:111-116) — Re-verified. When per-app routing is unsupported, buildSnapshot sets routingState=.monitorOnly with notes="Per-app route requires macOS 14.2+" and compatibility=.planned (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1037-1041). But the row renders notes only when routingState==.error (MixerRowView.swift:111). Since these apps are .monitorOnly, the explanatory note never appears in the UI (it only reaches diagnostics export). The slider/mute help text generically says "Move the slider… to start per-app control" (MixerRowView.swift:474) / "Adjust to enroll…" (211-215) and never mentions the OS requirement. Confirmed. | Fix: Render app.notes for monitorOnly apps too (or specifically when compatibility==.planned), or show the OS-requirement message in the slider/mute help text when supportsPerAppRouting is false.

Feature: Diagnostics export and system-status panel
User Story: As a user filing a bug report or verifying setup, I want an in-app diagnostics view and a copyable text export of route health and per-app state, so that I can confirm what's wrong and share it without exposing audio content.
Expected Behavior:
• diagnosticsExportText builds a plain-text report: macOS version, output device, audio-component-installed, capture-permission, route-recovery-healthy, last error, then every visible app's name, route-state displayName, volume %, mute, and boost, plus diagnostic checks (AppStore.swift:725-751).
• copyDiagnosticsToPasteboard writes that text to the general pasteboard and toasts "Diagnostics copied" (AppStore.swift:774-779).
• The DiagnosticsPanel disclosure group shows the diagnostics summary and each check with a colored status dot (passed=green, warning=orange, failed=red, info=secondary) plus a non-color VoiceOver label like "Failed: <title>" (MainWindowView.swift:522-589).
• Diagnostics are refreshed after start, device change, recovery, and on demand (AppStore.swift:215,807,1186,1204-1208).
• BackendStatus carries isAudioComponentInstalled, hasRequiredPermissions, isRouteRecoveryHealthy, lastError, all surfaced in the export (AudioSessionSnapshot.swift:45-63).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Copying diagnostics places a human-readable report containing OS, device, permission/component/route-health flags, and per-app route states on the clipboard.
• The diagnostics panel lists each check with a status dot and an accessible status label that doesn't depend on color.
• The export contains no audio data — only app names already visible in the mixer.
• Diagnostics refresh after a recovery or device change so the report reflects current health.
Impl Notes: Complete and matches the README's 'in-app diagnostics export' claim. The export honestly reports component/permission/route health and per-app route states. Minor: the DiagnosticsPanel relies on store.diagnostics being non-nil; if a backend diagnosticsReport() ever fails to populate, the disclosure body renders empty with no fallback message. The copy/export entry point's discoverability (which toolbar/menu invokes copyDiagnosticsToPasteboard) lives outside the assigned files; the function itself is wired correctly.
Errors Found:
• [LOW/ux] Diagnostics disclosure renders an empty body when diagnostics are nil (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MainWindowView.swift:530-557) — Re-verified. DiagnosticsPanel's disclosure content is wrapped in if let diagnostics = store.diagnostics with no else branch (MainWindowView.swift:531-557). If diagnostics has not populated yet (e.g. expanded before start() completes, or if diagnosticsReport ever fails to set it), expanding the disclosure shows a blank body with no loading/unavailable message, and nothing triggers a refresh on expand. Confirmed. | Fix: Add an else branch rendering a "Diagnostics not available yet" placeholder, and/or trigger refreshDiagnostics() on expand, so the panel is never blank.
• [LOW/correctness] Diagnostics export reflects stale route health after a partial recovery or a single successful apply (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:734) — Re-verified. diagnosticsExportText reports "Route recovery healthy: <status.isRouteRecoveryHealthy>" (AppStore.swift:734) and then lists each app's routingState.displayName (741). Because (a) reattachRoutes sets isRouteRecoveryHealthy = recoveredAnyRoute (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1366, ROUTE-03 issue 1) and (b) every single-app success path sets it = true unconditionally (73-74/99-100/131-132, ROUTE-03 NEW issue), a snapshot with some apps still in .error can export "Route recovery healthy: true" while listing those apps as "Error" in the per-app section. The exported report is internally contradictory. Confirmed. | Fix: Fix the isRouteRecoveryHealthy computation (see ROUTE-03 issues) so the exported flag is derived from the absence of any .error app, keeping it consistent with the per-app states.

Feature: Pin an app to a specific output device
User Story: As a Mac user running several audio apps, I want to send one app's sound to a specific output device (e.g. my headphones) while everything else stays on the system default, so that I can hear different apps on different speakers without changing my global output.
Expected Behavior:
• Each managed (non-excluded) mixer row has a context-menu submenu 'Output Device' listing 'System Default' plus every available output device (MixerRowView.swift:140-156).
• The currently chosen target shows a checkmark: 'System Default' is checked when app.targetDeviceUID == nil, otherwise the matching device row is checked (MixerRowView.swift:142-154).
• Selecting a device calls store.setOutputDevice(device, for: app), which sets session.apps[index].targetDeviceUID = device.id, invalidates the visible-apps cache, then calls backend.setOutputDevice(uid:forAppID:) (AppStore.swift:649-661).
• In the backend, the app's targetDeviceUID is recorded and any existing tap controller is torn down so the route can be rebuilt on the new device; if the app was already managed/live the route is re-applied via applyRoute, otherwise only the preference is stored for next time (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:710-741).
• When a managed route is (re)built, the per-app aggregate device is created with the chosen device UID as its main sub-device and clock device, so the tapped audio physically plays out the selected device (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:436-491).
• On success a toast 'Output set — AppName → DeviceName' is shown; on failure a toast 'Couldn't route AppName' with the error is shown and the app's state is re-merged from the backend (AppStore.swift:662-672).
• The choice is persisted via persistSessionSnapshot() so it survives relaunch (AppStore.swift:661).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Opening the context menu on a managed app shows 'System Default' plus all enumerated output devices, with exactly one checkmark on the active choice.
• Picking a non-default device on a currently-managed app moves its audible output to that device and the route stays in the managed state.
• app.targetDeviceUID equals the selected device's UID after selection and nil after choosing 'System Default'.
• A success toast naming the app and device appears within ~1.5s on success; an error toast appears on failure.
• After quitting and relaunching, the app is still pinned to the previously chosen device.
Impl Notes: The core path is fully wired and physically routes via the aggregate device's main sub-device. Gap: when the app is NOT yet managed (routingState == .monitorOnly), backend.setOutputDevice returns early after only recording the preference (no route is built, WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:723-727), yet AppStore still shows a success toast 'Output set — App → Device' (AppStore.swift:662-667). The user is told the output was set even though nothing audibly changed until they later move the slider/mute to enroll the app — misleading UX. Also setOutputDevice does not call refreshOutputDevices() first, so the menu relies on a possibly-stale availableDevices list captured at start()/onAppear.
Errors Found:
• [HIGH/ux] Success toast 'Output set' shown for monitor-only apps even though no route is built (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:662-667) — For any app whose routingState is .monitorOnly (the common case for a running-but-not-yet-enrolled app), backend.setOutputDevice records snapshot.apps[index].targetDeviceUID then returns early at `guard wasManaged else { return }` (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:725-727) without building a route or throwing. AppStore's do-block therefore succeeds and unconditionally shows 'Output set — AppName → DeviceName' (AppStore.swift:662-667). Re-verified: wasManaged is only .managed/.live; a Ready app is .monitorOnly so the guard returns. Audio keeps playing to the system default until the user later enrolls the app via slider/mute. | Fix: Have backend.setOutputDevice return whether a route was actually (re)built, or have AppStore inspect the merged app.routingState after the call; when still .monitorOnly, show an informational 'Output saved — applies when AppName is adjusted/starts playing' instead of a success 'Output set'.
• [MEDIUM/edge-case] Stale device list: per-app submenu never refreshes and there is no device add/remove listener (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MixerRowView.swift:140-156) — availableDevices is populated only on backend start (AppStore.swift:216), the menu-bar picker's onAppear (MenuBarMixerView.swift:156), and on a default-device change. The Core Audio listener is registered only on kAudioHardwarePropertyDefaultOutputDevice (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1410-1414); there is no listener on kAudioHardwarePropertyDevices, and the per-app Output Device contextMenu in MixerRowView (lines 140-156) has no .onAppear/.task calling store.refreshOutputDevices(). Plugging in a device after launch without making it the default leaves it absent from the per-app submenu until a default change or relaunch. | Fix: Add a kAudioHardwarePropertyDevices listener that yields a refresh, and/or call store.refreshOutputDevices() when the per-app Output Device menu appears, mirroring the menu-bar picker's onAppear.
• [MEDIUM/ux] No checkmark shown when the pinned device is missing from availableDevices (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MixerRowView.swift:141-155) — The checkmark logic checks app.targetDeviceUID == nil for 'System Default' (MixerRowView.swift:144) and app.targetDeviceUID == device.id per row (line 152). When targetDeviceUID != nil but that device is absent from store.availableDevices (unplugged, or list stale before refresh), neither 'System Default' nor any device row is checked, so zero checkmarks appear. | Fix: When targetDeviceUID is non-nil but absent from availableDevices, surface the saved device as a disabled/checked '<name> (unavailable)' row using store.targetDevice(for:) fallback, so the active-but-missing choice is still indicated.

Feature: Reset an app back to the system default output
User Story: As a user who previously pinned an app to a specific device, I want to send it back to following the system default output, so that it tracks whatever device I'm using without me having to re-pick it.
Expected Behavior:
• The 'Output Device' submenu always offers a 'System Default' entry at the top (MixerRowView.swift:141-145).
• Choosing 'System Default' calls store.setOutputDevice(nil, for: app), clearing targetDeviceUID to nil (MixerRowView.swift:142, AppStore.swift:653).
• With targetDeviceUID == nil, a (re)built route uses currentDefaultOutputDeviceUID() as the aggregate's output, so the app follows the live system default (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:443-444).
• The row subtitle stops showing the '→ Device' suffix once targetDeviceUID is nil (MixerRowView.swift:200-202).
• A success toast 'Output set — AppName → System default' is shown (AppStore.swift:663-664).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Selecting 'System Default' sets app.targetDeviceUID to nil and the checkmark moves to 'System Default'.
• A previously-pinned app then plays out whatever the current system default device is.
• The '→ DeviceName' subtitle suffix disappears after reset.
• Success toast reads '… → System default'.
Impl Notes: Clean and correct. Same monitor-only caveat as OUT-01 applies (toast can claim success when no live route exists yet), but for the reset-to-default case that is low-impact.

Feature: Enumerate selectable output devices (empty/error states)
User Story: As a user, I want Waves to show me the real set of output devices currently attached to my Mac, so that the routing menus only offer destinations that actually exist.
Expected Behavior:
• backend.availableOutputDevices() walks all Core Audio devices, keeps only those with output streams, reads each UID/name, classifies a DeviceKind, and returns them sorted case-insensitively by name (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:661-682, 766-775, 823-841).
• Waves' own private aggregate devices are filtered out so they never appear as user-selectable outputs: UIDs prefixed 'com.waves.aggregate.' are skipped, and any aggregate named containing 'waves' is skipped (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:667-672).
• The current default device is flagged via isCurrent (uid == currentUID) (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:663,677).
• On macOS older than 14.2 the function returns an empty list because supportsPerAppRouting is false (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:662, 310-316).
• availableDevices is populated on backend start (AppStore.swift:216), refreshed by refreshOutputDevices() on the menu-bar picker's onAppear (AppStore.swift:753-756, MenuBarMixerView.swift:156), and after a device change (AppStore.swift:808).
• The per-app routing menu shows a Divider only when availableDevices is non-empty so an empty list collapses to just 'System Default' (MixerRowView.swift:147); the menu-bar system picker shows 'No output devices found' when the list is empty (MenuBarMixerView.swift:136-138).
Acceptance Criteria:
• The device list contains exactly the attached output-capable devices, sorted by name.
• No device whose UID begins with 'com.waves.aggregate.' and no Waves-named aggregate ever appears in either menu.
• On macOS < 14.2 both routing menus show no real devices (per-app menu shows only 'System Default'; system picker shows the empty-state text).
• The device matching the current system default is reported with isCurrent == true.
Impl Notes: Enumeration, filtering of Waves' own aggregates, sorting, and empty/old-OS states are all handled. Minor concern: the per-app submenu in MixerRowView has no explicit 'No output devices found' message (it just shows 'System Default' with no Divider), whereas the menu-bar picker does — slightly inconsistent empty-state messaging. availableDevices can be momentarily stale because the per-app menu does not trigger a refresh on open the way the menu-bar picker does.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/edge-case] availableOutputDevices can be stale because no device-arrival listener exists (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1405-1435) — availableOutputDevices() correctly enumerates, filters, sorts and flags isCurrent (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:661-682), but results are only re-read on backend start, default-device change, and the menu-bar picker onAppear. There is no Core Audio listener on kAudioHardwarePropertyDevices (only kAudioHardwarePropertyDefaultOutputDevice at line 1410-1414), so hot-plug/unplug that does not change the default never refreshes availableDevices, and MixerRowView never calls refreshOutputDevices() on open. | Fix: Register an AudioObjectAddPropertyListenerBlock on kAudioHardwarePropertyDevices that yields a deviceChangeEvents tick (or refreshes), so availableDevices tracks hot-plug events.
• [LOW/dead-code] Secondary 'aggregate named waves' filter is unreachable for Waves' own aggregates (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:672) — availableOutputDevices() skips Waves aggregates by UID prefix (line 669) then, as a second guard, skips devices where kind == .aggregate && name contains 'waves' (line 672). But deviceKind() checks token.contains("aggregate")/"multi-output" (line 831) BEFORE token.contains("waves") (line 834); a 'Waves-AppName' device's token has 'waves' but not 'aggregate', so it is classified .virtual, never .aggregate. The line-672 guard is therefore ineffective; only the line-669 UID-prefix check actually filters Waves aggregates. Harmless (UID-prefix check already covers them), hence low. | Fix: Make the name guard kind-independent (skip when name.lowercased().contains("waves")) or reorder deviceKind so the aggregate check precedes the virtual/'waves' check.
• [LOW/inconsistency] Per-app submenu has no empty-state text, unlike the menu-bar picker (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MixerRowView.swift:147-155) — The menu-bar OutputDevicePicker shows 'No output devices found' when availableDevices is empty (MenuBarMixerView.swift:136-138). The per-app Output Device submenu only renders 'System Default' (no Divider, no message) when the list is empty (MixerRowView.swift:147-155), so on macOS < 14.2 or before the first refresh the user gets no indication real devices are unavailable. | Fix: Add a disabled 'No output devices found' (or 'Requires macOS 14.2+') row in the per-app submenu when store.availableDevices.isEmpty, mirroring the menu-bar picker.

Feature: Honest failure when a pinned device disappears
User Story: As a user who pinned an app to a device that later gets unplugged, I want Waves to tell me the route failed instead of silently moving the audio somewhere else, so that I'm never surprised by sound coming out of the wrong speaker.
Expected Behavior:
• When building a managed route for an app with a targetDeviceUID, the backend checks isDeviceAvailable(uid:) and, if the device is gone, throws managedRouteUnavailable with the message 'The chosen output device for AppName is unavailable. Pick another in the app's Output Device menu.' rather than falling back to the default (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:436-441).
• isDeviceAvailable scans all current device IDs for a matching UID (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:743-745).
• On that throw, setOutputDevice marks the app routingState = .error, stores the error text in notes and backendStatus.lastError, and rethrows (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:735-740).
• The mixer row renders the error: the RoutingStateIndicator chip turns red with an exclamation symbol and 'Route setup failed.' help text, and the app.notes string is shown beneath the row (MixerRowView.swift:111-116, 452-480).
• In the AppStore the error surfaces as a 'Couldn't route AppName' toast (AppStore.swift:668-671).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Pinning to a device and then removing that device causes the app's route to enter the .error state, not a silent fallback to the default device.
• The error message names the app and tells the user to pick another device.
• The row shows a red route-state chip and the failure note text.
• No audio is rerouted to an unexpected device on failure.
Impl Notes: Deliberately fails honestly with a clear, actionable message and a visible error state — matches PRODUCT.md's 'never overpromise' principle. Note: this honest-failure check only fires when a route is actively (re)built; a pinned-but-monitor-only app whose device is gone shows no error until enrolled. The recovery/auto-restore path (reattachRoutes) also depends on this same throw to flag the failure.
