Errors Found: • [MEDIUM/error-handling] Pinned-but-monitor-only app whose device is gone reports false success instead of an error (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:723-727) — The honest-failure check (isDeviceAvailable -> managedRouteUnavailable) only fires inside createController when a route is actively (re)built (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:436-441). In setOutputDevice a .monitorOnly app returns early at line 727 before any route build, so pinning to a currently-unavailable device produces no throw and AppStore shows the 'Output set' success toast (AppStore.swift:662-667). The .error state and the actionable 'pick another device' message only appear later when the app is enrolled. Re-verified against the early-return at line 727 and the error path at lines 436-441. | Fix: In setOutputDevice, when uid is non-nil, run isDeviceAvailable(uid:) on the monitor-only early-return path and throw managedRouteUnavailable (or return a 'saved but unavailable' status) so the UX is honest before enrollment.

Feature: Show which device each app is routed to
User Story: As a user scanning the mixer, I want each pinned app's row to display its destination device, so that I can tell at a glance where its audio is going without opening a menu.
Expected Behavior:
• When app.targetDeviceUID is non-nil, the mixer row subtitle appends '→ DeviceName' (MixerRowView.swift:200-202).
• The device name comes from store.targetDevice(for: app), which looks up the device in availableDevices by UID; if not found it falls back to the literal 'Custom output' (AppStore.swift:642-645, MixerRowView.swift:201).
• The suffix is joined with other subtitle parts (playing/frontmost/category) by commas (MixerRowView.swift:182-204).
Acceptance Criteria:
• A pinned app's row shows '→ <device name>' in its subtitle.
• If the pinned device's name can't be resolved from the current device list, the row shows '→ Custom output' rather than an empty arrow.
• An unpinned app's row shows no '→' suffix.
Impl Notes: Works and degrades gracefully to 'Custom output'. Because targetDevice(for:) resolves against availableDevices, a stale or empty device list (e.g. before refresh, or a disconnected device) will display 'Custom output' for a real pinned device — acceptable fallback but it hides the actual device name in that window.

Feature: Pinned routes survive device changes and relaunch
User Story: As a user, I want my per-app output pins to be re-established automatically after I switch the system output device or restart Waves, so that my carefully arranged routing isn't lost.
Expected Behavior:
• On a default-device change the backend re-establishes managed routes before emitting deviceChangeEvents, and the store reads the fresh snapshot rather than restoring again (AppStore.swift:792-808, WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1462-1473).
• On relaunch, reapplyRestoredAudioState treats a non-nil targetDeviceUID as 'customized' and re-establishes the app's route so it plays to the chosen device immediately, even at default volume (AppStore.swift:827-839).
• targetDeviceUID is preserved across snapshot merges (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1012, AppStore.swift:1404) and is Codable so it round-trips through the persisted session (AudioApp.swift:115,140,163,189).
• Auto-restore is gated by the autoRestoreDevice preference; if disabled, handleDeviceChange returns early (AppStore.swift:792-793).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Switching the system default output device while an app is pinned to a different device leaves that app on its pinned device (its route is rebuilt, not dropped).
• Relaunching Waves re-applies a pinned app's route to its saved device without the user re-selecting it.
• targetDeviceUID is present in the persisted/decoded session and survives snapshot merges.
• With Auto-restore disabled, no automatic re-establishment toast/flow runs on device change.
Impl Notes: Persistence and relaunch re-application of pinned routes are solidly wired. Concern: when the system DEFAULT device changes, the backend's autoRestoreDevice/reattachRoutes path rebuilds routes for managed apps, but a pin to a still-present non-default device should remain unchanged — that is correct. However, if a pinned app's specific device is the one that just disappeared, recovery will throw managedRouteUnavailable (OUT-04) and the app lands in .error with no automatic re-pin prompt; the user must manually re-pick. Also reapplyRestoredAudioState's re-establishment is skipped for excluded apps and silently swallows per-app errors (AppStore.swift:840-843), so a relaunch failure to re-pin is only visible via route state, not a toast.
Errors Found:
• [CRITICAL/correctness] After relaunch a pinned app is NOT routed to its saved device — it silently plays to the system default while the UI claims it is pinned/managed (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:832-839) — Re-verified the full path. On relaunch the persisted targetDeviceUID is merged only into the STORE session via mergedSession (AppStore.swift:1404). The injected backend's own snapshot is never seeded with the persisted session: backend.start() calls buildSnapshot(merging: snapshot) where the backend's prior snapshot is empty, and buildSnapshot only carries targetDeviceUID from previousByLogicalID (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1012) — i.e. from the backend's own (empty) snapshot, so every backend app has targetDeviceUID == nil. reapplyRestoredAudioState then re-establishes routes via backend.setVolumeBoost/setMuted/setDesiredVolume (AppStore.swift:833-835) — never backend.setOutputDevice. Those methods call applyRoute(for: snapshot.apps[index]) on the BACKEND entry (targetDeviceUID == nil) -> createController routes to currentDefaultOutputDeviceUID() (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:443-444). Meanwhile AppStore sets routingState = .managed (AppStore.swift:838) and the row subtitle shows '→ DeviceName' from the store's retained targetDeviceUID (MixerRowView.swift:200-201). Trigger: pin an app to a non-default device, quit, relaunch with that app running — its audio comes out of the system default while the UI claims it is pinned and Managed. | Fix: In reapplyRestoredAudioState, when app.targetDeviceUID != nil, call backend.setOutputDevice(uid: app.targetDeviceUID, forAppID:) (after enrolling), or seed the backend snapshot's targetDeviceUID before start/reapply so applyRoute honors the saved device.
• [MEDIUM/error-handling] Relaunch re-pin failures are swallowed — only logged at debug, never surfaced (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:840-843) — reapplyRestoredAudioState wraps each app's re-establishment in do/catch that only logs at debug and leaves saved state untouched (AppStore.swift:840-843). If re-establishing a pinned route fails (saved device gone, or createController throws managedRouteUnavailable), there is no toast, and because the catch is taken before line 838 the store's routingState is not set to .error for that app — the failure is effectively silent. Note: the backend DID set its own snapshot entry to .error inside setDesiredVolume/setMuted, but the store never merges that backend state on the catch path, so the store keeps the stale .recent/.monitorOnly state. | Fix: On catch, merge the backend's resulting state (which carries .error + notes) into the store for that app and surface at least one aggregated toast like 'Some pinned routes could not be restored' so the failure is discoverable.
• [MEDIUM/inconsistency] Auto-restore disabled still rebuilds backend routes and leaves the store snapshot/device name stale (state desync) (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:792-799) — MISSED BY FIRST TESTER. The autoRestoreDevice user preference is checked ONLY in AppStore.handleDeviceChange (AppStore.swift:793 `guard preferences.autoRestoreDevice else { return }`). The backend's own handleDeviceChange (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1462-1474) unconditionally calls autoRestoreDevice(), which disposeControllers(keeping: []) and reattachRoutes(...) for every managed app (lines 230-235) regardless of the user's preference. Consequences when auto-restore is OFF: (a) acceptance criterion 4 ('no automatic re-establishment runs on device change') is violated — the backend tears down and rebuilds all managed routes anyway; (b) because the store returns at line 793 before `session = await backend.currentSnapshot()` (line 799) and before refreshing availableDevices/currentDevice, the store keeps the PRE-change snapshot — so the main-window/menu-bar output-device name (currentDeviceName) and per-app routing/error/level state remain stale and desynced from the backend until some other event refreshes them. | Fix: Plumb the autoRestoreDevice preference into the backend so it can skip the dispose/reattach when disabled, and/or always update the store's currentDevice + availableDevices on a device-change event (refresh the lightweight snapshot) even when full auto-restore is off, so the UI never shows a stale output device.

Feature: System output device switching
User Story: As a menu-bar user, I want to switch the macOS system output device from inside Waves, so that I can move all audio to headphones/speakers without opening System Settings or Control Center.
Expected Behavior:
• The menu-bar panel shows an output-device picker (speaker icon + current device name) that lists every available output device
• Selecting a device that is not already current calls backend.setDefaultOutputDevice(uid:) which sets kAudioHardwarePropertyDefaultOutputDevice on the system object
• Selecting the already-current device is a no-op (guard device.id != currentDeviceID)
• On success a 'Output switched' toast is shown and the available-device list is refreshed so the checkmark moves to the new device
• The current device is marked with a checkmark; currentDeviceName falls back to 'No output device' when session.currentDevice is nil
• If the chosen device disappeared between enumeration and selection, setDefaultOutputDevice throws managedRouteUnavailable('That output device is no longer available.') and a 'Couldn't switch output' error toast is shown
Acceptance Criteria:
• Picking a different device in the menu-bar picker changes the macOS default output device system-wide
• Picking the current device performs no Core Audio write
• A success toast appears with the device name on a successful switch and the checkmark updates immediately
• An error toast appears when the device is no longer available and no crash occurs
• currentDeviceName shows 'No output device' when there is no current device
Impl Notes: Fully wired through real Core Audio. Minor UX gap: selectOutputDevice does not optimistically update currentDeviceID; the checkmark only moves after the device-change listener fires and the snapshot refreshes, so there can be a brief lag. The main-window header (MainWindowView.swift:442) shows currentDeviceName read-only and offers no switcher, so switching is only available from the menu bar.
Errors Found:
• [HIGH/logic] Checkmark never moves after a manual output switch when 'Auto-restore device' is off (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:759) — Verified: selectOutputDevice (AppStore.swift:759-772) calls backend.setDefaultOutputDevice then refreshes only availableDevices; it never writes session.currentDevice. The picker's checkmark is bound to device.id == store.currentDeviceID (MenuBarMixerView.swift:129), and currentDeviceID derives from session.currentDevice?.id (AppStore.swift:130-131). The only post-switch update to session.currentDevice is the store's handleDeviceChange, which early-returns when preferences.autoRestoreDevice is false (AppStore.swift:793). Note the refreshed availableDevices list DOES carry correct isCurrent flags (backend sets isCurrent: uid==currentUID at WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:677), but the picker ignores isCurrent and uses currentDeviceID, so the checkmark stays on the old device until an unrelated action (a volume change → mergeAppState, or refresh) updates session.currentDevice. | Fix: Either bind the picker checkmark to device.isCurrent (already populated on the freshly fetched availableDevices) instead of currentDeviceID, or optimistically set session.currentDevice in selectOutputDevice after a successful setDefaultOutputDevice. Decouple session.currentDevice refresh from the autoRestoreDevice gate.
• [LOW/ux] Manual switch produces two overlapping toasts (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:767) — Verified: selectOutputDevice shows an 'Output switched' success toast (AppStore.swift:767). The Core Audio default-device listener then fires the backend handleDeviceChange → deviceChangeEvents → store handleDeviceChange, which (when auto-restore is on) shows a second 'Output device changed' info toast (AppStore.swift:811-816) for the same user action. A user who switches output with auto-restore on sees two toasts in quick succession. | Fix: Suppress the handleDeviceChange info toast when the change was self-initiated (set a short-lived flag in selectOutputDevice that handleDeviceChange checks), or skip the info toast when the new device equals the one just selected.
• [LOW/ux] Brief checkmark/name lag on switch even when auto-restore is on (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:760) — Verified: selectOutputDevice does not optimistically update session.currentDevice; with auto-restore on, the checkmark and currentDeviceName only move once the async device-change listener fires and handleDeviceChange replaces session via currentSnapshot(). This is a visible lag between click and the UI updating even on the normal path. (This is the implNotes-acknowledged minor gap for DEV-01.) | Fix: Optimistically update session.currentDevice in selectOutputDevice to the selected device immediately after the backend write succeeds.

Feature: Available output device enumeration
User Story: As a user, I want Waves to show the real set of output devices on my Mac, so that the picker only offers devices I can actually route audio to.
Expected Behavior:
• availableOutputDevices enumerates all Core Audio devices and keeps only those with output streams (hasOutputStreams)
• Waves' own private aggregate devices are excluded: any UID prefixed 'com.waves.aggregate.' and any aggregate whose name contains 'waves' is skipped
• Each device is classified into a DeviceKind (builtInOutput/bluetooth/display/virtual/aggregate/unknown) via keyword matching on UID+name
• Devices are returned sorted case-insensitively by name and the current device is flagged isCurrent
• On macOS older than 14.2 (no per-app routing support) the list is returned empty
• The list is refreshed on menu-bar appearance (onAppear), at startup, after switching device, and on device-change events
• When availableDevices is empty the picker shows 'No output devices found'
Acceptance Criteria:
• Only devices that have output streams appear in the picker
• Waves-created aggregate devices never appear as selectable outputs
• The list is alphabetized and the current device is marked
• On an unsupported OS version the picker is empty and shows the empty-state text
• Refreshing returns an up-to-date device list after hardware is plugged/unplugged
Impl Notes: Solid. deviceKind classification is heuristic string-matching (e.g. 'usb-c' is mapped to .display, 'bluetooth/airpods/beats' to .bluetooth) so kind labels can be wrong for some devices, but kind only affects an icon/label, not routing correctness. recentDeviceIDs is computed and persisted (buildSnapshot:1075) via Array(Set(...)).sorted(), which discards recency order, and it is never surfaced in any UI — effectively dead data.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/inconsistency] Device list is not refreshed on device-change events when 'Auto-restore device' is off (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:808) — Verified: the only device-change-triggered refresh of availableDevices is inside the store's handleDeviceChange (AppStore.swift:808), which early-returns at line 793 when preferences.autoRestoreDevice is false. With auto-restore disabled, plugging/unplugging hardware does not refresh availableDevices until the menu-bar picker's onAppear re-runs refreshOutputDevices (MenuBarMixerView.swift:156) or another action triggers a refresh. The main-window header (MainWindowView.swift:442) offers no picker and never triggers a device refresh. The expectedBehavior explicitly lists 'on device-change events' as a refresh trigger. | Fix: Move the availableDevices (and session.currentDevice) refresh out of the autoRestoreDevice-gated branch so the device list always tracks hardware changes regardless of the auto-restore preference.
• [LOW/dead-code] Name-based Waves-aggregate exclusion guard is unreachable (and doubly moot) (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:672) — Verified: the name guard at WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:672 only fires when kind == .aggregate. Waves names its aggregates 'Waves-<app>' (line 472) with UID 'com.waves.aggregate.<uuid>' (line 473) and no 'aggregate'/'multi-output' token, so deviceKind classifies them as .virtual (the 'waves' token at line 834 is reached before any aggregate match), never .aggregate — so the kind==.aggregate name guard cannot match a real Waves aggregate. Severity is low/cosmetic, not just because the UID-prefix guard at line 669 already excludes them, but ALSO because these aggregates are created with kAudioAggregateDeviceIsPrivateKey: true (line 476), so they are not even enumerated by allDeviceIDs() in the first place. The guard is dead but causes no functional gap. | Fix: Drop the kind==.aggregate condition and skip any device whose name contains 'waves', or remove the misleading name guard entirely and rely on the UID prefix, so the documented behavior matches the code.

Feature: Per-app output device routing (pinning)
User Story: As a power user, I want to pin an individual app to a specific output device, so that, for example, music plays to my speakers while a call stays on my headset.
Expected Behavior:
• A per-app 'Output Device' submenu in the mixer row lists 'System Default' plus every available device, with a checkmark on the current target
• Choosing a device sets app.targetDeviceUID and, if the app is already managed/live, tears down the existing controller and rebuilds the route against the pinned device's aggregate
• Choosing 'System Default' clears targetDeviceUID so the app follows the system default output again
• If the app is not currently managed, the device preference is recorded for next time it is managed (wasManaged guard) without forcing a route
• The pinned device choice is persisted in the session snapshot and reflected in the row subtitle ('→ <device name>')
• Excluded apps cannot have an output device set (guard !isExcluded) and the submenu is hidden for excluded apps
• On success an 'Output set' toast shows 'app → device/System default'; on failure a 'Couldn't route' error toast is shown
• If the pinned device is unavailable when building the route, createController throws managedRouteUnavailable('The chosen output device for <app> is unavailable...') and the row goes to .error state rather than silently falling back to default
Acceptance Criteria:
• Pinning a running, managed app to a device moves only that app's audio to the chosen device
• Selecting 'System Default' returns the app to following the system output
• Pinning a non-managed app records the preference without error and applies it when the app later becomes managed
• The pinned device persists across the session and shows in the row subtitle
• Excluded apps offer no output-device menu
• A pinned device that becomes unavailable puts the app into an error state with an actionable message, not a silent default fallback
Impl Notes: Routing logic is complete and honest about unavailable devices. Edge case: a pinned-device route is built from the app's own aggregate whose main sub-device is the pinned UID, so it does NOT follow the system default — which is the intent. However, when a pinned device is unplugged there is no automatic re-pin or fallback; the route just errors until the user re-picks (createController:437). A pinned app's preference survives in-session but is keyed by logicalID in the snapshot, not in the per-device presets store, so it relies on session persistence to outlive a relaunch.

Feature: Auto-restore managed routes on output-device change
User Story: As a user with managed app volumes, I want Waves to re-establish my per-app routes automatically when I switch output devices, so that my volume/mute/boost settings keep working without manual recovery after plugging in headphones or switching to AirPods.
Expected Behavior:
• A Core Audio listener on kAudioHardwarePropertyDefaultOutputDevice fires handleDeviceChange whenever the default output device changes
• handleDeviceChange (backend) calls autoRestoreDevice(), which records the set of currently-managed logical IDs, disposes all existing controllers, rebuilds the snapshot, then reattaches routes for the previously-managed apps via reattachRoutes
• Each reattached app is set to .managed on success or .error on failure; backendStatus.isRouteRecoveryHealthy reflects whether any route recovered and lastError captures the latest failure
• After restoration the backend emits a deviceChangeEvents signal so the store refreshes UI, diagnostics, and the device list and shows an 'Output device changed' toast
• The store-level handleDeviceChange is gated by the 'Auto-restore device' preference (defaults on); when off, the store skips its refresh/preset work
• A manual 'Recover routes' action (recoverRoutes) provides the same reattach behavior on demand
• The listener is registered once (idempotent guard) and torn down on stop, with the change continuation finished
Acceptance Criteria:
• Switching the system output device while apps are managed results in those apps being re-tapped and their volume/mute/boost reapplied to the new device
• After a device switch the route-recovery health reflects success/failure and lastError is populated on failure
• An 'Output device changed' toast appears with the new device name when the store handles the event
• Disabling 'Auto-restore device' stops the store from acting on the change event
• No duplicate listeners are registered if start() runs twice; stopping removes the listener
Impl Notes: Correctness gap around the preference: the backend's handleDeviceChange ALWAYS calls autoRestoreDevice() and reattaches routes on every device change (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1464), regardless of the 'Auto-restore device' toggle. The preferences.autoRestoreDevice flag only gates the STORE's reaction (AppStore.swift:793) — UI refresh, preset restore, and the toast. So a user who turns auto-restore OFF still gets routes torn down and reattached at the backend level; they just don't see the toast or get presets restored. The preference name implies it disables auto-restore, but the actual route restoration cannot be disabled. Otherwise the restore flow itself is robust (controllers disposed before rebuild, race-safe reattach).
Errors Found:
• [HIGH/inconsistency] 'Auto-restore device' toggle does not actually disable route restoration (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1464) — Verified: the backend's handleDeviceChange unconditionally calls autoRestoreDevice() on every default-output change (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1463-1464), disposing all controllers (disposeControllers(keeping: []) line 230) and rebuilding/reattaching routes regardless of preferences.autoRestoreDevice. The preference only gates the STORE's reaction (AppStore.swift:793) — UI refresh, preset restore, and the toast. The backend has no knowledge of the preference (no setter; grep confirms autoRestoreDevice preference lives only in AppStore/UserPreferences/SettingsView). So with the toggle OFF, every managed app's tap/aggregate is still torn down and reattached on each device switch; only the visible feedback is suppressed. The toggle's label implies it disables auto-restore. | Fix: Thread the autoRestoreDevice preference into the backend (a setter the store keeps in sync, or have the store own the reattach decision) so that when auto-restore is off, the backend's handleDeviceChange only yields the deviceChangeEvents signal and skips autoRestoreDevice(). Alternatively relabel the toggle to reflect that core restoration always happens.
• [HIGH/ux] With auto-restore off, the UI shows a stale output device after a switch (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:793) — Verified: when preferences.autoRestoreDevice is false, the store's handleDeviceChange early-returns (AppStore.swift:793) and never replaces session via currentSnapshot(). The backend has already changed its snapshot's currentDevice and rebuilt routes, but the store's session.currentDevice is not updated, so currentDeviceName (main-window header MainWindowView.swift:442, Audio settings SettingsView.swift:188) and the menu-bar picker keep displaying the previous device. It only self-corrects when an unrelated action runs mergeAppState (which copies backendSession.currentDevice, AppStore.swift:1426) or a refresh occurs — not from the device change itself. | Fix: Always refresh session.currentDevice and availableDevices on a device-change event; gate only the preset-restore and the 'Output device changed' toast behind the autoRestoreDevice preference.
• [MEDIUM/concurrency] Overlapping device changes can drop route reattachment via actor reentrancy (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:223) — Verified by tracing the actor: autoRestoreDevice() captures managedLogicalIDs from apps whose routingState==.managed or with an active controller (lines 224-228), then disposeControllers(keeping: []) (synchronous, line 230), then awaits buildSnapshot (231) and reattachRoutes (235). disposeControllers empties controllers, and buildSnapshot then sets every non-.live app to .monitorOnly (lines 1044-1054 — the controller check at 1044 sees an empty controllers dict). Because the method suspends at await points, a second handleDeviceChange (rapid plug/unplug or AirPods reconnect storm) can interleave on the actor: it recomputes managedLogicalIDs from a snapshot whose routingState is now .monitorOnly and whose controllers are gone, yielding an EMPTY set, so it skips reattachRoutes (line 234 guard). Worse, if the second invocation's disposeControllers(keeping: []) runs while the first call's reattachRoutes is mid-flight (between its own applyRoute awaits), it disposes controllers the first call just rebuilt, and the second call won't rebuild them (empty managed set) — potentially leaving previously-managed apps un-reattached after a burst settles. | Fix: Serialize device-change handling (single-flight guard or debounce/coalesce so only the latest change runs to completion), or snapshot the managed-logical-ID set once at the top and protect it so a concurrent invocation cannot observe an empty set or dispose in-flight rebuilds.

Feature: Per-device volume presets (remember and restore volumes per output device)
User Story: As a user, I want Waves to remember each app's volume, mute, and boost separately per output device, so that switching from speakers to headphones automatically restores the levels I prefer on each.
Expected Behavior:
• When 'Per-device volume presets' is enabled, every volume/mute/boost change is captured into deviceVolumePresets keyed by [deviceID][appLogicalID] as AppVolumeSettings (desiredVolume, isMuted, volumeBoost)
• Volume changes update the in-memory preset on each tick but defer the disk write to commitDesiredVolume to avoid rewriting the file during a slider drag; mute and boost changes persist asynchronously immediately
• On a device change to a NEW device, restoreDeviceVolumePresets applies any saved per-app settings for that device to the live snapshot and the backend (desiredVolume, isMuted, volumeBoost), then re-persists the session
• Presets are stored on disk as deviceVolumePresets.json in Application Support with an atomic write, a 10MB size guard, and corrupt/oversized files backed up to a .corrupt sibling rather than wiped
• Loading a missing file returns empty defaults without writing; the toggle is exposed in Settings and defaults on
Acceptance Criteria:
• With the toggle on, adjusting an app's volume/mute/boost on device A and switching to device B then back to A restores A's saved levels for that app
• Settings persist across relaunch and are stored under Application Support/Waves/deviceVolumePresets.json
• A corrupt or oversized presets file is preserved as .corrupt and the app falls back to empty defaults without crashing
• With the toggle off, no per-device preset is captured or restored
• A slider drag does not rewrite the presets file on every tick (write deferred to commit)
Impl Notes: Capture and persistence are well implemented. The restore path is the weak link: restoreDeviceVolumePresets is only ever called from the store's handleDeviceChange (AppStore.swift:803), which early-returns when preferences.autoRestoreDevice is OFF (AppStore.swift:793). So a user who disables 'Auto-restore device' but keeps 'Per-device volume presets' ON will silently never have presets restored on a device switch — the two toggles are coupled in a way the UI does not communicate. Also, presets are only restored when previousDeviceID != newDeviceID, so a restore is skipped if the snapshot's currentDevice hasn't updated yet when the event is handled. Presets are keyed by deviceID (the device UID); there is no UI to view or clear stored presets (removeDevice exists but is never called), so stale entries for removed devices accumulate.
Errors Found:
• [HIGH/logic] Per-device presets silently never restore when 'Auto-restore device' is off (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:803) — Verified: restoreDeviceVolumePresets is only called from the store's handleDeviceChange (AppStore.swift:803), which early-returns when preferences.autoRestoreDevice is false (AppStore.swift:793). So a user who keeps 'Per-device volume presets' ON but turns 'Auto-restore device' OFF will have presets captured on every change (capture paths at AppStore.swift:371, 508, 555 gate only on enablePerDeviceVolumePresets) but never restored on a device switch. The two Settings toggles are presented as fully independent (SettingsView.swift:78-86 and 105-113, no help text/coupling note) yet are silently coupled. | Fix: Decouple the two preferences: run the per-device preset restore on device-change whenever enablePerDeviceVolumePresets is true, independent of autoRestoreDevice. Move the restore (and the minimal session/currentDevice refresh it needs) outside the autoRestoreDevice gate.
• [MEDIUM/edge-case] With auto-restore off, the first per-device capture after a switch is keyed to the stale (old) device (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:371) — New defect (missed by first tester). Per-device capture in setDesiredVolume/setMuted/setVolumeBoost keys on currentDeviceID = session.currentDevice?.id (AppStore.swift:371, 508, 555, 130-131). When auto-restore is OFF, a device switch does NOT update session.currentDevice (handleDeviceChange early-returns at line 793, and selectOutputDevice never sets it). session.currentDevice only catches up later via mergeAppState (line 1426) AFTER the async backend apply completes. But the capture at the top of setDesiredVolume runs synchronously BEFORE that apply. So the first volume/mute/boost change made on the new device after a switch is written into deviceVolumePresets under the OLD device's UID, corrupting per-device data. Subsequent changes use the corrected device once mergeAppState has run. | Fix: Ensure session.currentDevice is refreshed on every device-change event regardless of autoRestoreDevice (same root fix as DEV-04), so currentDeviceID reflects the live device before any capture occurs.
• [MEDIUM/edge-case] Saved presets for the current device are not applied on relaunch (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:214) — Verified: restoreDeviceVolumePresets is only invoked from handleDeviceChange (a device switch). On startup, start() calls reapplyRestoredAudioState (AppStore.swift:214) which reapplies only the single set of volume/mute/boost values stored in the session snapshot (lines 824-844); it never consults deviceVolumePresets for the current device. If the default output device changed while Waves was closed, the app applies the last-session values rather than that device's saved preset, so per-device levels are not honored across relaunch in that case. | Fix: During start(), after building the snapshot and resolving currentDeviceID, call restoreDeviceVolumePresets(for: currentDeviceID) when enablePerDeviceVolumePresets is true, so the live device's saved preset is applied at launch.
• [LOW/dead-code] removeDevice is dead code; no UI to view or clear stored per-device presets (Sources/Waves/Stores/UserPreferences.swift:111) — Verified: DeviceVolumePresets.removeDevice(_:) (UserPreferences.swift:111) is never called anywhere (grep confirms the only removeDevice* hits are the unrelated removeDeviceChangeListener). There is no UI surface to inspect or clear deviceVolumePresets.json. Presets are keyed by device UID, so entries for removed/forgotten devices accumulate indefinitely with no pruning or user control. | Fix: Either wire removeDevice into a 'Clear stored device presets' action in Settings (and/or prune entries for devices not seen recently), or remove the dead removeDevice method if pruning is intentionally out of scope.

Feature: Output-device visibility in onboarding/setup status
User Story: As a new user, I want the setup checklist to confirm Waves can see my output device, so that I know audio routing has a valid target before I start adjusting volumes.
Expected Behavior:
• Onboarding/setup tracks outputDeviceVisible, set true when the session snapshot has a non-nil currentDevice (syncOnboarding)
• buildSnapshot always produces a currentDevice, falling back to the previous snapshot's device or a synthetic 'System Output' placeholder when Core Audio cannot report one, so the setup step does not falsely fail
• The setup completion/route-health step depends on output device visibility (onboarding step gating)
• The current output device name is shown in Settings and the main window header as read-only status
Acceptance Criteria:
• The setup checklist marks the output-device step complete when a current device is present
• If Core Audio cannot report a device, a 'System Output' placeholder keeps the snapshot's currentDevice non-nil instead of crashing or blanking
• Settings and the main window display the current output device name
Impl Notes: Straightforward and defensive: the synthetic 'System Output' fallback (id 'system-output') guarantees currentDevice is non-nil, so outputDeviceVisible is effectively always true on a supported OS — meaning this setup step rarely, if ever, signals a real problem. It is more of a status indicator than a gating check.

Feature: Capture per-app volume/mute/boost per output device
User Story: As a user who moves between speakers, headphones, and external interfaces, I want Waves to silently remember each app's volume, mute, and boost separately for the device I'm using, so that I don't have to re-tune levels every time I plug in a different output.
Expected Behavior:
• When the per-device feature is enabled and an output device is active, changing an app's volume (setDesiredVolume) records an AppVolumeSettings (desiredVolume, isMuted, volumeBoost) keyed by [deviceID][appLogicalID] in the in-memory deviceVolumePresets
• Muting/unmuting (setMuted) and changing boost (setVolumeBoost) likewise write an updated AppVolumeSettings for the current device
• The settings captured reflect the app's other current attributes (e.g. a volume change captures the app's current isMuted and volumeBoost, not just the changed field)
• deviceVolumes is a nested dictionary [deviceID: [appID: AppVolumeSettings]] so each device has an independent set of per-app settings
• Keys are the app's logicalID (stable across PID changes), and deviceID is session.currentDevice?.id
• If the feature toggle is off, or there is no current output device (currentDeviceID == nil), no preset is recorded
Acceptance Criteria:
• With the toggle on and a device active, setting app A to 40% then reading deviceVolumePresets.getVolumeSettings(for: A.logicalID, deviceID:) returns desiredVolume == 0.4
• Muting app A updates the same entry's isMuted to true while preserving its desiredVolume and volumeBoost
• Switching the active output device and setting app A to a different volume leaves the first device's stored value unchanged
• With the toggle off, changing a volume does not add or modify any entry in deviceVolumes
• With no current output device, changing a volume does not crash and records nothing
Code Refs:
• Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:356
• Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:371
• Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:491
• Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:508
• Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:544
• Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:555
• Sources/Waves/Stores/UserPreferences.swift:97
• Sources/Waves/Stores/UserPreferences.swift:100
• Sources/Waves/Stores/UserPreferences.swift:64
Impl Notes: Capture path is fully wired across volume, mute, and boost mutations and keyed correctly by logicalID + deviceID. Minor staleness concern: setMuted (line 508-514) and setVolumeBoost (line 555-561) build AppVolumeSettings from the passed-in `app` parameter (a snapshot taken at call time) rather than the freshly-mutated session.apps[index], so if the caller's `app` is stale the stored desiredVolume/boost could lag by one edit. In practice each handler only changes one field so this is low-risk, but it is an inconsistency versus setDesiredVolume which reads from the live session row.
Errors Found: • [LOW/inconsistency] setMuted/setVolumeBoost capture the unchanged preset fields from the stale passed-in `app` snapshot, not the live session row (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:510) — setDesiredVolume (line 372) reads `let currentApp = session.apps[index]` and builds the preset from the freshly mutated live row. But setMuted (lines 509-513) builds AppVolumeSettings from `app.desiredVolume`/`app.volumeBoost` and setVolumeBoost (lines 556-560) from `app.desiredVolume`/`app.isMuted` — both read the `app` value parameter the caller passed, not session.apps[index]. If the caller's `app` is one edit behind the live session (a SwiftUI row bound to a value captured before a near-simultaneous change merged in), the persisted preset records a stale desiredVolume/boost. Confirmed: the asymmetry between the three capture sites is real. Severity remains low because in practice the SwiftUI binding for a given row is usually current and only the changed field is authoritative; the window for divergence is narrow. | Fix: After locating `index`, read `let currentApp = session.apps[index]` in setMuted and setVolumeBoost and build AppVolumeSettings from currentApp (using the freshly mutated field for the changed value), mirroring setDesiredVolume.

Feature: Restore saved per-device settings when the output device changes
User Story: As a user who switches output devices while Waves is running, I want my previously-saved volumes, mutes, and boosts for that device to be reapplied automatically, so that each device sounds the way I last left it without manual re-adjustment.
Expected Behavior:
• On a device-change event from the backend, handleDeviceChange captures the previous deviceID, refreshes the session snapshot, and detects the new deviceID
• If the feature is enabled and the device actually changed (previousDeviceID != newDeviceID), restoreDeviceVolumePresets runs for the new device
• For each running app that has a saved AppVolumeSettings for the new device, the stored desiredVolume, isMuted, and volumeBoost are written back into session.apps and pushed to the backend (setDesiredVolume, setMuted, setVolumeBoost)
• Apps with no saved settings for that device are left untouched
• After restoration the session snapshot is persisted and the visible-apps cache invalidated; a 'Output device changed' toast is shown
• Restoration only happens when preferences.autoRestoreDevice is also true, because handleDeviceChange early-returns if it is off
Acceptance Criteria:
• With saved settings for device B and a switch from A to B, each app with a B entry has its session volume/mute/boost updated to the saved values and the same values are sent to the backend
• An app with no entry for device B retains its current volume after the switch
• If previousDeviceID == newDeviceID (spurious event), restoreDeviceVolumePresets is not invoked
• If autoRestoreDevice is off, no preset restoration occurs even when enablePerDeviceVolumePresets is on
• A backend failure while applying one app's settings is logged and does not abort restoration of the remaining apps
Impl Notes: Restoration logic is correct and per-app failures are isolated (logged, loop continues). Two real gaps: (1) restoration is gated behind preferences.autoRestoreDevice (handleDeviceChange returns early at line 793), so a user who wants per-device presets but disables auto-restore-device silently gets no preset restoration — the two settings are coupled in a way the UI does not communicate. (2) restoreDeviceVolumePresets only iterates apps currently present in session.apps, so an app that isn't running at switch time never has its saved settings reapplied even if it launches later on that device.
Errors Found:
• [HIGH/logic] Per-device preset restoration is silently gated behind the unrelated autoRestoreDevice toggle (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:793) — handleDeviceChange() returns at line 793 when preferences.autoRestoreDevice is false; the per-device restore (restoreDeviceVolumePresets) is reached only after that guard, at line 803. 'Auto-restore device' and 'Per-device volume presets' are two independent toggles in SettingsView (lines 78-86 and 105-113) with no UI coupling. A user who turns OFF auto-restore-device but leaves per-device presets ON gets no restoration on a device switch, with no UI indication the two are coupled. Corroborated by feature-status.json implNotes for DVP-02 (line 1791) and the named feature (line 1680). | Fix: Run the preset-restore block whenever enablePerDeviceVolumePresets is true regardless of autoRestoreDevice (move the early-return so it only guards the route/snapshot refresh), or surface the dependency in Settings (disable/explain the per-device toggle when auto-restore is off).
• [MEDIUM/edge-case] Saved presets are never reapplied to an app that launches after the device switch (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:849) — restoreDeviceVolumePresets (line 849) iterates only `session.apps.indices` — apps present at the moment of the device-change event. An app that launches later, while that device is still active, never has its saved AppVolumeSettings applied because nothing re-runs device-preset restoration on app appearance; the merge path restores the persisted session values, not the device-specific preset. Confirmed; corroborated by feature-status.json (lines 1680, 1791). | Fix: On new-app appearance (mergedSession / app-discovery path), if per-device presets are enabled look up deviceVolumePresets.getVolumeSettings(for: app.logicalID, deviceID: currentDeviceID) and apply it for newly added apps.
• [MEDIUM/correctness] Restore updates desiredVolume/isMuted/volumeBoost but leaves appliedVolume and routingState stale (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:852) — restoreDeviceVolumePresets writes settings.desiredVolume/isMuted/volumeBoost into session.apps[index] (lines 852-854) and pushes them to the backend, but never updates session.apps[index].appliedVolume or routingState. Contrast reapplyRestoredAudioState (lines 837-838) which sets appliedVolume = isMuted ? 0 : desiredVolume and routingState = .managed. After restore the row's appliedVolume retains the prior snapshot value, so applied/mute visuals and any appliedVolume-derived logic disagree with the restored desiredVolume until the next backend merge. Confirmed by direct comparison of the two functions. | Fix: After writing the three fields, set session.apps[index].appliedVolume = settings.isMuted ? 0 : settings.desiredVolume and routingState = .managed (matching reapplyRestoredAudioState).
• [LOW/concurrency] Index-based restore loop with awaits can write to the wrong app if session.apps is replaced concurrently (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:850) — restoreDeviceVolumePresets loops over session.apps.indices and, across three `await backend.set...` suspension points per iteration, subscripts session.apps[index]. AppStore is @MainActor (line 24), so a true data race / out-of-bounds crash cannot happen mid-statement, but another @MainActor task CAN run during an await: mergeAppState assigns `session = backendSession` when an app isn't found (lines 1419/1433), and scheduleVolumeApply's completion (line 429) calls mergeAppState. If that replaces session.apps between this loop's awaits, a later subscript at the captured `index` can write the next app's restored fields onto a different positional row (mergeAppState normally preserves order, so a full-replace mismatch is the realistic case, not a crash). Severity downgraded from medium to low because MainActor serialization rules out the OOB-crash framing the first tester gave and order is usually preserved; the wrong-app write is a narrow window. | Fix: Snapshot (logicalID, settings) pairs up front, then after each await re-resolve via session.apps.firstIndex(matchingAppKey:) before mutating, skipping apps that disappeared.
• [LOW/concurrency] In-flight slider commit can overwrite a just-restored value because device change does not cancel pending volume-apply tasks (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:802) — handleDeviceChange/restoreDeviceVolumePresets never cancel pendingVolumeApplyTasks or clear pendingVolumeTargets. If a slider commit for app A is still in flight (sleeping on volumeApplyDelay at line 419, or awaiting backend.setDesiredVolume at line 427) when a device change restores A's preset, the pending task completes afterward and re-pushes pendingVolumeTargets[A] (the prior device's drag target) to the backend, clobbering the restored level. setExcluded shows the correct pattern (cancel + remove at lines 709-710). Confirmed reachable. | Fix: Before restoring, cancel pendingVolumeApplyTasks and remove pendingVolumeTargets for apps being restored, or clear the pending target for each restored appID so restore takes precedence.

Feature: Persist per-device presets to disk and reload across launches
User Story: As a user, I want my per-device volume memory to survive quitting and relaunching Waves, so that my carefully tuned per-device levels are not lost between sessions.
Expected Behavior:
• DeviceVolumePresetsStore writes deviceVolumePresets as pretty-printed JSON to Application Support/Waves/deviceVolumePresets.json using an atomic write on a background queue
• commitDesiredVolume flushes the in-memory presets to disk after a slider interaction (per-tick writes during a drag are deferred to avoid rewriting the file on every change)
• setMuted and setVolumeBoost each persist asynchronously via a Task after updating the in-memory presets
• On init, AppStore loads the persisted presets via deviceVolumePresetsStore.load(); a missing file is treated as the normal first-launch case and yields empty defaults without writing
• Saving/loading is decoded through PersistedSchema (schema-versioned) and tolerant of older AppVolumeSettings missing volumeBoost (defaults to 1.0)
Acceptance Criteria:
• After changing a volume and committing, deviceVolumePresets.json exists and contains the app/device entry
• Relaunching the app loads the same deviceVolumePresets into AppStore.deviceVolumePresets
• First launch with no file present returns empty presets and does not create the file until a save occurs
• An AppVolumeSettings JSON object lacking volumeBoost decodes with volumeBoost == 1.0
• Writes occur off the main thread and use atomic replacement
Impl Notes: Persistence is solid: atomic background writes, schema-versioned decode, and graceful first-launch. One UX gap versus the README claim ('Remember volume settings for each app across different audio devices'): saved presets are NOT reapplied on app launch — start() calls reapplyRestoredAudioState() (which replays the persisted session snapshot, DVP-04) but never calls restoreDeviceVolumePresets for the current device. So at launch the device-specific presets only matter once the user switches devices; if the last persisted session was tuned on a different device than the one active at launch, the wrong device's levels apply until a switch occurs.
Errors Found: • [MEDIUM/inconsistency] Device-specific presets are not applied at launch for the currently-active device (only on a later device switch) (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:214) — start() calls reapplyRestoredAudioState() (line 214), which replays the persisted session snapshot's volumes/mutes/boosts, but never calls restoreDeviceVolumePresets(for: currentDeviceID). Device-preset restoration only runs inside handleDeviceChange when previousDeviceID != newDeviceID. So if the last persisted session was tuned while a different device was active than the one at launch, the wrong device's levels apply at startup and the active device's saved presets are ignored until the user switches devices. Confirmed; corroborated by feature-status.json DVP-03 implNotes (line 1829). | Fix: In start(), after reapplyRestoredAudioState() and once currentDeviceID is known, if preferences.enablePerDeviceVolumePresets call restoreDeviceVolumePresets(for: currentDeviceID).

Feature: Resilient load on corrupt or oversized preset files
User Story: As a user, I don't want a damaged or tampered preset file to crash Waves or silently erase all my saved per-device levels, so that I can recover or at least keep using the app with sane defaults.
Expected Behavior:
• On load, if the file size exceeds the 10MB cap, the file is backed up to deviceVolumePresets.json.corrupt and empty defaults are returned
• If decoding throws, the unreadable file is preserved by moving it to a .corrupt sidecar (logged) and empty defaults are returned instead of wiping data
• backupCorruptFile removes any prior .corrupt backup first, then moves the bad file aside
• If the Application Support directory cannot be obtained, the store falls back to ~/.Waves/deviceVolumePresets.json
• All load/save work runs on a dedicated serial queue (load synchronously, save asynchronously)
Acceptance Criteria:
• A malformed JSON preset file causes load() to return empty DeviceVolumePresets and leaves a .corrupt backup on disk
• A file larger than 10MB is rejected, backed up, and defaults returned
• Calling load() twice on a corrupt file does not throw and overwrites the single .corrupt backup rather than accumulating many
• With Application Support unavailable, presets are read/written under ~/.Waves without crashing
Impl Notes: Error and edge-case handling is thorough and clearly intentional (preserve-not-destroy on corruption, size cap to bound memory, directory fallback). No user-facing notification is shown when a corrupt file is detected — recovery is silent (log only) — which is acceptable but means a user who loses presets to corruption gets no explanation. AppVolumeSettings/DeviceVolumePresets have no clamping on decode of desiredVolume beyond AppVolumeSettings' boost clamp, so an out-of-range desiredVolume from a hand-edited file would pass through; downstream setDesiredVolume clamps to [0,1] so audible effect is bounded.
Errors Found:
• [LOW/ux] Corruption recovery is entirely silent — user is never told their per-device presets were reset (Sources/Waves/Services/Persistence/DeviceVolumePresetsStore.swift:51) — On an oversized (>10MB) or undecodable preset file, load() backs the file up to .corrupt and returns empty DeviceVolumePresets, logging only (DeviceVolumePresetsStore.swift lines 40-42 and 51-53). No toast or user-facing signal; load() returns no recoverable flag the caller could surface. A user whose presets vanish after corruption gets no explanation and no pointer to the .corrupt backup. Confirmed. | Fix: Have load() return/expose a recoverable flag (or post a notification) when backupCorruptFile() runs, and show a one-time warning toast from AppStore.
• [LOW/edge-case] desiredVolume is not range-validated on decode of a hand-edited/tampered preset file (Sources/Waves/Stores/UserPreferences.swift:83) — AppVolumeSettings clamps volumeBoost to [1,4] in init (UserPreferences.swift:72), but desiredVolume is stored as-is (line 70/83-86); a tampered deviceVolumePresets.json with desiredVolume = 9.0 or -2.0 decodes unclamped and is stored back. Audible effect is bounded because setDesiredVolume clamps to [0,1] downstream, but restoreDeviceVolumePresets assigns session.apps[index].desiredVolume = settings.desiredVolume (AppStore.swift:852) before any clamp, carrying the out-of-range value into the slider model. Confirmed. | Fix: Clamp desiredVolume to [0,1] in AppVolumeSettings.init alongside the existing boost clamp.

Feature: Enable/disable per-device presets via Settings toggle
User Story: As a user who prefers one global volume per app regardless of device, I want a settings toggle to turn per-device volume presets off, so that Waves stops recording and restoring device-specific levels.
Expected Behavior:
• Settings shows a 'Per-device volume presets' toggle bound to preferences.enablePerDeviceVolumePresets
• Toggling it updates the preference and immediately persists preferences (persistPreferences)
• The preference defaults to true (on) for new installs
• When off, all capture sites (setDesiredVolume/setMuted/setVolumeBoost) skip writing presets and handleDeviceChange skips restoreDeviceVolumePresets
• The preference decodes resiliently: an older preferences file missing the key loads with the default (true) rather than failing
Acceptance Criteria:
• Toggling the setting off then on round-trips through preferences and persists across relaunch
• With the setting off, a volume change makes no change to deviceVolumePresets and a device switch does not reapply saved levels
• A new install starts with the feature enabled
• A preferences JSON predating enablePerDeviceVolumePresets decodes with the field == true and other settings intact
Impl Notes: Toggle is fully wired and persisted, default-on, with backward-compatible decoding covered by a test. UX nuance: disabling the toggle stops capture and restore but does NOT clear any presets already saved on disk, and there is no UI to view, edit, or delete stored per-device settings (see DVP-06). The toggle's effect is also entangled with autoRestoreDevice for the restore half (DVP-02).

But (1) turning it off leaves every saved entry on disk with no clear/reset UI (see DVP-06 — removeDevice has no callers), and (2) the restore half is gated behind autoRestoreDevice (DVP-02), yet the toggle has no .help() text or visual coupling (verified: only the URL-scheme toggle has .help() at line 125; the per-device toggle has none). Plain Toggle gives no feedback and no scope description. Confirmed; corroborated by feature-status.json (line 1900). | Fix: Add a .help() describing that restoration also requires 'Auto-restore device', and provide a 'Clear stored per-device settings' action wiring removeDevice / a clear-all.

Feature: Pruning/management of stale device entries (gap)
User Story: As a user who connects many short-lived or one-off audio devices, I want stale per-device preset entries to be cleaned up or manageable, so that the preset file doesn't grow unbounded with devices I'll never use again and I can reset a device's settings.
Expected Behavior:
• DeviceVolumePresets exposes removeDevice(deviceID:) to drop all stored settings for a device
• A 10MB file-size cap exists as a last-resort guard against unbounded growth (oversized files are discarded on load)
Acceptance Criteria:
• There exists a code path or UI that removes presets for devices that are no longer present / that the user wants to reset
• removeDevice is invoked somewhere in the app flow (device removal, settings 'clear', etc.)
• The preset store cannot grow without bound across many transient devices
Impl Notes: DeviceVolumePresets.removeDevice(_:) is defined but has ZERO callers anywhere in Sources — confirmed via grep. There is no pruning when a device is unplugged/forgotten and no UI to view, edit, reset, or delete per-device presets. Entries for every device ever used accumulate indefinitely (keyed by Core Audio device UID/ID), bounded only by the coarse 10MB load-time cap which discards the entire file rather than pruning. Disabling the feature toggle also does not clear stored data. This is a real management/UX gap rather than a correctness crash, hence P3.

No code path removes presets when a device is unplugged/forgotten, and there is no UI to view, edit, reset, or delete per-device presets — only the on/off toggle. Entries accumulate for every Core Audio device ever used, bounded only by the destructive 10MB load-time cap (DeviceVolumePresetsStore.swift:39-43) which discards the whole file rather than pruning; disabling the toggle does not clear data. Confirmed via grep; corroborated by feature-status.json (lines 1928, 5490). | Fix: Wire removeDevice into a device-removal handler (prune entries for devices absent from availableOutputDevices) and/or add a Settings affordance to view/delete/reset a device's presets, plus a clear-all.

Feature: Save current mix as a named preset
User Story: As a macOS user who has dialed in per-app volumes, I want to save the current mix as a named preset, so that I can recall that exact configuration later without re-adjusting every slider.
Expected Behavior:
• A '+' toolbar button (help/label 'Save preset') in the main window opens a 'Save Preset' sheet with a text field and Cancel/Save buttons
• Save is disabled while the trimmed name is empty; pressing Save (default action / Return) commits
• The name is trimmed of whitespace/newlines and rejected client-side if empty or longer than 100 characters
• On save, the backend captures every app in the current snapshot into PresetEntry values (appID, desiredVolume, isMuted, volumeBoost)
• Apps in the user's excluded list are filtered out of the saved entries so re-applying won't re-tap them
• If a preset already exists with the same name (case-insensitive), it is overwritten in place — keeping the original id/name/createdAt and bumping updatedAt — instead of creating a duplicate
• Otherwise the new preset is appended to the library and the whole list is persisted via PresetStore
• A success toast 'Preset saved' with the name is shown; backend failures show a 'Save failed' toast
Acceptance Criteria:
• Saving with a non-empty name creates a preset whose entries reflect current app volumes/mute/boost
• Saving with a name matching an existing preset (any case) replaces it rather than duplicating, preserving the original id and createdAt
• Excluded app IDs never appear in the saved preset's entries
• A name >100 chars or empty/whitespace-only does not create a preset
• After save, the preset appears in the Settings Presets list and the sidebar Presets section
• Saved presets survive an app relaunch (written to presets.json)
Impl Notes: Fully wired end-to-end. Two UX concerns: (1) saveCurrentPreset captures ALL apps in the snapshot (not just managed/active ones), so a saved preset can include dozens of momentary apps at their default volume=1.0/unmuted, which then re-asserts those volumes on every apply. (2) Name collision is resolved silently with no overwrite confirmation, so a user can clobber an existing preset by reusing a name. The 100-char limit is enforced only in the view's savePreset(), not in AppStore.savePreset, but the backend Preset has no length cap of its own.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/ux] Save button silently no-ops for names over 100 characters (dead control, no feedback) (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MainWindowView.swift:143) — In SavePresetSheet the Save button is disabled only when the trimmed name is empty (MainWindowView.swift:612). For a name >100 chars the button stays enabled, but savePreset() hits `guard trimmed.count <= maxLength else { return }` (line 143) and returns BEFORE store.savePreset or dismissPresetSheet(). No toast is shown and the sheet stays open, so Save/Return appears to do nothing. There is no length cap on the TextField, so a >100-char name is easy to produce via paste. | Fix: Disable Save when trimmed.count > 100 (mirror the empty-name disable at line 612) with inline helper text, or in savePreset() show a 'Name too long (max 100 characters)' warning toast in the >100 branch instead of a bare return.
• [LOW/dead-code] Backend saveCurrentPreset appends to a write-only presets array that is never read (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:200) — WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.saveCurrentPreset does `presets.append(preset)` (line 200) on the backend's private `presets` property (declared line 13, seeded line 25). I grepped every use of `presets` in the file: it is written at init and at line 200 but never read anywhere. The authoritative library lives in AppStore.presets and is persisted via presetStore.save; the backend copy silently accumulates every saved preset for the process lifetime and is dead state. | Fix: Remove the `presets` stored property and the append; have saveCurrentPreset return the constructed Preset without mutating backend state.

Feature: Apply a saved preset to restore a mix
User Story: As a user switching contexts (focus, meeting, gaming), I want to apply a saved preset, so that all my app volumes, mutes, and boosts snap back to that known configuration in one click.
Expected Behavior:
• Saved presets are listed in the main-window sidebar 'Presets' section and in the menu-bar 'Presets' quick picker; clicking one calls applyPreset
• applyPreset first strips entries for currently-excluded apps so they are never re-tapped
• The backend walks each preset entry, finds the matching app by logicalID, and sets desiredVolume, isMuted, and volumeBoost, then applies the route
• appliedVolume is set to 0 when muted or to desiredVolume otherwise; successful apps move to routingState .managed
• On success the session snapshot is refreshed, diagnostics updated, auto-pause-music re-checked, the snapshot persisted, and a 'Preset applied' toast shown
• On a thrown error the store falls back to the backend's current snapshot, refreshes diagnostics, persists, and shows a 'Preset apply failed' error toast
Acceptance Criteria:
• Applying a preset sets each matching running app's volume/mute/boost to the stored values
• Apps in the preset that are not currently present in the snapshot are skipped without aborting the rest
• Excluded apps are not affected by an applied preset even if the preset contains them
• A successful apply shows the 'Preset applied' toast with the preset name; a backend failure shows an error toast and leaves the session in a consistent state
• Per-app route failures during apply mark that app's routingState as .error and surface lastError without failing the whole apply
Impl Notes: Core restore path is solid and resilient (per-entry errors don't abort the whole apply). Gap: entries whose appID isn't currently in the snapshot are silently skipped (continue) with no user feedback, so applying a 'Focus' preset when Spotify isn't running quietly does nothing for Spotify and the toast still says 'Preset applied' — the user can't tell which apps were actually restored. Per-app route errors are aggregated into a single backendStatus.lastError and a generic success toast, so partial failures may not be obvious.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/inconsistency] Applying a named preset does not update per-device presets, so a later device switch silently reverts it (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1001) — applyPreset (AppStore.swift:1001) sets each app's desiredVolume/isMuted/volumeBoost via the backend but never writes the new values into deviceVolumePresets. I grepped every deviceVolumePresets.saveVolumeSettings call — only setDesiredVolume (380), setMuted (514), setVolumeBoost (561) update it, never applyPreset. With per-device presets ON (default), the per-device store keeps the pre-preset volume for the current device; on the next output-device switch handleDeviceChange -> restoreDeviceVolumePresets (848) re-applies those stale stored settings, silently undoing the preset the user just applied for any app that has a prior per-device entry. | Fix: After a successful applyPreset, when enablePerDeviceVolumePresets and a currentDeviceID exist, write each applied app's settings into deviceVolumePresets (and persist), so the named-preset result and the per-device store agree.
• [LOW/dead-code] Store's 'Preset apply failed' fallback is unreachable with the shipped backend (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1021) — WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.applyPreset (line 156) is `async throws` but its body never propagates a throw: the only throwing call (applyRoute) is in a per-entry do/catch that swallows the error and marks that app .error (lines 166-181); firstIndex/array access cannot throw. So the catch in AppStore.applyPreset (line 1021) that resets `session = await backend.currentSnapshot()` and shows the 'Preset apply failed' toast can never fire on the production path. The protocol declares throws, so the catch is defensible for other conformers but is dead for the shipped backend. | Fix: Either keep/document the catch as defensive for non-shipping backends, or have the backend throw when zero entries could be applied / a fatal failure occurs so the user-facing 'Preset apply failed' path is reachable.
• [LOW/ux] Menu-bar Presets quick picker shows an empty, dead menu when no presets exist (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MenuBarMixerView.swift:164) — PresetQuickPicker (MenuBarMixerView.swift:160) is rendered unconditionally (line 40) and ForEach's over store.presets with no empty-state row. With zero presets (e.g. after deleting both seeded defaults) the menu opens to nothing — unlike the main-window sidebar which hides the Presets section entirely when presets.isEmpty (MainWindowView.swift:261). A state-desync between the menu-bar and main-window surfaces. | Fix: When store.presets.isEmpty, hide the picker or show a disabled 'No presets yet' item, matching the sidebar's conditional rendering.

Feature: Manage the preset library (list, empty state, delete)
User Story: As a user accumulating presets, I want to see and delete my saved presets in Settings, so that I can keep the library tidy and remove ones I no longer use.
Expected Behavior:
• Settings has a 'Presets' tab showing each preset's name and an entry count ('N app'/'N apps')
• When no presets exist, an empty-state line is shown: 'No presets yet. Save a mix from the main window, or import one.'
• Each row has a per-row 'Delete' (destructive) button with accessibility label 'Delete preset <name>', and the List also supports swipe/onDelete
• deletePresets removes the preset(s) at the given offsets, persists the updated list, and shows a 'Preset removed' info toast
• First launch with no presets.json seeds two default presets ('Focus', 'Meeting') onto disk and into the library
Acceptance Criteria:
• The Presets settings tab lists all saved presets with correct singular/plural app counts
• Deleting a preset removes it from the list and from presets.json and shows a 'Preset removed' toast
• With zero presets the empty-state message is displayed
• On a clean install the 'Focus' and 'Meeting' default presets appear without user action
Impl Notes: List/delete/empty-state are complete. There is no rename or reorder affordance — to change a preset's name the user must delete and re-save, or import a renamed JSON. The seeded default presets ('Focus'/'Meeting') hardcode legacy entries without volumeBoost (defaulting to 1.0); they reference fixed bundle IDs (Safari, Zoom, Spotify, Discord) that most users won't all have, so applying a default preset often touches only a subset of apps. Delete is immediate with no confirmation (only a toast), though it is reversible only by re-saving.

Feature: Export and import presets as JSON
User Story: As a user who wants to back up or share my configurations, I want to export a preset to a JSON file and import presets from JSON, so that I can move them between machines or share them with others.
Expected Behavior:
• Each preset row in Settings has an 'Export' button that opens an NSSavePanel pre-filled with '<name>.json' (JSON content type) and writes the pretty-printed encoded preset atomically
• An 'Import' button opens an NSOpenPanel limited to single JSON files
• Import rejects files larger than 10MB, presets with an empty name, and presets with more than 1000 entries, each with a specific error toast
• On import, a name collision (case-insensitive) overwrites the existing preset in place preserving its id/name/createdAt; otherwise the imported preset is assigned a fresh UUID and createdAt before being appended
• After a successful import the library is persisted and a 'Preset imported' success toast is shown; export shows 'Preset exported'
• If no main window is available, export/import show a failure toast instead of crashing
Acceptance Criteria:
• Exporting writes a valid, re-importable pretty-printed JSON file named after the preset
• Importing a well-formed preset adds it to the library and persists it
• Importing a file >10MB, with an empty name, or >1000 entries is rejected with the corresponding error toast and no library change
• Importing a preset whose name matches an existing one replaces that preset rather than duplicating it
• An imported non-colliding preset receives a new UUID so it never collides with an existing SwiftUI list identity
Impl Notes: Robust import validation (size, name, entry count) and atomic export. Minor concern: imported PresetEntry values are sanitized by PresetEntry.init (appID truncated to 256, volume clamped 0–1, boost clamped 1–4), but there is no validation of the appID format itself, and import name-collision overwrites silently with no confirmation. Export uses a fresh JSONEncoder rather than PersistedSchema, so exported files lack the schema-version wrapper used internally — round-tripping works because import uses a plain JSONDecoder, but the on-disk export format diverges from the internal persisted format.

Feature: Apply a preset via waves:// URL automation
User Story: As a power user automating my setup, I want to trigger a named preset from a waves://apply-preset URL, so that scripts, Shortcuts, or links can switch my mix hands-free.
Expected Behavior:
• A waves://apply-preset?name=<name> URL is routed to handleURLApplyPreset
• The name query item is required and must be <=256 characters, otherwise a 'URL command blocked' warning toast is shown
• The preset is matched case-insensitively by name; if no match, a 'Preset not found: <name>' warning toast is shown (name truncated to 64 chars in the message)
• On a match, the normal applyPreset path runs (including excluded-app stripping and toasts)
• URL command handling is gated behind a user preference (URL automation is off by default)
Acceptance Criteria:
• waves://apply-preset?name=Focus applies the 'Focus' preset when URL automation is enabled
• A missing/oversized name parameter produces a 'URL command blocked' toast and applies nothing
• An unknown preset name produces a 'Preset not found' toast and applies nothing
• Name matching is case-insensitive
Impl Notes: Clean, validated, and correctly reuses applyPreset so all the excluded-app and toast logic is consistent. Inherits PRE-02's limitation that apps not currently running are silently skipped. The 256-char name cap here is looser than the 100-char cap enforced when saving via the UI, so a URL could reference a name that the in-app save flow would never produce.
Errors Found: • [LOW/inconsistency] Unknown-preset URL uses toast title 'URL command blocked' instead of 'Preset not found' (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:338) — handleURLApplyPreset, when no preset matches, calls showToast(title: "URL command blocked", detail: "Preset not found: <name>", ...) at AppStore.swift:338. The story's acceptance criteria describe a 'Preset not found' toast; the descriptive text lives only in the detail line while the prominent title says 'URL command blocked' — the same title used for a malformed/oversized name parameter (line 333), conflating two distinct failure modes. | Fix: Set the title to 'Preset not found' for the no-match case so it is distinguishable from the invalid-parameter 'URL command blocked' case and matches the story copy.

Feature: Per-device automatic volume presets (save/restore per output device)
User Story: As a user who switches between speakers, headphones, and a dock, I want each app's volume/mute/boost remembered separately per output device and auto-restored when I switch devices, so that my headphone mix doesn't follow me onto loud desk speakers.
Expected Behavior:
• When the 'Per-device volume presets' preference is enabled (on by default), changing an app's volume/mute/boost stores an AppVolumeSettings keyed by deviceID and app logicalID in DeviceVolumePresets
• In-memory presets update on every change but disk writes are deferred/coalesced to avoid thrashing DeviceVolumePresetsStore
• On detecting an output-device change (previousDeviceID != newDeviceID), restoreDeviceVolumePresets re-applies each app's stored settings for the new device via the backend and re-persists the session
• Stored settings clamp volumeBoost to [1.0,4.0]; decoding tolerates missing fields with defaults (volume 1.0, unmuted, boost 1.0)
• Persistence uses a separate deviceVolumePresets.json file with 10MB size guard and corrupt-file backup, defaulting to empty presets on first launch or unreadable data
Acceptance Criteria:
• With the feature on, adjusting an app's volume while on device A and switching to device B restores B's previously-saved volume for that app
• Per-device data is persisted to deviceVolumePresets.json and survives relaunch
• With the feature off, device switches do not rewrite app volumes from stored per-device data
• A corrupt or oversized deviceVolumePresets.json is backed up to .corrupt and the app falls back to empty presets without losing the bad file
• First launch with no file returns empty presets and writes nothing until a change occurs
Impl Notes: This is a separate auto-preset subsystem from the named-preset library and is the README's 'Per-Device Volume Presets' feature. It is fully wired but has edge-case gaps: restoreDeviceVolumePresets logs per-app backend failures but surfaces nothing to the user, so a failed restore after a device swap is silent. There is no UI to inspect, edit, or clear per-device stored volumes (only removeDevice exists on the model and isn't exposed); stale entries accumulate per deviceID indefinitely. Restore depends on a correct currentDeviceID at switch time — if device identity changes (e.g. a renamed/aggregate device), stored settings won't match and won't restore.
Errors Found:
• [HIGH/logic] Per-device volume restore is gated behind autoRestoreDevice, not just enablePerDeviceVolumePresets (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:793) — handleDeviceChange begins with `guard preferences.autoRestoreDevice else { return }` (AppStore.swift:793); the per-device restore call restoreDeviceVolumePresets (line 803) sits inside that guard, as does the entire snapshot refresh / availableDevices refresh / toast. autoRestoreDevice and enablePerDeviceVolumePresets are two independent Settings toggles (SettingsView.swift:79 and :106) with no documented dependency; both default true so the default config works. But if a user turns OFF 'Auto-restore device' while leaving 'Per-device volume presets' ON, the whole device-change handler returns immediately — per-device volumes are still saved on change but never re-applied on a switch. selectOutputDevice (line 759) routes manual switches through the same event, so even an explicit device switch won't restore. | Fix: Decouple the features: in handleDeviceChange refresh the snapshot and call restoreDeviceVolumePresets when enablePerDeviceVolumePresets is on, regardless of autoRestoreDevice; let autoRestoreDevice gate only the managed-route re-establishment. Move the restoreDeviceVolumePresets branch out from under the autoRestoreDevice guard.
• [LOW/inconsistency] Disk-write coalescing only applies to volume; mute and boost write the file on every change (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:517) — For volume, the in-memory preset updates per tick and the disk write defers to commitDesiredVolume (AppStore.swift:389-391), matching the story's coalescing claim. But setMuted (lines 516-518) and setVolumeBoost (lines 562-564) each call deviceVolumePresetsStore.save(deviceVolumePresets) inside a Task on EVERY change with no coalescing. Boost can be stepped rapidly, so the full deviceVolumePresets.json is re-encoded and rewritten per change. | Fix: Route mute/boost per-device persistence through the same deferred/coalesced commit used for volume (or a debounced save), so rapid boost/mute changes do not rewrite the file each time.

Feature: Preset persistence integrity and corruption recovery
User Story: As a user who relies on my saved presets, I want them stored durably and protected when the file is damaged, so that a corrupt file never silently destroys my whole preset library.
Expected Behavior:
• Presets persist to Application Support/Waves/presets.json, with a ~/.Waves fallback directory if Application Support is unavailable
• Loading checks file size against a 10MB cap and, if exceeded, backs up the file (.corrupt) and returns defaults
• If decoding fails, the unreadable file is preserved by moving it to presets.json.corrupt and defaults are returned (the user's data is not destroyed in place)
• Saves are serialized on a dedicated queue and written atomically; load uses the PersistedSchema versioned codec
• A missing presets.json on first launch is treated as normal and seeded with default presets
Acceptance Criteria:
• A valid presets.json round-trips through save/load without data loss
• A corrupt or oversized presets.json is moved to a .corrupt backup and the app continues with defaults instead of crashing or wiping data
• Concurrent saves do not interleave or produce partially-written files (atomic write on a serial queue)
• First launch seeds and persists the default presets
Impl Notes: Solid defensive persistence: corrupt files are preserved rather than overwritten, size is bounded, writes are atomic and queue-serialized. One subtlety: on a corrupt file the loader returns Preset.defaults, which the caller then holds in memory; the next save will overwrite the (now backed-up) path with defaults, so without manual recovery from the .corrupt file the user effectively reverts to the two seeded presets. The backup uses a single fixed .corrupt name, so a second corruption overwrites the first backup.

Feature: Export a preset to a JSON file
User Story: As a Waves user, I want to export a saved preset to a JSON file, so that I can back it up or send my mix to someone else.
Expected Behavior:
• Each preset row in Settings > Presets shows an 'Export' button (SettingsView.swift:246-250).
• Tapping Export encodes the chosen Preset with a pretty-printed JSONEncoder (AppStore.swift:1082-1084).
