• [LOW/inconsistency] 'Running apps' count counts visible (filtered) apps, not actually running apps (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:155-159) — Verified: sourceInventorySummary labels the count 'running app(s)' but computes it from visibleApps.count (AppStore.swift:155-159), which excludes system-category apps when 'Show system processes' is off. The acceptance criterion explicitly ties the number to visible apps (which the code satisfies), so this is a label-wording mismatch rather than a functional defect: the header under-reports true running audio apps when system processes are hidden. | Fix: Relabel to 'Apps shown' / 'Visible apps' to match what is counted, or compute from the unfiltered running set if 'Running apps' wording is intended.

Feature: Setup checklist with live completion progress
User Story: As a new Waves user, I want a guided setup checklist that shows which prerequisites for per-app audio are met, so that I know whether Waves is ready to manage my audio and what is still missing.
Expected Behavior:
• The Setup view shows a 'Welcome to Waves' header and renders four ordered steps: confirm managed audio support, validate output device visibility, test device switching, and enable keyboard-shortcut (Accessibility) permission (OnboardingView.swift:7-28,45-72).
• A ProgressView reflects completion as completedSteps/3 over the first three steps only (audioComponentInstalled, outputDeviceVisible, routeHealthReady); the Accessibility step is intentionally excluded from progress and from isFullyComplete (OnboardingView.swift:30-43).
• Each step row shows one of three states via icon/color: complete (green checkmark), needs-action (warning triangle + action button), or pending (gray circle), with matching accessibility labels 'Completed'/'Needs action'/'Pending' (OnboardingView.swift:185-218).
• When all three core steps are complete the header switches to 'You're all set!', the progress tint turns green, and a green 'Ready to use' summary card listing post-setup capabilities is shown (OnboardingView.swift:51-58,74-117).
• Step completion is driven by syncOnboarding mapping backend status onto OnboardingState: audioComponentInstalled, hasRequiredPermissions, AXIsProcessTrusted(), currentDevice != nil, and isRouteRecoveryHealthy (AppStore.swift:1356-1364).
• The checklist updates only when a refresh/recover/device-change re-runs syncOnboarding; there is no continuous polling of permission/device state (AppStore.swift:228-254,1181-1202,1356-1364).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Given a fresh launch with no managed support, the progress bar reads 0% and steps 0-2 show the warning state.
• Given audioComponentInstalled, outputDeviceVisible, and routeHealthReady are all true, the header reads 'You're all set!', the bar is full and green, and the 'Ready to use' card is visible.
• Granting only Accessibility (step 3) does not change the progress bar or trigger the 'You're all set!' state.
• Each step icon and its accessibility label correctly reflect complete/needs-action/pending for the underlying flag.
Impl Notes: Wired and functional as a status checklist. Concern: progress is hard-coded to /3 and excludes the Accessibility step, so a user who completes that optional step sees no progress credit, and the meaning of '100%' silently ignores one of the four listed steps. State is snapshot-driven, not live, so the checklist can show stale completion until the user triggers a refresh.
Errors Found:
• [HIGH/correctness] Checklist shows a false "You're all set!" from stale persisted backendStatus on launch (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:100) — Verified: backendStatus (including isRouteRecoveryHealthy/isAudioComponentInstalled/hasRequiredPermissions) is fully Codable (AudioSessionSnapshot.swift:45-64) and is persisted to disk by SessionStore.save (it copies snapshot.backendStatus verbatim into the payload). At init the persisted session is loaded (AppStore.swift:83) and syncOnboarding(using: session) runs synchronously at AppStore.swift:100, seeding routeHealthReady/audioComponentInstalled/outputDeviceVisible from the stale snapshot. In start() the warm path also re-syncs from the same warmSnapshot (AppStore.swift:206) before backend.start(). Only the post-start sync at AppStore.swift:218 reflects live state. So if the previous run ended healthy but capture permission was since revoked (or the device unplugged), the Setup tab shows green checks, a full green bar, and the 'You're all set!' card for the whole async start() window (seconds). | Fix: Do not seed onboarding route/permission flags from the persisted session. Reset routeHealthReady/audioComponentInstalled/permissionsGranted to false until the first live syncOnboarding after backend.start(), or neutralize backendStatus on load (set isRouteRecoveryHealthy/isAudioComponentInstalled/hasRequiredPermissions to false) so the checklist never shows green from cached state.
• [MEDIUM/inconsistency] Acceptance [0] cannot hold: step 1 completion is independent of managed-audio support (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1360) — Confirmed. Step 1 (outputDeviceVisible) is set purely from snapshot.currentDevice != nil (AppStore.swift:1360), which is unrelated to supportsPerAppRouting. Moreover buildSnapshot always fabricates a fallback 'System Output' device when none is readable (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1062-1070), so currentDevice is effectively never nil after a live build — meaning step 1 is essentially always green and completionProgress is at least 1/3 (~33%) even on an unsupported OS, contradicting the acceptance's '0%'. | Fix: Either correct the acceptance criterion (step 1 is intentionally orthogonal to OS support), or gate the device step's completion/credit behind audioComponentInstalled so the progress narrative matches 'no managed support => 0%'.
• [MEDIUM/ux] The 'guided setup checklist' is never surfaced to new users (Sources/Waves/Settings/SettingsView.swift:13) — Confirmed. WavesApp launches into MainWindowView, the Settings scene, and a MenuBarExtra (WavesApp.swift:30-67); there is no first-run flag and no auto-presentation of onboarding. OnboardingView is reachable only as the 'Setup' tab in SettingsView (SettingsView.swift:13). The openSettings() calls in MainWindowView/MenuBarMixerView open Settings to its default (General) tab, not Setup. Grep confirms no hasSeenOnboarding/firstLaunch logic exists. A genuinely new user is never shown the prerequisites checklist unless they happen to open Settings and click the Setup tab. | Fix: Auto-present the Setup tab (or a dedicated onboarding window/sheet) on first launch using a persisted 'hasSeenOnboarding' flag, and/or add a visible 'Setup' affordance in the main window/menu bar when isFullyComplete is false.
• [LOW/ux] Optional Accessibility step gives no progress credit, so completing it appears to do nothing (Sources/Waves/Features/Onboarding/OnboardingView.swift:36) — Confirmed. completionProgress and isFullyComplete count only the first three steps (OnboardingView.swift:30-43). Step 3 renders identically to the others — a warning triangle and a 'Needs action' label when ungranted (EnhancedSetupStepRow, OnboardingView.swift:188-191) — yet granting it never moves the bar and the row is not visually marked 'optional' beyond its body text. | Fix: Visually badge step 3 as 'Optional' (a chip/label distinct from the warning triangle) and/or show a separate 'optional extras' indicator, so users understand it is intentionally excluded from core progress.
• [LOW/dead-code] Unused @State selectedStep in OnboardingView (Sources/Waves/Features/Onboarding/OnboardingView.swift:5) — Confirmed. OnboardingView declares @State private var selectedStep = 0 (OnboardingView.swift:5) and it is never read or written anywhere. Dead state. | Fix: Delete the selectedStep property.
• [LOW/accessibility] Setup ProgressView has no accessibility label/value describing setup completion (Sources/Waves/Features/Onboarding/OnboardingView.swift:57) — Confirmed. The ProgressView (OnboardingView.swift:57-58) carries no .accessibilityLabel or .accessibilityValue, so VoiceOver announces only a generic progress indicator with no indication that this is setup progress or 'X of 3 steps complete'. (Note: the individual step icons do carry accessibilityLabel at OnboardingView.swift:191, so only the overall bar is unlabeled.) | Fix: Add .accessibilityLabel("Setup progress") and .accessibilityValue("\(completedSteps) of 3 steps complete") to the ProgressView.

Feature: Confirm managed audio support / capture readiness
User Story: As a user on a supported Mac, I want Waves to confirm that local Core Audio process-tap routing is available, so that I trust audio stays on my Mac and per-app routing will work.
Expected Behavior:
• Step 0 explains Waves uses local Core Audio process taps on macOS 14.2+ and that audio stays on-device, with a 'Refresh Status' action (OnboardingView.swift:8-12).
• Step 0 is marked complete when onboarding.audioComponentInstalled is true, which is backendStatus.isAudioComponentInstalled == supportsPerAppRouting (macOS 14.2+ capability gate) (OnboardingView.swift:141, AppStore.swift:1357, WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1087).
• The action calls store.refresh(), which re-runs the backend refresh, recomputes backend status, and re-syncs onboarding, then shows a 'Library refreshed' toast (OnboardingView.swift:159-163, AppStore.swift:228-254).
• Actual audio-capture authorization is probed separately by creating/destroying a private process tap and is mapped to onboarding.permissionsGranted (hasRequiredPermissions), surfaced in diagnostics but not as its own onboarding step (AppStore.swift:1358, WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:327-358,1088).
Acceptance Criteria:
• On macOS 14.2+ where per-app routing is supported, tapping 'Refresh Status' marks step 0 complete.
• On an unsupported OS, step 0 never completes and the action only re-refreshes without offering an alternative.
• The capture-permission (hasRequiredPermissions) result is reflected somewhere visible to the user after a refresh.
Impl Notes: Step 0 only verifies OS-version capability (supportsPerAppRouting), not whether TCC audio-capture permission is granted. permissionsGranted/hasRequiredPermissions is computed and probed but is NOT its own onboarding step and has no dedicated 'grant permission' action — the title 'Confirm managed audio support' conflates capability with permission. A user who is on a supported OS but has denied capture permission can see step 0 'complete' yet still have non-functional routing (caught only later via the route-health step). On an unsupported OS the only offered action is a refresh that can never succeed, with no guidance.
Errors Found:
• [HIGH/correctness] Step 0 'Confirm managed audio support' marks complete on OS-version capability alone, ignoring denied capture permission (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1357) — Confirmed. Step 0 completion = audioComponentInstalled = backendStatus.isAudioComponentInstalled, which buildSnapshot sets to supportsPerAppRouting (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1087), i.e. merely 'macOS 14.2+'. The real TCC capture grant is a separate probe mapped to hasRequiredPermissions/permissionsGranted (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1088, refreshCaptureAuthorization at 332-357) but has no onboarding step and no 'grant permission' action. A user on a supported OS who has denied audio capture sees step 0 green while per-app routing silently does nothing. | Fix: Add a dedicated capture-permission step driven by onboarding.permissionsGranted with an 'Allow audio recording in System Settings' action, or fold permissionsGranted into step 0 so 'Confirm managed audio support' requires both supportsPerAppRouting AND captureAuthorization == .authorized.
• [MEDIUM/edge-case] On an unsupported OS, step 0's only action is a Refresh that can never succeed and offers no guidance (Sources/Waves/Features/Onboarding/OnboardingView.swift:161) — Confirmed. On macOS < 14.2 supportsPerAppRouting is false (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:310-315), so audioComponentInstalled is permanently false and canPerformAction(0) stays true, exposing the 'Refresh Status' button (OnboardingView.swift:151). handleAction case 0 calls store.refresh() (OnboardingView.swift:161-162) which can never flip the flag. No copy explains the OS requirement. (Diagnostics text 'needs macOS 14.2 or newer' exists only in the separate Advanced tab.) | Fix: When audioComponentInstalled is false due to OS version, replace the Refresh action with an informational message ('Requires macOS 14.2 or newer') and hide the dead button.
• [LOW/dead-code] Real capture-permission state (permissionsGranted / isReadyForEverydayUse) is computed but never consumed by the onboarding UI (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1504) — New finding (missed by first tester). syncOnboarding faithfully computes onboarding.permissionsGranted from the live TCC probe (AppStore.swift:1358), and OnboardingState exposes isReadyForEverydayUse = permissionsGranted && outputDeviceVisible && routeHealthReady (AppStore.swift:1504-1506). Grep confirms permissionsGranted is read ONLY by isReadyForEverydayUse, and isReadyForEverydayUse is referenced nowhere in the codebase. The OnboardingView instead bases step 0/'fully complete' on audioComponentInstalled (OS support), so the one flag that actually tracks capture permission is dead. This is the root cause of the ONB-02 'capability vs permission' conflation: the correct signal exists but is wired to nothing. | Fix: Drive the checklist off isReadyForEverydayUse (or surface permissionsGranted as its own step), or delete the unused members. Reusing isReadyForEverydayUse would also fix the step-0 conflation in this story.

Feature: Validate output device visibility
User Story: As a user, I want Waves to confirm it can see an audio output device, so that I know managed routes can be created for my speakers or headphones.
Expected Behavior:
• Step 1 instructs the user to ensure speakers/headphones are connected and recognized, with a 'Refresh Devices' action (OnboardingView.swift:14-17).
• Step 1 completes when onboarding.outputDeviceVisible is true, set from snapshot.currentDevice != nil (OnboardingView.swift:142, AppStore.swift:1360).
• The action calls store.refresh(), re-acquiring the current device and re-syncing onboarding (OnboardingView.swift:164, AppStore.swift:228-254).
• OnboardingState.outputDeviceVisible defaults to true before any sync, so the initial pre-refresh state optimistically assumes a device is present (AppStore.swift:1500).
Acceptance Criteria:
• With a recognized output device, step 1 shows complete after the initial sync/refresh.
• With no current output device, step 1 shows the needs-action state and 'Refresh Devices' re-checks.
• Disconnecting all output devices and refreshing flips step 1 back to needs-action.
Impl Notes: Straightforward and correct. Minor UX concern: outputDeviceVisible defaults to true (AppStore.swift:1500), so on the very first render before syncOnboarding completes the step can briefly appear satisfied; in practice the initializer calls syncOnboarding immediately (AppStore.swift:100) so the window is tiny.
Errors Found: • [LOW/edge-case] outputDeviceVisible defaults to true, so step 1 can momentarily show 'complete' before the first sync (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1500) — Confirmed but narrow. OnboardingState.outputDeviceVisible defaults to true (AppStore.swift:1500). In the init path, OnboardingState is constructed at AppStore.swift:96-98 and then syncOnboarding(using:) runs synchronously at AppStore.swift:100, so the optimistic default is overwritten before any view renders — the real exposure window is essentially zero in the normal flow. The semantic concern (asserting a device exists before anything checked) stands, but practically the default is masked immediately. Low severity is correct. | Fix: Default outputDeviceVisible to false to match the other flags' semantics, so the step starts in needs-action until syncOnboarding confirms a device.

Feature: Test device switching / route recovery
User Story: As a user who switches between speakers and headphones, I want to verify Waves automatically re-establishes managed routes after a device change, so that I trust audio keeps working without restarting the app.
Expected Behavior:
• Step 2 explains routes are re-established automatically on device switches and offers a 'Test Recovery' action (OnboardingView.swift:18-22).
• Step 2 completes when onboarding.routeHealthReady is true, from backendStatus.isRouteRecoveryHealthy (supportsPerAppRouting && capture authorized && no route errors) (OnboardingView.swift:143, AppStore.swift:1361, WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1089).
• The action is only enabled when a device is visible but route health is not yet ready (canPerformAction case 2), preventing a recovery attempt with no device (OnboardingView.swift:153).
• 'Test Recovery' calls store.recoverRoutes(), which reattaches managed routing paths, re-syncs onboarding, and shows a 'Routes recovered' success toast; on failure it reloads the current snapshot and shows a 'Recovery failed' error toast (OnboardingView.swift:165-166, AppStore.swift:1181-1202).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Step 2's action button is disabled/hidden until step 1 (device visible) is satisfied.
• Tapping 'Test Recovery' on a healthy system marks step 2 complete and shows a success toast.
• If recovery throws, step 2 stays incomplete and an error toast with the failure detail is shown.
• After a real output-device change, route health re-syncs and step 2 reflects the new state.
Impl Notes: Well-gated (action only enabled once a device is visible) and has explicit success/failure toasts. Because routeHealthReady also requires capture authorization, a permission-denied user will see this step stuck on 'needs action' even though the failure root-cause (no capture permission) is never named in the onboarding UI — the user is told to 'Test Recovery' rather than to grant permission.
Errors Found:
• [HIGH/error-handling] 'Test Recovery' shows a green 'Routes recovered' success toast even when route health is still not ready (e.g. capture permission denied) (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1189) — Confirmed. backend.recoverRoutes() optimistically sets isRouteRecoveryHealthy = true (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:212) but then rebuilds the snapshot via buildSnapshot (line 214), which recomputes isRouteRecoveryHealthy = supportsPerAppRouting && captureAuthorization == .authorized && !hasRouteErrors (line 1089). If capture is not authorized the returned snapshot has routeHealthReady = false, and recoverRoutes does NOT throw. Back in AppStore.recoverRoutes the no-throw path runs syncOnboarding (which sets routeHealthReady=false, AppStore.swift:1188) and then unconditionally shows the 'Routes recovered' success toast (AppStore.swift:1189-1193). Net: green success toast while step 2 stays on 'needs action' with the button still present — contradicts acceptance [1] and defeats acceptance [2] (the common failure never throws). | Fix: After recoverRoutes() returns, branch on the resulting onboarding.routeHealthReady (or returned backendStatus): show success only if route health is now ready; otherwise show a warning/error toast naming the unmet prerequisite (capture permission denied / no device) and pointing the user to grant capture permission.
• [MEDIUM/edge-case] Device-change route-health re-sync is skipped entirely when 'auto-restore device' is disabled (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:793) — Confirmed. handleDeviceChange() is the sole device-change handler (observeDeviceChanges wires only it, AppStore.swift:781-789) and it early-returns at AppStore.swift:793 when preferences.autoRestoreDevice is false, before reaching syncOnboarding at AppStore.swift:809. autoRestoreDevice is a user toggle (default true, toggled at SettingsView.swift:78-86). With it off, a real output-device change does not re-sync onboarding, so step 2/step 1 go stale until a manual Refresh — contradicting acceptance [3] and the step's own 'no restart needed' copy. | Fix: Always re-read the snapshot and re-sync onboarding/diagnostics on a device-change event; gate only the route re-establishment behavior on autoRestoreDevice.
• [MEDIUM/ux] Permission-denied user is told to 'Test Recovery' instead of to grant capture permission (Sources/Waves/Features/Onboarding/OnboardingView.swift:166) — Confirmed. routeHealthReady requires captureAuthorization == .authorized (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1089). When capture is denied, step 2 sits on 'needs action' offering 'Test Recovery' (canPerformAction case 2 / handleAction case 2 -> recoverRoutes, OnboardingView.swift:153,165-166), but recovery cannot create a TCC grant, so the user loops on a button that addresses the wrong root cause. The onboarding UI never names 'audio capture permission' (only the Advanced/diagnostics tab does). | Fix: When routeHealthReady is false specifically because captureAuthorization != .authorized, change the step's action/detail to 'Allow audio recording in System Settings' (open the Microphone privacy pane) instead of 'Test Recovery'.

Feature: Enable Accessibility permission for global shortcuts
User Story: As a user who wants global keyboard shortcuts, I want a step that helps me grant Accessibility permission, so that Waves can control volume system-wide; and I want it clearly marked optional since routing works without it.
Expected Behavior:
• Step 3 states Accessibility is optional and that per-app routing works without it, with an 'Open System Settings' action (OnboardingView.swift:24-27).
• Step 3 completion reflects onboarding.accessibilityPermissionGranted, set from AXIsProcessTrusted() during syncOnboarding (OnboardingView.swift:144, AppStore.swift:1359).
• The action opens the Accessibility pane via the x-apple.systempreferences Privacy_Accessibility URL using NSWorkspace (OnboardingView.swift:167-170).
• Step 3 is deliberately excluded from completionProgress and isFullyComplete, so it does not block the 'You're all set!' state (OnboardingView.swift:30-43).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Tapping 'Open System Settings' opens the macOS Accessibility privacy pane.
• After granting Accessibility and triggering a refresh, step 3 shows complete via AXIsProcessTrusted().
• Leaving Accessibility ungranted does not prevent the checklist from reaching the fully-complete state.
Impl Notes: Opens the right settings pane but does no live re-check: AXIsProcessTrusted() is only re-evaluated on the next syncOnboarding (refresh/recover/device-change), so after the user grants permission in System Settings the step keeps showing 'needs action' until they manually hit a Refresh button — there is no AXIsProcessTrustedWithOptions prompt or polling. The step's value also depends on enableKeyboardShortcuts being on (default true), which is configured elsewhere in General settings, not surfaced here.
Errors Found:
• [LOW/ux] Step 3 does not live re-check Accessibility; it stays 'needs action' until a manual refresh (Sources/Waves/Features/Onboarding/OnboardingView.swift:167) — Confirmed. handleAction case 3 only opens the Accessibility pane via NSWorkspace (OnboardingView.swift:167-170) with no prompt or polling. accessibilityPermissionGranted is re-evaluated via AXIsProcessTrusted() only inside syncOnboarding (AppStore.swift:1359), which runs on refresh/recover/device-change. After granting Accessibility and returning to Waves, the step keeps showing 'needs action' until a manual Refresh. Acceptance [1] only requires correctness after a refresh, so this is a UX shortfall rather than an acceptance failure. | Fix: Re-run syncOnboarding when the app/window becomes active (didBecomeActiveNotification / scenePhase active), or use AXIsProcessTrustedWithOptions(prompt:true) so the grant is reflected promptly.
• [LOW/inconsistency] Step 3 ignores the enableKeyboardShortcuts preference it implicitly depends on (Sources/Waves/Features/Onboarding/OnboardingView.swift:144) — Confirmed. The step's value is enabling global shortcuts, which also require preferences.enableKeyboardShortcuts (default true; gated in increase/decrease/toggle handlers, e.g. AppStore.swift:1211,1230,1249). The step checks only AXIsProcessTrusted() (isStepComplete case 3 -> accessibilityPermissionGranted, OnboardingView.swift:144) and neither checks nor surfaces the toggle. A user who disabled keyboard shortcuts can grant Accessibility, see the step 'complete', yet shortcuts still won't fire, with no hint that the General-settings toggle governs the feature. | Fix: Mention (or surface a toggle for) enableKeyboardShortcuts within this step, or note that shortcuts must also be enabled in General settings for the grant to take effect.

Feature: Quick actions: global refresh and enable launch-at-login from setup
User Story: As a user finishing setup, I want quick buttons to re-check status and to enable launch-at-login, so that I can finalize my configuration without leaving the setup screen.
Expected Behavior:
• A prominent 'Refresh Status' button calls store.refresh() and store.refreshDiagnostics(), updating both the session-derived onboarding flags and the diagnostics report (OnboardingView.swift:121-126, AppStore.swift:228-254,1204-1208).
• An 'Enable Launch at Login' button is shown only while onboarding.launchAtLoginEnabled is false; tapping it sets store.launchAtLoginEnabled = true (OnboardingView.swift:128-133).
• Setting launchAtLoginEnabled registers the login item via LoginItemService, persists preferences, and re-syncs onboarding.launchAtLoginEnabled; if the OS reports the item still disabled (needs approval) a 'Login item needs approval' warning toast is shown, and registration errors show a failure toast (AppStore.swift:161-186).
• launchAtLoginEnabled state is also re-synced from loginItemService.status on every syncOnboarding so the button visibility tracks the real system state (AppStore.swift:1362-1364).
Acceptance Criteria:
• 'Refresh Status' re-runs backend refresh and diagnostics and updates all step states.
• The 'Enable Launch at Login' button disappears once launch-at-login is actually enabled.
• If enabling launch-at-login requires user approval, a warning toast appears and the button remains until approval is granted.
• A registration error surfaces an error toast and does not crash.
Impl Notes: Both quick actions are fully wired with proper toasts and state re-sync. Note the top 'Refresh Status' button duplicates step 0's 'Refresh Status' action label, which may confuse users about scope. There is no explicit empty/error state in onboarding for a backend that fails to start — failures surface only as transient toasts, and the checklist simply shows steps as incomplete.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/error-handling] No onboarding state for a backend that fails to start; failures appear only as a transient toast (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:221) — Confirmed, and raised from 'likely' to confirmed / low->medium. If backend.start() throws, the catch resets isBootstrapped=false and shows only a transient 'Startup failed' toast (AppStore.swift:221-224); syncOnboarding is never reached on that path, so onboarding flags keep whatever stale/seeded values they had and the Setup tab simply renders steps incomplete with no failure context. Worse, because isBootstrapped was reset to false, the Setup tab's bottom 'Refresh Status' -> store.refresh() is gated by isRefreshing/started-backend assumptions and there is no visible retry of start(); the persistent lastError is only shown in the separate Advanced/diagnostics tab (SettingsView.swift:324). The user has no in-Setup indication that the backend never started, nor a clear recovery path. | Fix: Surface session.backendStatus.lastError (or a dedicated 'backend failed to start' banner) within OnboardingView when start() failed, with an explicit retry affordance that re-invokes start().
• [LOW/ux] Two different 'Refresh Status' buttons with the same label but different scope (Sources/Waves/Features/Onboarding/OnboardingView.swift:122) — Confirmed. The bottom quick-action 'Refresh Status' calls both store.refresh() and store.refreshDiagnostics() (OnboardingView.swift:122-125), while step 0's needs-action button is also labeled 'Refresh Status' (steps array, OnboardingView.swift:11) but handleAction case 0 calls only store.refresh() (OnboardingView.swift:161-162). Identical labels for actions of different scope can confuse users. | Fix: Rename the bottom quick action (e.g. 'Re-check Everything' / 'Refresh All'), or have step 0 also call refreshDiagnostics() so the two truly match.

Feature: Help reference accessible from Settings
User Story: As a new Waves user, I want an in-app help reference reachable from the app's settings, so that I can learn how to use the mixer without leaving the app or finding external docs.
Expected Behavior:
• A 'Help' tab appears in the Settings window TabView with a question-mark-circle icon (SettingsView.swift:33-36).
• Selecting the tab renders HelpView, a vertically scrolling document with six sections: header, Quick Start, Keyboard Shortcuts, URL Scheme Automation, Presets, Troubleshooting (HelpView.swift:8-18).
• Content is laid out in a ScrollView so long help text remains reachable on small windows (HelpView.swift:8, 19).
• The header shows the title 'Waves Help' and a subtitle 'Your comprehensive guide to Waves audio control' (HelpView.swift:24-28).
• Help is only reachable through the Settings scene; there is no standalone Help window, sheet, or macOS Help-menu entry wired (only reference is SettingsView.swift:33).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Opening Settings (Cmd+,) and clicking the Help tab shows the help document.
• All six sections render and the view scrolls when content exceeds the window height.
• No crash or blank tab when the Help tab is selected with no audio apps running (content is fully static).
• There is no other entry point to Help besides the Settings tab.
Impl Notes: Wired and renders correctly as a Settings tab. Two concerns: (1) HelpView declares @Environment(\.dismiss) at HelpView.swift:5 but never uses it — dead code, and there is no dismiss/close affordance because the view lives inside a tab. (2) Help is buried as the last Settings tab with no Help-menu item or toolbar shortcut, so discoverability is low for a 'comprehensive guide'. README.md:264 directs users to 'the in-app diagnostics panel or check the troubleshooting section', implying Help is meant to be a primary support surface, which the current placement undercuts.
Errors Found:
• [LOW/dead-code] Unused @Environment(\.dismiss) in HelpView (dead code, no close affordance) (Sources/Waves/Features/Help/HelpView.swift:5) — HelpView declares `@Environment(\.dismiss) private var dismiss` at line 5 but never references `dismiss` anywhere in the file (verified by reading all 250 lines). HelpView is embedded as a Settings TabView tab (SettingsView.swift:33-36); there is no dismiss/close button, so the environment value is meaningless here and the declaration is dead. | Fix: Delete line 5 (`@Environment(\.dismiss) private var dismiss`). The Help tab is dismissed by closing the Settings window, so no dismiss handling is needed.
• [LOW/ux] Help is buried as the last Settings tab with no Help-menu / toolbar entry point (Sources/Waves/App/WavesApp.swift:43-48) — The only path to Help is Settings (⌘,) → last of six tabs (SettingsView.swift:33-36). WavesApp.swift only registers a CommandGroup(after: .toolbar) for 'Refresh' (lines 43-48); the default .help command group is never replaced, so macOS shows only an auto-generated empty Help menu (search field) with no 'Waves Help' entry. There is no toolbar '?' button and no standalone Help window/sheet. For a surface titled 'Your comprehensive guide to Waves audio control', discoverability is low. (Note: README.md:264 points users to the in-app diagnostics panel / its own troubleshooting section, not explicitly to in-app Help, so the README dependency the first tester implied is weaker than stated — but the discoverability gap itself is real.) | Fix: Add a standard Help menu command (CommandGroup(replacing: .help)) that calls openSettings() and selects the Help tab, or add a '?' toolbar item in MainWindowView that opens Settings to Help.

Feature: Quick Start onboarding steps
User Story: As a first-time user, I want a short numbered quick-start walkthrough, so that I immediately understand the core actions (detect apps, adjust volume, mute, pin).
Expected Behavior:
• Quick Start renders four numbered steps in a material-backed card (HelpView.swift:32-62).
• Step 1 'Launch Waves' states apps are auto-detected; Step 2 'Adjust Volumes' describes per-app sliders; Step 3 'Mute Apps' describes the speaker icon; Step 4 'Pin Apps' says to right-click apps to pin (HelpView.swift:38-57).
• Each step shows an accent-colored number badge in a circle next to a bold title and secondary description (helpStep, HelpView.swift:191-207).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Four steps appear in order with badges 1–4.
• Step 4's right-click-to-pin instruction matches a real context-menu Pin action (MixerRowView.swift:135-137).
• Step 3's claim that the speaker icon mutes/unmutes matches the mute control in the mixer row.
• Descriptions reflect actual app behavior, not aspirational features.
Impl Notes: Accurate. All four steps map to real features (auto-detection, sliders, mute toggle, right-click Pin via contextMenu at MixerRowView.swift:135-137 / togglePinned at AppStore.swift:590). Content is fully static text — correct for a help doc, no functional risk. Minor: it omits that adjusting volume/mute on an unmanaged app first enrolls it in managed routing (see MixerRowView.swift:211-221 tooltips), which can surprise users; Quick Start presents control as instantaneous.

Feature: Keyboard shortcuts reference
User Story: As a power user, I want a list of Waves keyboard shortcuts, so that I can control volume, mute, refresh, and save presets without the mouse.
Expected Behavior:
• Lists six shortcuts in a monospaced card: Increase volume Cmd+Opt+Up, Decrease volume Cmd+Opt+Down, Toggle mute Cmd+Opt+M, Refresh app list Cmd+R, Save preset Cmd+S, Open Settings Cmd+, (HelpView.swift:64-95).
• Shows a caption: 'Enable keyboard shortcuts in General Settings to use these globally.' (HelpView.swift:97-99).
• Each row renders the action label (secondary) and the key glyphs (medium weight) via shortcutRow (HelpView.swift:209-217).
Acceptance Criteria:
• The three global volume/mute shortcuts (Cmd+Opt+Up/Down/M) match the real global hotkey handler keyCodes 126/125/46 (WavesApp.swift:179-188).
• Cmd+R maps to a real Refresh command (WavesApp.swift:44-47, MainWindowView.swift:46) and Cmd+S to the real Save-preset toolbar button (MainWindowView.swift:36).
• Cmd+, opens Settings via the standard Settings scene.
• The 'enable globally' caption is true only for the volume/mute shortcuts, not for the always-on app shortcuts.
Impl Notes: Shortcuts are real but the section mixes two categories and the caption is misleading. Cmd+Opt+Up/Down/M are global hotkeys that only fire when preferences.enableKeyboardShortcuts is on (WavesApp.swift:105-112, 164-188). Cmd+R, Cmd+S, and Cmd+, are standard SwiftUI command/toolbar/Settings shortcuts that work regardless of that toggle (WavesApp.swift:44-47; MainWindowView.swift:36,46). The single caption 'Enable keyboard shortcuts in General Settings to use these globally' implies the whole list is gated, which is incorrect for ⌘R/⌘S/⌘,. Also, the listed step is 'increase/decrease volume' but the global handler uses a 0.1 step (WavesApp.swift:181-183 -> increaseVolumeForFrontmostApp default 0.1 at AppStore.swift:1210) while in-row accessibility uses 0.05 (MixerRowView.swift:224) — not documented, minor.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/correctness] Misleading caption: 'Enable keyboard shortcuts to use these globally' applies to only 3 of the 6 listed shortcuts (Sources/Waves/Features/Help/HelpView.swift:97-99) — The Keyboard Shortcuts card lists six shortcuts (⌘⌥↑, ⌘⌥↓, ⌘⌥M, ⌘R, ⌘S, ⌘,) then shows one blanket caption 'Enable keyboard shortcuts in General Settings to use these globally.' (HelpView.swift:97). Verified in code: only the three ⌘⌥ shortcuts are global hotkeys gated by preferences.enableKeyboardShortcuts (AppStore.swift:1211/1230/1249 each `guard preferences.enableKeyboardShortcuts else { return }`; installed via NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents in WavesApp.swift:105-112,150-189). ⌘R is a SwiftUI command (WavesApp.swift:43-48), ⌘S is the save-preset toolbar shortcut (MainWindowView.swift:36), and ⌘, is the standard Settings shortcut — all three work regardless of the toggle and are app-local (only while a Waves window is key), not system-wide. So the caption is doubly wrong for ⌘R/⌘S/⌘,: it implies they are gated (they are not) and global (they are not). | Fix: Split into two groups — 'Global shortcuts (require Enable keyboard shortcuts)': ⌘⌥↑/↓/M; and 'App shortcuts (always available in Waves)': ⌘R, ⌘S, ⌘, — and scope the 'enable globally' caption to the first group only.
• [LOW/inconsistency] Volume step differs between global shortcut (0.1) and in-row/keyboard nudge (0.05), undocumented (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1210) — The global ⌘⌥↑/↓ shortcuts step volume by a default 0.1 (AppStore.swift:1210 increaseVolumeForFrontmostApp(step: Float = 0.1); 1229 decrease), while the in-row accessibility adjustable action steps by 0.05 (MixerRowView.swift:224) and the list key handlers '='/'-' nudge by 0.05 (MainWindowView.swift:383-384). Help documents only 'Increase/Decrease volume' with no step, so this is not a stated falsehood, but the two volume-adjust paths use inconsistent increments and Help never notes it. Tangential to the Help area (it is really an app-level inconsistency), kept as low. | Fix: Pick one canonical step (e.g. 0.05) for both the global default and in-row nudge, or note the 10%/5% difference in Help. Low priority.

Feature: URL scheme automation reference
User Story: As an automation user, I want documentation of the waves:// URL scheme, so that I can script volume, mute, preset, and refresh commands from Shortcuts or scripts.
Expected Behavior:
• Documents four commands: waves://set-volume?app=APP_ID&volume=0.5 (0.0-1.0), waves://mute?app=APP_ID&muted=true, waves://apply-preset?name=Focus, waves://refresh (HelpView.swift:105-131).
• Each row shows the scheme (accent monospaced), optional params, and a plain-language description via urlSchemeRow (HelpView.swift:219-237).
• Shows a caption telling the user to 'Enable URL scheme automation in General Settings before using these commands.' (HelpView.swift:133-135).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Each documented host matches a real branch in the URL handler: set-volume, mute, apply-preset, refresh (AppStore.swift:277-288).
• Documented params match what the handlers parse: app+volume (AppStore.swift:291-310), app+muted (AppStore.swift:312-328), name (AppStore.swift:330-343).
• The volume range 0.0-1.0 documented matches the handler's validation volume>=0 && volume<=1 (AppStore.swift:296-298).
• The 'enable in General Settings' caption matches the real gate preferences.enableURLScheme (AppStore.swift:257-260).
Impl Notes: Documentation is accurate and complete: all four hosts, their query params, the 0.0-1.0 range, and the enable-in-settings prerequisite all match the implementation (AppStore.swift:256-343). Not documented (acceptable for brevity but worth noting): commands are rate-limited (checkURLSchemeRateLimit, AppStore.swift:345-354) and silently/with-toast rejected when the app/preset isn't found, and 'APP_ID' format (logicalID/appKey matching, AppStore.swift:303) is not explained, so users may guess the wrong identifier.

Feature: Presets usage reference
User Story: As a user managing multiple audio setups, I want help on saving, applying, exporting, and importing presets, so that I can reuse volume/mute configurations.
Expected Behavior:
• Bulleted list explains: click the + toolbar button to save, name it to capture current config, click presets in the sidebar to apply, export to share, import from JSON (HelpView.swift:141-152).
• Caption clarifies presets remember volume and mute states for all running apps (HelpView.swift:155-157).
Acceptance Criteria:
• The '+' toolbar save flow matches the real Save-preset toolbar button (MainWindowView.swift:28-36).
• Export and Import bullets match real store actions exportPreset/importPreset (SettingsView.swift:221-247; AppStore.swift:1079, 1112).
• The import description ('from JSON files') matches the importer behavior.
• The caption's claim about captured state matches what a preset actually stores.
Impl Notes: Claims map to real features: save via + toolbar button, export/import via the Presets settings tab (SettingsView.swift:221-247 -> AppStore.swift:1079/1112). One UX mismatch: the bullet says 'Click on presets in the sidebar to apply them', but preset apply/export controls in code live in the Presets settings tab list (SettingsView.swift:221-247), not necessarily the main-window sidebar — the wording may point users to the wrong place. Import also enforces limits not mentioned here (10MB, max 1000 entries, non-empty name; AppStore.swift:1131-1146), so a rejected import could confuse a user following only this help.

Feature: Troubleshooting guidance
User Story: As a user hitting problems, I want troubleshooting tips for common failures, so that I can self-resolve detection, routing, shortcut, and device issues without contacting support.
Expected Behavior:
• Lists four issue/solution pairs: no apps detected (play sound, enable 'Show system processes', refresh ⌘R), volume not applying (check Diagnostics in Advanced, 'Recover routes now'), shortcuts not working (enable in Settings, check accessibility permissions, conflicts), device switching (enable 'Auto-restore device', recover routes, check connection) (HelpView.swift:163-189).
• Each item renders a bold issue title and a secondary solution line via troubleshootingItem (HelpView.swift:239-248).
Acceptance Criteria:
• 'Show system processes' refers to a real toggle (SettingsView.swift:69).
• 'Auto-restore device' refers to a real toggle (SettingsView.swift:79).
• The recover-routes remedy maps to a real action store.recoverRoutes (SettingsView.swift:199, 336).
• 'Diagnostics in Advanced settings' matches the real Advanced/Diagnostics tab (SettingsView.swift:28-31, 269).
Impl Notes: Guidance is mostly accurate and points at real toggles/actions, but two label mismatches reduce trust: the help says 'Recover routes now' while the actual buttons are labeled 'Recover Routes' (SettingsView.swift:198, 338) and the toolbar tooltip is 'Recover managed routes' (MainWindowView.swift:53) — no control is literally named 'Recover routes now'. The shortcuts remedy says 'check accessibility permissions', but the global hotkey monitor uses NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents (WavesApp.swift:152) which requires Input Monitoring/Accessibility approval that the app never prompts for or surfaces — so this tip may not be actionable for the user. Otherwise 'Show system processes' and 'Auto-restore device' toggles and the Advanced/Diagnostics tab all exist.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/correctness] Troubleshooting points to 'Recover routes now' on the Advanced/Diagnostics tab, where that button is normally absent (Sources/Waves/Features/Help/HelpView.swift:175) — Help (HelpView.swift:175) tells users with volume issues to 'Check Diagnostics in Advanced settings, try \'Recover routes now\''. The Advanced tab is DiagnosticsSettingsView (SettingsView.swift:28-31,269). Its .onAppear calls store.refreshDiagnostics() (SettingsView.swift:310-312) which populates store.diagnostics; when diagnostics is non-nil the tab renders only the checks plus a 'Copy Diagnostics' button (SettingsView.swift:283-303) and NO recover button. The 'Recover Routes' button on that tab lives exclusively inside DiagnosticsUnavailableView (SettingsView.swift:335-340), shown only in the `else` branch when store.diagnostics == nil. So once diagnostics load (the normal steady state) a user following this tip finds no recover control on the Advanced tab. The reliably-present 'Recover Routes' button is on the Audio tab (SettingsView.swift:198-200) and the main-window toolbar (MainWindowView.swift:48-55), neither of which the help mentions. | Fix: Either add a persistent 'Recover Routes' button to DiagnosticsSettingsView (not just the unavailable state), or change the help text to point at the Audio tab / main-window toolbar Recover control.
• [LOW/inconsistency] Help label 'Recover routes now' matches no real control name (Sources/Waves/Features/Help/HelpView.swift:175) — Help says 'Recover routes now' (HelpView.swift:175) but the actual controls are labeled 'Recover Routes' (SettingsView.swift:198, 338) and the toolbar tooltip/accessibility label is 'Recover managed routes' (MainWindowView.swift:53-54). No control is literally named 'Recover routes now', so a user scanning the UI for that exact label will not find it. | Fix: Quote the real label: change 'Recover routes now' to 'Recover Routes' to match SettingsView.swift:198/338.

Feature: Route health report (diagnostics checks)
User Story: As a Waves user whose per-app volume controls aren't behaving, I want a structured route-health report listing each system prerequisite and its pass/warn/fail status, so that I can see exactly which capability (OS support, capture permission, accessibility, route recovery, app coverage) is blocking control.
Expected Behavior:
• Backend's diagnosticsReport() builds a DiagnosticsReport with a summary plus five checks: Audio component, Audio capture permission, Accessibility permission, Route recovery, and Support matrix (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:241-278)
• 'Audio component' is .passed when isAudioComponentInstalled, else .warning with detail 'Per-app routing needs macOS 14.2 or newer.' (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:245-251)
• 'Audio capture permission' maps captureAuthorization: .authorized->passed, .notGranted->failed, .undetermined/.unsupported->warning, each with a tailored remediation detail pointing to System Settings (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:252-256, 280-299)
• 'Accessibility permission' is .passed when AXIsProcessTrusted(), else .warning noting global shortcuts need it but routing still works (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:257-263, 318-320)
• 'Route recovery' is .passed when isRouteRecoveryHealthy else .warning advising 'Recover routes and retry.' (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:264-270)
• 'Support matrix' is always .informational showing coverageSummary 'N/M validated' (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:271-275, SupportMatrix.swift:10-13)
• summary (recoverabilitySummary) reports count of currently managed routes, or that routing is unavailable on the OS version (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:301-308)
• Each check renders in Settings as title + detail cards (SettingsView.swift:291-303) and in the mixer DiagnosticsPanel with a colored status dot and VoiceOver status label (MainWindowView.swift:531-589)
Acceptance Criteria:
• When capture permission is denied, the 'Audio capture permission' check shows status failed and a red dot, with detail directing the user to System Settings > Privacy & Security > Microphone
• On macOS earlier than 14.2 the 'Audio component' check shows a warning referencing the 14.2 requirement
• The Support matrix check always shows as informational (gray dot), never pass/fail, and its detail equals 'supportedCount/totalCount validated'
• The mixer DiagnosticsPanel summary string reflects the number of currently-managed apps
• Each check's status color in the panel maps passed=green, warning=orange, failed=red, informational=secondary
Impl Notes: Fully wired and rendered in two places. Concern: the DiagnosticsPanel in the mixer shows check titles and details with status dots but never surfaces the status label visually for sighted users beyond the dot color (status word is VoiceOver-only), so a color-blind sighted user can't distinguish warning vs failed without the announced label. Minor: 'Route recovery' uses .warning (orange) even when routing is fully broken, never .failed, so a genuinely failed route looks the same severity as a non-blocking accessibility warning.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/inconsistency] Route recovery check can never report .failed, so a fully broken route looks like a benign warning (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:264-270) — Re-read WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:264-270: the 'Route recovery' check status is a binary ternary (snapshot.backendStatus.isRouteRecoveryHealthy ? .passed : .warning). isRouteRecoveryHealthy is false whenever per-app routing is genuinely broken — capture not granted, OS unsupported, or any app in .error routingState (hasRouteErrors at :1061 / isRouteRecoveryHealthy at :1089). In all of those cases the check is rendered as orange .warning, the same severity as the explicitly non-blocking Accessibility warning. No code path yields .failed for route recovery even though DiagnosticsStatus.failed exists (AudioControlBackend.swift:88) and both panels map it to red. Confirmed. | Fix: Distinguish hard failures from soft ones: when supportsPerAppRouting && captureAuthorization == .authorized but hasRouteErrors, set Route recovery to .failed (red); reserve .warning for transient/recoverable states. Thread hasRouteErrors / captureAuthorization into the check status.
• [MEDIUM/accessibility] Mixer DiagnosticsPanel encodes check status only as a colored dot for sighted users (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MainWindowView.swift:537-554) — Re-read MainWindowView.swift:537-554: each check renders check.title and check.detail visually; status is conveyed solely by the fill color of a 7pt Circle (color(for:) at 565-576). That circle is accessibilityHidden(true) (line 543) and the status word ('Passed'/'Warning'/'Failed'/'Info') is placed only in the VoiceOver accessibilityLabel (line 547). A sighted user with a color-vision deficiency cannot distinguish passed (green) from warning (orange) from failed (red) — a 7pt dot's hue is the only cue and no text/shape status is shown. WCAG 'use of color' gap. Confirmed. | Fix: Render the statusLabel text or a shape-differentiated SF Symbol (checkmark.circle / exclamationmark.triangle / xmark.octagon / info.circle) next to the dot so status is distinguishable without relying on hue alone.
• [MEDIUM/accessibility] Settings Diagnostics check cards show NO status indicator at all — not even a colored dot or VoiceOver label (Sources/Waves/Settings/SettingsView.swift:291-303) — Missed by the first tester. Re-read SettingsView.swift:291-303: in DiagnosticsSettingsView each check renders only Text(check.title) and Text(check.detail) inside a material card. Unlike the mixer DiagnosticsPanel, there is no colored dot, no status text, and no accessibility status label whatsoever. check.status is completely dropped on this surface. So in the primary Settings › Diagnostics tab a passing check is visually and to VoiceOver indistinguishable from a failed one — a worse accessibility/scannability gap than the mixer panel, and the surface where users actually go to read diagnostics. State-desync corollary: the two diagnostics surfaces (mixer panel vs Settings tab) present the same checks with different fidelity. | Fix: Mirror the mixer panel's status dot + statusLabel accessibility label (ideally with a shape-differentiated SF Symbol) in the Settings check cards, or factor a shared CheckRow view used by both surfaces.

Feature: Copy plain-text diagnostics to clipboard for bug reports
User Story: As a user filing a bug report, I want to copy a plain-text route-health snapshot to my clipboard with one click, so that I can paste system, device, permission, per-app, and check state into an issue without manually transcribing it.
Expected Behavior:
• copyDiagnosticsToPasteboard() clears NSPasteboard.general and writes diagnosticsExportText as a string, then shows a success toast 'Diagnostics copied / Paste into a bug report.' for 1.4s (AppStore.swift:774-779)
• diagnosticsExportText composes: header 'Waves diagnostics', macOS version string, output device name, audio component installed bool, capture permission granted/not granted, route recovery healthy bool, and lastError if present (AppStore.swift:725-736)
• It then lists every visible app as 'name — routingState, NN%' with optional ', muted' and ', boost Nx' suffixes (AppStore.swift:737-742)
• If a diagnostics report is loaded, it appends a 'Checks:' section with '[status] title: detail' per check (AppStore.swift:743-749)
• The Settings Diagnostics tab exposes a 'Copy Diagnostics' button with a clipboard icon and help text 'Copy a plain-text route-health report to the clipboard.' (SettingsView.swift:283-288)
• The doc comment asserts the text contains no audio and no sensitive content beyond already-visible app names (AppStore.swift:722-724)
Acceptance Criteria:
• Clicking 'Copy Diagnostics' places a multi-line plain-text report on the system clipboard and shows the success toast
• The report's first line is 'Waves diagnostics' and includes lines for macOS, Output device, Audio component installed, Audio capture permission, and Route recovery healthy
• A muted app appears in the Apps list with ', muted'; an app with boost > 1 appears with ', boost Nx'
• When status.lastError is nil, no 'Last error:' line is emitted; when present it is included
• If store.diagnostics is nil, the 'Checks:' section is omitted entirely but the rest of the report still copies
Impl Notes: Works and is privacy-conscious (clipboard-only, no file write, no PIDs/audio). Note this is 'export' only in the copy-to-clipboard sense — despite README phrasing 'diagnostics export', there is no save-to-file path for diagnostics (the NSSavePanel/.json code at AppStore.swift:1086+ is for preset export, not diagnostics). UX gap: the 'Copy Diagnostics' button is always enabled even before a report loads, so an early click copies a report with no 'Checks:' section. Boost is printed as Int(volumeBoost) which truncates fractional boosts (e.g. 2.5x shows as '2x'). No automated test covers diagnosticsExportText formatting.
Errors Found:
• [LOW/correctness] Boost in diagnostics export is truncated by Int(), dropping fractional boosts (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:740) — Re-read AppStore.swift:740: ', boost \(Int(app.volumeBoost))x'. volumeBoost is a Float clamped to [0.0, 10.0] in AudioApp.init (AudioApp.swift:88) and decoded from persistence (AudioApp.swift:138) / imported presets (Preset.swift:41 clamps to [1.0,4.0] but allows fractions). The mixer UI only offers integers and setVolumeBoost clamps to [1.0,4.0] (AppStore.swift:548), but a persisted or imported preset can carry a fractional value (e.g. 2.5). Int(2.5) prints '2x', misreporting the actual boost in a bug-report snapshot. Confirmed; correctly low severity (narrow input path). | Fix: Use String(format: "%g", app.volumeBoost) so 2.0 shows '2x' and 2.5 shows '2.5x'.
• [LOW/ux] Copy Diagnostics button is always enabled, so an early click copies a report with no Checks section (Sources/Waves/Settings/SettingsView.swift:283-288) — Re-read SettingsView.swift:283-289 (the Button has no .disabled) and AppStore.swift:743-749 (the 'Checks:' section is only appended when store.diagnostics != nil). DiagnosticsSettingsView triggers refreshDiagnostics() in onAppear (SettingsView.swift:310-312) but that is async; a click in the brief window before the first report resolves yields a report missing the Checks section, while copyDiagnosticsToPasteboard still shows the 'Diagnostics copied' success toast (AppStore.swift:774-779). Silent partial export. Confirmed. | Fix: Disable Copy Diagnostics while store.diagnostics == nil, or have copyDiagnosticsToPasteboard await a fresh diagnosticsReport() before composing the text so Checks is always present.
• [LOW/inconsistency] Export header capture-permission line can contradict the appended Checks section (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:733) — Re-read AppStore.swift:733: header line 'Audio capture permission: granted/not granted' derives from status.hasRequiredPermissions, which is captureAuthorization == .authorized (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1088). On .undetermined or .unsupported this collapses to 'not granted'. The same export's Checks section prints the 'Audio capture permission' check as [warning] with detail 'status is not yet known' (.undetermined) or 'needs macOS 14.2 or newer' (.unsupported) (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:284-298). So the header says flatly 'not granted' while the check says 'not yet known' / unsupported — confusing in a bug report. Confirmed. | Fix: Report capture permission in the header using the same vocabulary as the check (granted / not granted / not yet determined / unsupported) rather than a boolean.

Feature: Diagnostics empty/unavailable state with refresh and recover actions
User Story: As a user who opens the Diagnostics settings before a report has been generated (or after a backend error), I want a clear unavailable state that explains what's wrong and lets me retry, so that I'm not stuck with a blank panel.
Expected Behavior:
• DiagnosticsSettingsView calls store.refreshDiagnostics() in .onAppear so the report loads when the tab is shown (SettingsView.swift:310-312, AppStore.swift:1204-1208)
• When store.diagnostics is nil, DiagnosticsUnavailableView renders instead of the checks list (SettingsView.swift:304-306)
• The unavailable view shows a 'Diagnostics are not loaded' label and surfaces session.backendStatus.lastError, or a fallback prompt to refresh and inspect permissions/route recovery/support coverage (SettingsView.swift:316-325)
• It offers a prominent 'Refresh Diagnostics' button (calls refreshDiagnostics) and a bordered 'Recover Routes' button (calls recoverRoutes) (SettingsView.swift:327-341)
• recoverRoutes() reattaches managed routes, then re-fetches diagnostics on both success and failure paths and toasts the result (AppStore.swift:1181-1202)
Acceptance Criteria:
• Opening the Diagnostics settings tab triggers a diagnostics refresh automatically
• While diagnostics is nil, the panel shows 'Diagnostics are not loaded' with Refresh and Recover Routes buttons
• If backendStatus.lastError is set, the unavailable view shows that error text instead of the generic prompt
• Clicking 'Recover Routes' from the unavailable view attempts recovery and refreshes diagnostics regardless of outcome
• After a successful refresh, the unavailable view is replaced by the list of checks
Impl Notes: Because backend.diagnosticsReport() always returns a non-nil report and refreshDiagnostics runs on .onAppear, the nil/unavailable branch is effectively only visible for the brief window before the first async refresh completes — and the mixer's DiagnosticsPanel (MainWindowView.swift:531) silently renders nothing while nil rather than showing an empty state. So the carefully-built DiagnosticsUnavailableView (with lastError surfacing and recover action) is nearly dead UI in practice. The lastError shown here is whatever is on the current session snapshot, which may be stale relative to the actual refresh that just succeeded.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/correctness] Unavailable view shows a stale lastError because refreshDiagnostics never updates the session snapshot (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1204-1208) — Re-read AppStore.swift:1204-1208: refreshDiagnostics() only assigns diagnostics = await backend.diagnosticsReport(); it never re-reads currentSnapshot(), so session.backendStatus.lastError is left at whatever the last full session sync produced. DiagnosticsUnavailableView surfaces store.session.backendStatus.lastError (SettingsView.swift:324). Thus the error text in the unavailable state can be stale relative to the report that the same refresh just produced. (Note: recoverRoutes at :1184-1188 does resync session, so this is specific to the standalone refreshDiagnostics path wired to onAppear and the Refresh button.) Confirmed. | Fix: Have refreshDiagnostics() also refresh the session snapshot (session = await backend.currentSnapshot()) when fetching the report, or have DiagnosticsReport carry a lastError field the view reads so the displayed error matches report generation.
• [LOW/dead-code] DiagnosticsUnavailableView is near-unreachable and the mixer panel shows no empty state when diagnostics is nil (Sources/Waves/Settings/SettingsView.swift:304-306) — Re-read: backend.diagnosticsReport() returns a non-optional DiagnosticsReport (AudioControlBackend.swift:54) so it is never nil, store.diagnostics starts nil (AppStore.swift:30) and DiagnosticsSettingsView refreshes onAppear, so store.diagnostics is nil only for the brief async window before the first report resolves. The DiagnosticsUnavailableView (SettingsView.swift:304-306, 316-346) with its lastError surfacing + Refresh + Recover Routes is therefore effectively dead UI. Worse, the mixer DiagnosticsPanel renders an empty DisclosureGroup body when diagnostics is nil (MainWindowView.swift:530-557: 'if let diagnostics' with no else), so during that window an expanded panel shows nothing. Confirmed. | Fix: Give the mixer DiagnosticsPanel an explicit empty/loading state mirroring DiagnosticsUnavailableView, or let diagnosticsReport()/refreshDiagnostics return nil on real backend failure so the unavailable state is genuinely reachable.

Feature: Route health badge in the mixer header
User Story: As a user glancing at the mixer, I want an at-a-glance routing-status badge in the output header, so that I instantly know whether per-app control is Ready, Limited, or Needs attention without opening diagnostics.
Expected Behavior:
• RouteHealthBadge shows a capsule label with icon, driven by backendStatus (MainWindowView.swift:479-520)
• When isRouteRecoveryHealthy it reads 'Ready' with a green checkmark.circle.fill (MainWindowView.swift:493-509)
• When not healthy but lastError != nil it reads 'Needs attention' in red with exclamationmark.triangle.fill (MainWindowView.swift:497-512)
• When not healthy and no lastError it reads 'Limited' in secondary color (MainWindowView.swift:500-514)
• The badge exposes a VoiceOver/help string 'Routing status: <title>' (MainWindowView.swift:489-490, 517-519)
• It is placed in the OutputSummaryView header next to the device name and live summary (MainWindowView.swift:461)
Acceptance Criteria:
• When route recovery is healthy the badge shows 'Ready' with a green checkmark
• When there is a lastError and routing is unhealthy the badge shows 'Needs attention' in red with a warning triangle
• When routing is unhealthy with no recorded error the badge shows 'Limited' in a muted color
• The badge's accessibility label reads 'Routing status: ' followed by the current title
• The badge updates reactively when session.backendStatus changes
Impl Notes: Clean, reactive, and accessible. The 'Limited' vs 'Needs attention' distinction hinges entirely on whether lastError happens to be populated, which is a fragile proxy: an unhealthy route with a transient/cleared error will read as the softer 'Limited' even when control is fully broken. The warning-triangle icon is shared by both unhealthy states, so only the title/color differentiate them.
Errors Found: • [LOW/ux] RouteHealthBadge 'Limited' state shows a warning-triangle icon and is decided by a fragile lastError proxy (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MainWindowView.swift:503-505) — Re-read MainWindowView.swift:503-505: systemImage returns 'exclamationmark.triangle.fill' for every non-healthy state, so the muted 'Limited' state pairs a secondary-gray label with a warning triangle — visually contradictory. And per title (493-501) / color (507-515), the only thing separating 'Limited' from 'Needs attention' is whether session.backendStatus.lastError is non-nil. An unhealthy route whose error was transient or cleared reads as the softer 'Limited' even though per-app control is fully broken, understating the problem. Confirmed. | Fix: Use a distinct lower-alarm SF Symbol for 'Limited' (e.g. minus.circle or exclamationmark.circle) and base the Limited/Needs-attention distinction on a concrete capability signal (captureAuthorization / hasRouteErrors) rather than solely lastError != nil.

Feature: Route health gates onboarding readiness
User Story: As a first-run user, I want the onboarding flow to confirm route health is ready before declaring setup complete, so that I don't finish setup while per-app routing is still broken.
Expected Behavior:
• syncOnboarding maps onboarding.routeHealthReady = snapshot.backendStatus.isRouteRecoveryHealthy (AppStore.swift:1361)
• OnboardingState.isComplete requires permissionsGranted && outputDeviceVisible && routeHealthReady (AppStore.swift:1501-1505)
• The onboarding route-health step calls store.refreshDiagnostics() and gates step completion/advancement on routeHealthReady (OnboardingView.swift:34-42, 124, 143, 153)
• Diagnostics are refreshed across virtually every state-changing path (start, refresh, preset apply, device change, recover, exclude) keeping routeHealthReady current (AppStore.swift:215, 246, 430, 455, 526, 537, 572, 583, 807, 1012, 1024, 1186, 1197)
Acceptance Criteria:
• Onboarding cannot report complete unless isRouteRecoveryHealthy is true at the time of the last sync
• The onboarding route-health step is marked ready only when route recovery is healthy
• Triggering 'Recover Routes' during onboarding refreshes diagnostics and can flip the route-health step to ready
• routeHealthReady reflects the most recent snapshot after any session-changing action
Impl Notes: Tightly integrated: route-health state is a first-class onboarding gate and is kept fresh by refreshing diagnostics on nearly every mutation. This is more a consumer of route health than a distinct diagnostics-export feature, but it is the most user-visible consequence of the route-health signal. Concern: isRouteRecoveryHealthy can be true even when capture permission is undetermined in some snapshot paths (it is computed from supportsPerAppRouting && captureAuthorization==.authorized && !hasRouteErrors at WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1089), so the gate is sound at the source but depends on captureAuthorization having been probed first.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/logic] routeHealthReady is not updated on volume/mute/boost/preset paths despite diagnostics being refreshed there (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:525-537) — Re-read all syncOnboarding / diagnostics call sites. routeHealthReady is only assigned inside syncOnboarding(using:) (AppStore.swift:1361), called at 100, 206, 218, 247, 809, 1188 — start, refresh, device-change restore, exclude, recoverRoutes. The volume-apply (429-430/453-455), mute (525-526/536-537), boost (571-572/581-583), and preset-apply (1012, 1024) paths refresh store.diagnostics and call mergeAppState (which DOES update session.backendStatus at :1429, so the RouteHealthBadge re-derives correctly) but never call syncOnboarding. So after a mute/boost/volume/preset action, onboarding.routeHealthReady goes stale relative to the now-fresh session.backendStatus.isRouteRecoveryHealthy — a real state-desync: the mixer route badge can flip while the onboarding route-health step (and isFullyComplete) lag until the next full sync. Confirmed. | Fix: Call syncOnboarding(using: session) after mergeAppState on the mute/boost/volume/preset-apply paths (or factor a helper that always pairs a session refresh with syncOnboarding) so routeHealthReady tracks every session-changing action.
• [MEDIUM/dead-code] OnboardingState.isReadyForEverydayUse (the cited completion gate) is unused; live UI uses a divergent completeness check (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1504-1506) — Re-read AppStore.swift:1504-1506 and grep'd the whole tree: OnboardingState.isReadyForEverydayUse (permissionsGranted && outputDeviceVisible && routeHealthReady) is never referenced anywhere except its own definition. The completeness shown to the user is OnboardingView.isFullyComplete (OnboardingView.swift:39-43), which checks audioComponentInstalled && outputDeviceVisible && routeHealthReady — it gates on audioComponentInstalled, NOT permissionsGranted. So the only completeness logic that drives UI omits the capture-permission gate (a 14.2 system with capture denied can still read 'You're all set!'), while the permission-aware gate is dead code. Confirmed. | Fix: Delete isReadyForEverydayUse, or have OnboardingView.isFullyComplete delegate to store.onboarding.isReadyForEverydayUse so there is a single source of truth for completeness.
• [LOW/inconsistency] Story claims onboarding refreshes diagnostics in .onAppear, but OnboardingView has no .onAppear (Sources/Waves/Features/Onboarding/OnboardingView.swift:45-137) — Re-read OnboardingView.swift in full: there is no .onAppear modifier anywhere. Lines 30-43 are completionProgress/isFullyComplete computed properties; 122-125 is the global 'Refresh Status' button (store.refresh + store.refreshDiagnostics); 139-157 are isStepComplete/canPerformAction; the route-health step (index 2) action is store.recoverRoutes() (line 166), which does refresh diagnostics and syncOnboarding internally. So the gate can still flip via explicit action, but there is no automatic onAppear refresh — on first showing, the route-health step reflects whatever the last sync produced rather than a fresh probe. Confirmed (low: requires only one extra user click). | Fix: Add .onAppear { store.refresh(); store.refreshDiagnostics() } to OnboardingView (or the route-health step) so route-health state is probed when the flow is shown, matching the documented behavior.

Feature: Guided setup checklist with progress
User Story: As a new Waves user, I want a step-by-step setup checklist that shows what is done and what still needs attention, so that I can get per-app audio working without guessing.
Expected Behavior:
• The Settings window exposes a "Setup" tab (checklist icon) that renders OnboardingView (SettingsView.swift:13-16).
• The checklist shows four ordered steps: confirm managed audio support, validate output-device visibility, test device switching/recovery, and enable accessibility for keyboard shortcuts (OnboardingView.swift:7-28).
• A ProgressView reflects completion as completedSteps/3, counting only audioComponentInstalled, outputDeviceVisible, and routeHealthReady — the accessibility step is intentionally excluded from progress (OnboardingView.swift:30-37).
• Each step row shows a green check when complete, an amber warning triangle with an action button when actionable, or a grey pending circle otherwise, with accessibility labels Completed/Needs action/Pending (OnboardingView.swift:185-218).
• Header copy switches to "You're all set!" and a green "Ready to use" capabilities panel appears once audioComponentInstalled && outputDeviceVisible && routeHealthReady are all true (OnboardingView.swift:39-43, 51-53, 74-117).
• A bottom "Refresh Status" button calls store.refresh() + store.refreshDiagnostics(); an "Enable Launch at Login" button appears only while launchAtLoginEnabled is false (OnboardingView.swift:121-134).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Opening Settings › Setup shows exactly four steps in the listed order.
• With all three gating flags true the progress bar reads 100%, tints green, and the "Ready to use" panel is visible.
• With any gating flag false the header reads the "Let's get Waves set up" copy and the panel is hidden.
• Granting/denying accessibility changes step 4's icon but does NOT move the progress bar.
• "Enable Launch at Login" button is hidden once launch-at-login is enabled.
Impl Notes: Wired end to end. UX gap: the most decisive permission for routing to actually work — audio-capture (microphone/TCC) — is NOT one of the four checklist steps. A user can reach a 100% green "You're all set" checklist while capture is denied, in which case per-app volume/mute silently does nothing; capture status only surfaces in the separate Advanced diagnostics tab. The unused shared component SetupStepRow (Sources/Waves/Shared/UI/SetupStepRow.swift) is dead code — OnboardingView uses its own private EnhancedSetupStepRow.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/ux] Decisive audio-capture (Microphone/TCC) permission is never surfaced on the setup checklist (Sources/Waves/Features/Onboarding/OnboardingView.swift:7-28) — Verified in code. The four checklist steps (OnboardingView.swift:7-28) cover OS support, output-device visibility, route recovery, and accessibility. The decisive gate for per-app volume/mute/boost — Core Audio capture authorization (captureAuthorization == .authorized) — is only folded into routeHealthReady (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1089). When capture is denied, routeHealthReady is false so step 3 stays incomplete, the user is stranded at 2/3 (66%) with only a 'Test Recovery' button (which silently no-ops, see SETUP-04), and no copy anywhere on the checklist names Microphone permission as the blocker. Capture status only appears in the separate Advanced tab. Note: the first tester's framing that the user can reach 100% 'all set' while capture is denied is slightly imprecise (routeHealthReady gates isFullyComplete, so 100% is NOT reachable with capture denied); the real, confirmed defect is the unexplained stuck step with no guided fix path. | Fix: Add a fifth checklist step (or split step 3) for 'Allow audio capture' bound to backend captureAuthorization, with an 'Open System Settings' action deep-linking to Privacy_Microphone and copy explaining routing silently no-ops without it.
• [LOW/dead-code] Shared SetupStepRow component is dead code (Sources/Waves/Shared/UI/SetupStepRow.swift:3) — Confirmed via grep: 'SetupStepRow(' is never constructed anywhere; only the struct declaration at SetupStepRow.swift:3 exists. OnboardingView builds its own private EnhancedSetupStepRow (OnboardingView.swift:62,177). The shared component is also strictly less capable (no action button, no accessibility labels). | Fix: Delete Sources/Waves/Shared/UI/SetupStepRow.swift, or consolidate OnboardingView onto it.
• [LOW/dead-code] OnboardingState.isReadyForEverydayUse and permissionsGranted are unused (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1504) — Confirmed via grep. syncOnboarding writes onboarding.permissionsGranted (AppStore.swift:1358) but the only reader is OnboardingState.isReadyForEverydayUse (AppStore.swift:1504-1506), which itself has zero references anywhere. OnboardingView computes its own isFullyComplete (OnboardingView.swift:39-43) instead. This is a parallel readiness computation that can silently diverge from the live one. | Fix: Remove isReadyForEverydayUse and permissionsGranted, or have OnboardingView consume a single store-level readiness computation.
• [LOW/accessibility] Progress bar exposes no value/label to VoiceOver (Sources/Waves/Features/Onboarding/OnboardingView.swift:57) — Confirmed. ProgressView(value: completionProgress) at OnboardingView.swift:57 has no accessibilityLabel/accessibilityValue, so VoiceOver gets no 'X of 3 steps complete' context. (Note: completionProgress = completedSteps/3, so the suggested completionProgress*3 yields the integer step count — math is correct.) | Fix: Add .accessibilityLabel("Setup progress") and .accessibilityValue("\(Int(completionProgress*3)) of 3 steps complete") to the ProgressView.
• [LOW/inconsistency] Two simultaneously visible buttons labeled 'Refresh Status' (Sources/Waves/Features/Onboarding/OnboardingView.swift:122) — Confirmed. steps[0].action is 'Refresh Status' (OnboardingView.swift:11), rendered when canPerformAction(0) = !audioComponentInstalled (line 151,204). The bottom global button is also 'Refresh Status' (line 122) and additionally calls refreshDiagnostics(). When step 1 is incomplete both render at once with identical labels but slightly different behavior — ambiguous for keyboard/VoiceOver users. | Fix: Rename the step-0 action (e.g. 'Re-check support') or give the buttons distinct accessibility labels.

Feature: Managed-audio support check (macOS 14.2+ / process taps)
User Story: As a user on an older or unsupported macOS, I want Waves to tell me clearly that per-app routing requires macOS 14.2+, so that I understand why the feature is unavailable instead of seeing silent failures.
Expected Behavior:
• Step 1 ("Confirm managed audio support") is complete iff onboarding.audioComponentInstalled, which mirrors snapshot.backendStatus.isAudioComponentInstalled (OnboardingView.swift:141, AppStore.swift:1357).
• isAudioComponentInstalled is set to supportsPerAppRouting, true only under #available(macOS 14.2, *) (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:310-316, 1087).
• When incomplete, the row's "Refresh Status" action re-runs store.refresh() to re-probe support (OnboardingView.swift:151, 161-162).
• The Advanced diagnostics tab shows an "Audio component" check: passed with "Process tap routing is supported" or warning "Per-app routing needs macOS 14.2 or newer." (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:245-251).
Acceptance Criteria:
• On macOS 14.2+ step 1 reads complete and the Audio component diagnostic passes.
• On macOS < 14.2 step 1 stays incomplete and shows the 14.2 warning copy in both the checklist detail and the diagnostics check.
• Tapping "Refresh Status" on this step calls refresh() and updates audioComponentInstalled from a fresh snapshot.
Impl Notes: Correctly gated on the OS version that actually enables Core Audio process taps. This step measures OS capability only, not whether capture is granted — accurate to its label.

Feature: Output-device visibility check
User Story: As a user, I want Waves to confirm it can see my output device before it tries to route audio, so that I know speakers/headphones are recognized by macOS.
Expected Behavior:
• Step 2 ("Validate output device visibility") is complete iff onboarding.outputDeviceVisible, set from snapshot.currentDevice != nil (OnboardingView.swift:142, AppStore.swift:1360).
• When incomplete (no current device), the row exposes a "Refresh Devices" action that calls store.refresh() (OnboardingView.swift:152, 163-164).
• Step 3's recovery action is gated on step 2 being complete: canPerformAction(2) requires outputDeviceVisible && !routeHealthReady (OnboardingView.swift:153).
• Current device name is shown in the Audio settings tab and the diagnostics export ("Output device: …" / "No output device") (AppStore.swift:139-141, 731).
Acceptance Criteria:
• With at least one output device present, step 2 reads complete.
• With no current device, step 2 shows the warning and a "Refresh Devices" button that triggers refresh().
• Step 3's "Test Recovery" button is disabled/hidden until step 2 is complete.
Impl Notes: Two correctness/UX concerns. (1) OnboardingState.outputDeviceVisible defaults to true (AppStore.swift:1500), so before the first snapshot loads the step optimistically reads complete. (2) buildSnapshot almost always synthesizes a fallback currentDevice ("System Output") when currentOutputDevice() fails (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1062-1070), so snapshot.currentDevice is rarely nil and this step will read complete even when no real hardware device is selectable — making the check close to a no-op in practice.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/correctness] Output-device check is a near no-op because buildSnapshot synthesizes a fallback 'System Output' device (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1062-1070) — Confirmed in code. outputDeviceVisible is set from snapshot.currentDevice != nil (AppStore.swift:1360).
