But buildSnapshot computes currentDevice = (try? currentOutputDevice()) ?? previousSnapshot?.currentDevice ?? AudioDevice(id: "system-output", name: "System Output", ...) (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1062-1070), so currentDevice is effectively never nil even when currentDefaultOutputDeviceID() throws managedRouteUnavailable (lines 654-656). Step 2 therefore reads complete even when no real hardware output is selectable — the AC state 'With no current device, step 2 shows the warning and a Refresh Devices button' is practically unreachable. The synthetic fallback even sets isManagedRouteAvailable: supportsPerAppRouting, masking the failure. | Fix: Drive outputDeviceVisible from the presence of a real device (e.g. !availableOutputDevices().isEmpty, or a synthesized-vs-real flag on AudioDevice) and do not let the synthetic 'System Output' fallback satisfy the visibility check.
• [LOW/edge-case] outputDeviceVisible defaults to true and can show complete from a stale cached session before the live probe (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1500) — Confirmed. OnboardingState.outputDeviceVisible defaults to true (AppStore.swift:1500). init() runs syncOnboarding(using: session) where session = sessionStore.load() ?? emptySession (AppStore.swift:83,100), and start() also calls syncOnboarding from the warm/cached snapshot before backend.start() (AppStore.swift:203-207) and again after the live snapshot (line 218). For emptySession it correctly flips to false (currentDevice nil), but a cached session with a now-stale currentDevice makes step 2 read complete from cache, momentarily reflecting a device that may no longer be present until the live buildSnapshot lands. | Fix: Default outputDeviceVisible to false and only set true from a freshly built live snapshot, or mark cached-derived status as provisional until backend.start() completes.

Feature: Route-health / recovery test
User Story: As a user, I want to verify that Waves can establish and reattach managed routes (e.g. after a device switch), so that I trust per-app control will survive device changes.
Expected Behavior:
• Step 3 ("Test device switching") is complete iff onboarding.routeHealthReady, mirroring snapshot.backendStatus.isRouteRecoveryHealthy (OnboardingView.swift:143, AppStore.swift:1361).
• isRouteRecoveryHealthy is true only when supportsPerAppRouting && captureAuthorization == .authorized && no app is in routingState .error (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:1089).
• The step's "Test Recovery" action calls store.recoverRoutes(), which disposes controllers, rebuilds the snapshot, reattaches previously-managed routes, refreshes diagnostics, re-syncs onboarding, and toasts success or failure (OnboardingView.swift:166, AppStore.swift:1181-1202; backend recoverRoutes WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:204-221).
• On recovery failure the store falls back to currentSnapshot(), still refreshes diagnostics, and shows an error toast (AppStore.swift:1194-1200).
• The Advanced diagnostics tab shows a "Route recovery" check: passed "Per-app routing is active and can be reapplied" or warning about active setup/control errors (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:264-270).
Acceptance Criteria:
• With routing supported, capture authorized, and no route errors, step 3 reads complete and the Route recovery diagnostic passes.
• Tapping "Test Recovery" invokes recoverRoutes(); a success toast "Routes recovered" appears on success and an error toast on failure.
• If capture is not authorized, step 3 cannot become complete regardless of the recovery action.
Impl Notes: Logic is sound and route health correctly folds in capture authorization. Subtle UX trap: because routeHealthReady requires capture authorization, a user with capture denied sees step 3 stuck incomplete with only a "Test Recovery" button — but the actual blocker (microphone permission) is never surfaced on the checklist, only in the Advanced tab. Recovery here can therefore appear to "not work" with no on-checklist explanation.
Errors Found:
• [HIGH/error-handling] 'Test Recovery' always shows a success toast even when recovery leaves routes unhealthy (capture denied) (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1189-1193) — Confirmed. AppStore.recoverRoutes() shows the green 'Routes recovered' toast on any non-throwing return (AppStore.swift:1189-1193). Verified that backend.recoverRoutes() (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:204-221) has no throwing calls (disposeControllers, buildSnapshot, reattachRoutes are all non-throwing), so it cannot throw in production. buildSnapshot recomputes isRouteRecoveryHealthy = supportsPerAppRouting && captureAuthorization == .authorized && !hasRouteErrors (line 1089). So with capture denied: recoverRoutes() does not throw, the success toast fires, yet syncOnboarding sets routeHealthReady = false (AppStore.swift:1361) and step 3 visibly stays in the warning state. The user gets a green 'Routes recovered' confirmation contradicting the just-displayed incomplete step. (The success detail text 'Managed routing paths were reattached' is also misleading when nothing was managed.) | Fix: After recoverRoutes, branch on session.backendStatus.isRouteRecoveryHealthy: show success only when true; otherwise show a warning toast naming the real blocker (e.g. 'Audio capture not granted — open Microphone settings').
• [LOW/dead-code] Recovery failure toast path is effectively unreachable with the production backend (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1194-1200) — Confirmed. AppStore.recoverRoutes()'s catch branch (AppStore.swift:1194-1200) shows 'Recovery failed' and falls back to currentSnapshot(). The protocol declares recoverRoutes() async throws, but WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.recoverRoutes() (lines 204-221) contains no throwing calls, so it never throws in production — the error toast and currentSnapshot() fallback are dead in practice, and real failure conditions surface as a success toast (prior issue). | Fix: Have the backend surface real failures (throw when reattach fails or routes remain in .error), or drive success/failure messaging from the resulting isRouteRecoveryHealthy rather than from try/catch.
• [LOW/dead-code] Backend sets isRouteRecoveryHealthy = true then immediately overwrites it (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:212) — Confirmed. recoverRoutes() assigns snapshot.backendStatus.isRouteRecoveryHealthy = true and lastError = nil (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:212-213), but the next statement snapshot = await buildSnapshot(merging: snapshot) (line 214) replaces the entire backendStatus with a freshly computed one (lines 1086-1091). The line-212/213 writes have no observable effect. | Fix: Remove the line-212/213 assignments and let buildSnapshot be the single source of route-health truth.
• [LOW/ux] Once routes are healthy the 'Test Recovery' button disappears, so the check cannot be re-run from the checklist (Sources/Waves/Features/Onboarding/OnboardingView.swift:153) — Confirmed. canPerformAction(2) = outputDeviceVisible && !routeHealthReady (OnboardingView.swift:153) and the action button only renders when canPerformAction is true (OnboardingView.swift:204). Once routeHealthReady is true the button is hidden, yet the step copy says 'Run a quick check here' (OnboardingView.swift:20). A user wanting to re-verify recovery has no control on the checklist (only the Audio/Advanced tab's 'Recover Routes'). | Fix: Keep a secondary 'Re-test' button on the completed step, or move the test action out of the canPerformAction gate.

Feature: Accessibility permission step (optional, opens System Settings)
User Story: As a user who wants global keyboard shortcuts, I want a one-tap way to grant Accessibility, so that I can enable shortcuts while understanding per-app routing works without it.
Expected Behavior:
• Step 4 ("Enable keyboard shortcut permission") is complete iff onboarding.accessibilityPermissionGranted, set from AXIsProcessTrusted() during syncOnboarding (OnboardingView.swift:144, AppStore.swift:1359).
• The detail copy explicitly states the permission is optional and that per-app volume routing works without it (OnboardingView.swift:24-27).
• When not granted, the action button "Open System Settings" deep-links to x-apple.systempreferences:com.apple.preference.security?Privacy_Accessibility (OnboardingView.swift:167-170).
• This step is excluded from completionProgress and from isFullyComplete, so the checklist can reach 100%/"all set" without it (OnboardingView.swift:30-43).
• The Advanced diagnostics tab shows an "Accessibility permission" check reflecting AXIsProcessTrusted() with guidance copy (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:257-263, 318-320).
Acceptance Criteria:
• When Accessibility is not trusted, step 4 shows the warning state and an "Open System Settings" button that opens the Privacy_Accessibility pane.
• Granting Accessibility (then refreshing) flips step 4 to complete without changing the progress bar or "all set" state.
• The Accessibility diagnostic check matches the step's complete/incomplete state.
Impl Notes: Correctly treated as optional and clearly communicated. accessibilityPermissionGranted only refreshes on syncOnboarding (refresh/recover/device-change), so after the user grants it in System Settings the checklist won't update until they hit "Refresh Status" — acceptable but worth a note. The deep-link URL is the standard pane and matches README troubleshooting guidance.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/inconsistency] Accessibility diagnostic and checklist step can disagree after granting permission (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1204-1208) — Confirmed. Step 4 completion uses onboarding.accessibilityPermissionGranted, refreshed only in syncOnboarding (AppStore.swift:1359), which is called by refresh/recoverRoutes/handleDeviceChange/start. The Advanced tab's Accessibility diagnostic reads hasAccessibilityPermission = AXIsProcessTrusted() live on every diagnosticsReport() (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:259,318-320). DiagnosticsSettingsView.onAppear calls store.refreshDiagnostics() (SettingsView.swift:310-312), which only rebuilds the report (AppStore.swift:1204-1208) and does NOT call syncOnboarding. So after granting Accessibility and opening Advanced, the diagnostic flips to 'passed' while step 4 still reads incomplete until 'Refresh Status' is pressed. | Fix: Have refreshDiagnostics() also re-read AXIsProcessTrusted() into onboarding.accessibilityPermissionGranted (or run a lightweight permission re-sync), so both surfaces share one source.
• [LOW/ux] Granting Accessibility does not auto-update the checklist; requires manual Refresh (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1359) — Confirmed. After 'Open System Settings' (OnboardingView.swift:167-170) and granting Accessibility, returning to Waves does not flip step 4 because accessibilityPermissionGranted is only re-read on the next syncOnboarding (refresh/recover/device-change/start). There is no window-activation or polling refresh, so step 4 stays stale with no hint that a refresh is needed. | Fix: Re-probe AXIsProcessTrusted() when the Settings window/app reactivates (e.g. NSApplication.didBecomeActive or scenePhase .active) or when the Setup tab reappears.

Feature: Advanced diagnostics: permission/route report and copy-to-clipboard
User Story: As a user troubleshooting why audio control isn't working, I want a detailed diagnostics panel I can copy into a bug report, so that I (or support) can see permission, device, and route-health status at a glance.
Expected Behavior:
• The Settings "Advanced" tab renders a running-apps summary plus a list of DiagnosticsReport checks, refreshing diagnostics on appear (SettingsView.swift:28-31, 269-313).
• Diagnostics include five checks: Audio component, Audio capture permission, Accessibility permission, Route recovery, and an informational Support matrix coverage summary (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:241-277).
• Audio-capture status comes from a real TCC probe: refreshCaptureAuthorization() creates and immediately destroys a private global process tap; success => authorized, failure => notGranted, run on every buildSnapshot (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:325-358, 988-991).
• Capture-not-granted detail directs the user to System Settings › Privacy & Security › Microphone and to refresh (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:288-298).
• "Copy Diagnostics" writes a plain-text report (macOS version, output device, component/permission/route flags, last error, per-app routing/volume/mute/boost, and check results) to the pasteboard and toasts confirmation (SettingsView.swift:283-289, AppStore.swift:725-779).
• When no diagnostics are loaded, a fallback view shows the last backend error and "Refresh Diagnostics" / "Recover Routes" buttons (SettingsView.swift:304-347).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Opening Advanced populates the five diagnostic checks; the capture check reflects an actual tap-create probe rather than a static flag.
• With capture denied, the Audio capture permission check reads failed with the Microphone-settings guidance.
• "Copy Diagnostics" places a multi-line report on the clipboard containing macOS version, output device, all three status booleans, and per-app rows, and shows a "Diagnostics copied" toast.
• With diagnostics nil, the unavailable view shows the last error (or default copy) and both recovery buttons.
Impl Notes: Strong, honest implementation — the capture probe genuinely tests TCC rather than trusting OS version, and the report is comprehensive. Concern: this Advanced tab is the ONLY place the decisive audio-capture permission is shown; it is absent from the Setup checklist (see SETUP-01), so a user who never opens Advanced has no guided path to discover/grant Microphone permission. The probe's create/destroy tap on every snapshot is cheap but does run frequently.
Errors Found: • [MEDIUM/correctness] Opening Advanced does not re-run the capture probe; the capture check can show a stale TCC result (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:241-256) — Confirmed. The real tap-create probe refreshCaptureAuthorization() (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:332-358) runs only inside buildSnapshot (line 991), i.e. on start/refresh/recoverRoutes/device-change. DiagnosticsSettingsView.onAppear calls store.refreshDiagnostics() (SettingsView.swift:310-312) -> backend.diagnosticsReport(), which reads the cached captureAuthorization property via captureAuthorizationStatus/captureAuthorizationDetail (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:254-255,280-299) and does NOT re-probe. So if the user changes Microphone permission and merely opens/reopens Advanced, the 'Audio capture permission' check reflects the last probe, not current TCC state, until a full refresh. | Fix: Call refreshCaptureAuthorization() (or backend.refresh()) before building the report on Advanced-tab appear, or have diagnosticsReport() re-probe capture authorization itself.

Feature: Toggle Launch at Login from Settings
User Story: As a Waves user, I want a "Launch at login" toggle in Settings so that Waves starts automatically every time I log into my Mac without my having to open it manually.
Expected Behavior:
• GeneralSettingsView renders a SwiftUI Toggle labeled "Launch at login" bound to store.launchAtLoginEnabled (SettingsView.swift:51-56).
• Turning the toggle on calls AppStore.launchAtLoginEnabled setter, which calls loginItemService.setEnabled(true) -> SMAppService.mainApp.register() (AppStore.swift:165, LoginItemService.swift:29-31).
• Turning the toggle off calls setEnabled(false) -> SMAppService.mainApp.unregister() (LoginItemService.swift:32-34).
• After registering/unregistering, the setter re-reads the live SMAppService status and writes status.isEnabled back into preferences and onboarding, then persists (AppStore.swift:166-169).
• The toggle's displayed value comes from preferences.launchAtLoginEnabled, which is reconciled to the actual OS login-item state on every relevant refresh (AppStore.swift:161-162, 1362-1364).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Enabling the toggle registers Waves as an SMAppService login item and, after a real login, Waves launches automatically.
• Disabling the toggle unregisters the login item and Waves no longer auto-launches.
• The toggle visually reflects the actual OS state, not just the last requested value, because the setter reconciles from loginItemService.status after each change.
• The preference change is persisted (preferencesStore.save) so it survives an app restart.
Impl Notes: Cleanly wired end-to-end using the modern ServiceManagement SMAppService.mainApp API (correct for sandboxed/notarized apps, no separate helper bundle needed). The setter is robust: it reconciles the toggle to the real OS status after every call rather than trusting the requested value, so the UI cannot drift out of sync. Persistence and onboarding mirror are both updated. No automated tests cover this path.

Feature: Toggle Launch at Login from the menu bar mixer
User Story: As a menu-bar-first user, I want to flip "Launch at login" directly from the menu bar mixer popover so that I can change startup behavior without opening the full Settings window.
Expected Behavior:
• MenuBarMixerView shows a switch-style Toggle labeled "Launch at login" (labelsHidden, accessibilityLabel set) bound to the same store.launchAtLoginEnabled property (MenuBarMixerView.swift:90-100).
• Flipping it runs the identical AppStore.launchAtLoginEnabled setter path as the Settings toggle, so register/unregister, reconciliation, persistence, and toast behavior are shared (AppStore.swift:161-186).
• Because both surfaces read the same computed property, changing it in one place is reflected in the other.
Acceptance Criteria:
• The menu bar switch enables/disables the login item identically to the Settings toggle.
• Toggling in the menu bar updates the Settings toggle state (shared source of truth) and vice versa.
• The switch exposes an accessibility label "Launch at login" for VoiceOver.
Impl Notes: Duplicate entry point onto the same setter, so it inherits all the correctness of LOGIN-01. Good that it reuses the binding rather than reimplementing. Minor UX note: with labelsHidden the bare switch relies entirely on adjacent context/accessibility label for meaning; sighted users see only an unlabeled switch in the popover footer.
Errors Found: • [LOW/ux] Menu-bar Launch-at-login switch has no visible label for sighted users (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MenuBarMixerView.swift:99) — Confirmed by re-reading MenuBarMixerView.swift:90-101. The Toggle wraps Text("Launch at login") but applies .labelsHidden(), and the footer HStack contains only the "Open Waves" and "Settings" buttons, a Spacer, and then the bare switch. There is no adjacent visible caption. Sighted users see an unlabeled switch in the popover's bottom-right corner; only VoiceOver users get meaning via accessibilityLabel("Launch at login"). Acceptance criteria (shared binding + VO label) are technically met, but the on-screen affordance is ambiguous. | Fix: Remove .labelsHidden() so the inline "Launch at login" label renders, or add a small visible caption (e.g. a leading Text or a .help tooltip) next to the switch, keeping the accessibilityLabel for VoiceOver.

Feature: Enable Launch at Login during onboarding
User Story: As a first-time user, I want an "Enable Launch at Login" button in onboarding so that I can opt into auto-start as part of initial setup.
Expected Behavior:
• OnboardingView shows an "Enable Launch at Login" bordered button only when store.onboarding.launchAtLoginEnabled is false (OnboardingView.swift:128-133).
• Tapping it sets store.launchAtLoginEnabled = true, running the standard register + reconcile + persist path (OnboardingView.swift:130, AppStore.swift:163-177).
• Once enabled (onboarding.launchAtLoginEnabled becomes true via reconciliation), the button disappears.
• onboarding.launchAtLoginEnabled is seeded from the live SMAppService status at store init and re-synced on every syncOnboarding call (AppStore.swift:95-98, 1362-1363).
Acceptance Criteria:
• The button is visible only while the login item is not enabled.
• Pressing it registers the login item and then hides the button after status reconciles to enabled.
• The onboarding state reflects the real OS login-item status, not a stale value, because it is sourced from loginItemService.status.
Impl Notes: Onboarding offers a one-way enable (no disable from here), which is reasonable for a setup flow. Note the button visibility depends on onboarding.launchAtLoginEnabled being refreshed; it is updated in syncOnboarding, but syncOnboarding is only invoked on refresh/snapshot events, so immediately after pressing the button the disappearance relies on the @Observable store mutation in the setter (which does update onboarding.launchAtLoginEnabled at AppStore.swift:168) rather than on a snapshot refresh — that path is covered, so it works.

Feature: Reconcile and surface approval-required / failure states
User Story: As a user whose system requires approval (or where registration fails), I want clear feedback when enabling Launch at Login does not fully take effect so that I understand why Waves may not auto-start.
Expected Behavior:
• LoginItemService.status maps SMAppService.status to a LoginItemStatus: .enabled->"Enabled", .requiresApproval->isEnabled=false/"Requires approval", .notRegistered->"Disabled", .notFound->"Not found", @unknown->"Unknown" (LoginItemService.swift:13-27).
• When the requested value differs from the reconciled status.isEnabled (e.g. macOS returns .requiresApproval), the setter shows a warning toast titled "Login item needs approval" with status.statusDescription as detail (AppStore.swift:170-177).
• If setEnabled throws, the catch block re-reads status, resyncs preferences + onboarding, persists, and shows an error toast "Login item update failed" with the localized error (AppStore.swift:178-184).
• In all branches the persisted/displayed preference is forced back to the true OS status so the toggle reflects reality even after an approval or failure (AppStore.swift:167-168, 180-181).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Toggling on while the OS reports .requiresApproval leaves the toggle off and shows the "Login item needs approval" warning toast.
• A thrown error from register/unregister shows the "Login item update failed" error toast and leaves the toggle matching the real OS state.
• After any approval-required or error outcome, the toggle does not falsely show "on".
• The status description string corresponds to the SMAppService.Status case.
Impl Notes: State reconciliation and user feedback are genuinely handled (warning vs error toasts, toggle never lies), which is better than most implementations. The real gap is the .requiresApproval recovery UX: macOS routes login-item approval to System Settings > General > Login Items, but the code never deep-links there (the only x-apple.systempreferences link in the app points to Privacy_Accessibility, OnboardingView.swift:168). The user is told "Requires approval" via a 2.4s toast but is given no button/affordance to open the Login Items pane to grant it, so they may be stuck. Also the toast auto-dismisses quickly and the status string is not surfaced persistently anywhere in Settings. No automated tests exist for any branch (status mapping, approval, or error).
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/inconsistency] Warning toast is always titled "Login item needs approval" even for non-approval mismatches (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:172) — Confirmed at AppStore.swift:170-177. The setter shows the warning toast whenever status.isEnabled != newValue. When enabling (newValue == true) this fires for ANY status whose isEnabled is false per LoginItemService.swift:18-25: .requiresApproval ("Requires approval"), .notRegistered ("Disabled"), .notFound ("Not found"), and @unknown ("Unknown"). In every case the title is hardcoded to "Login item needs approval" while only the detail reflects the real status. So if register() returns without throwing but the item lands on .notRegistered/.notFound/.unknown, the user is told it "needs approval" with a contradicting detail (e.g. "Not found"), pointing them at a System-Settings approval recovery path that does not apply. | Fix: Branch the toast on the real status: title "Login item needs approval" only when the underlying SMAppService.status is .requiresApproval; otherwise use a generic title such as "Couldn't enable Launch at login" with status.statusDescription as detail. Expose the SMAppService.Status case (or a richer enum) from LoginItemService so the store can distinguish them.
• [MEDIUM/ux] No affordance to open the Login Items pane when approval is required (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:171) — Confirmed. An SMAppService.mainApp registration returning .requiresApproval must be approved in System Settings > General > Login Items & Extensions. The code surfaces only a 2.4s warning toast (AppStore.swift:170-177) with no button or deep link to that pane. Grep confirms the only x-apple.systempreferences URL in the app targets Privacy_Accessibility (OnboardingView.swift:168), not Login Items, and there is no SMAppService.openSystemSettingsLoginItems() call anywhere. The toast auto-dismisses and the approval status is never surfaced persistently in GeneralSettingsView, so a user whose enable requires approval is told "Requires approval" but has no way to act on it, leaving auto-start silently non-functional. The feature-status implNotes for LOGIN-04 itself flag this gap. | Fix: When the status indicates .requiresApproval, add a persistent status row/banner in GeneralSettingsView showing status.statusDescription plus a button that opens the Login Items pane via SMAppService.openSystemSettingsLoginItems() (or the x-apple.systempreferences Login Items URL). Consider a non-auto-dismissing message for this case.
• [LOW/ux] Pending-approval login item is silently shown as OFF on launch/refresh with no indication (Sources/Waves/Services/LoginItem/LoginItemService.swift:19) — Missed by the first tester. LoginItemService.status maps .requiresApproval to isEnabled=false (LoginItemService.swift:18-19). The init reconcile (AppStore.swift:95) and every syncOnboarding (AppStore.swift:1362-1364) overwrite preferences.launchAtLoginEnabled with status.isEnabled. So a user who enabled Launch-at-login but whose item is registered-pending-approval will, on the next app launch or refresh, see both the Settings and menu-bar toggles show OFF with no toast and no hint that the item is actually registered and only awaiting approval. This is a silent state-surfacing gap distinct from the missing deep link: even before reaching System Settings the user gets no signal that approval is the blocker. Low severity because re-toggling re-triggers the approval toast, but the resting state is misleading. | Fix: Surface the SMAppService.Status (not just a Bool) in the UI: show a persistent "Requires approval" caption/row in GeneralSettingsView when status is .requiresApproval, so the resting toggle state is not silently misleading after a reconcile.

Feature: Persist preference but treat OS as source of truth across launches
User Story: As a returning user, I want Waves to show the correct Launch-at-login state on every launch so that the toggle always matches what the system will actually do, even if the login item was changed outside the app.
Expected Behavior:
• launchAtLoginEnabled defaults to false in UserPreferences and is decoded defensively so an older prefs file missing the key does not wipe settings (UserPreferences.swift:4, 40-49).
• On store initialization the persisted preference is overwritten with loginItemService.status.isEnabled before first use, and re-persisted (AppStore.swift:95-99).
• syncOnboarding re-reads the live login-item status on each refresh and writes it back into both onboarding and preferences (AppStore.swift:1362-1364).
• persistPreferences saves the preferences via preferencesStore (AppStore.swift:1264-1267).
Acceptance Criteria:
• On launch the toggle reflects the actual SMAppService registration state, even if the login item was enabled/disabled via System Settings while the app was closed.
• A preferences file from an older build that lacks the launchAtLoginEnabled key loads without error and other settings are preserved.
• The reconciled state is written back to disk so it is consistent on subsequent reads.
Impl Notes: Strong design choice: the OS login-item status is the source of truth and the stored preference is just a cache that gets reconciled on init and on each syncOnboarding. This prevents the classic drift where the app shows 'on' but the OS disabled it. Defensive Codable decoding avoids the saved preference being a brittle field. One caveat: reconciliation happens on init and on snapshot/refresh events, not via a scenePhase/didBecomeActive observer, so if the user changes the login item in System Settings while the Waves window is already open, the toggle may show a stale value until the next refresh.
Errors Found:
• [LOW/correctness] syncOnboarding reconciles preferences.launchAtLoginEnabled in memory but never persists it (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1364) — Confirmed. syncOnboarding writes both onboarding.launchAtLoginEnabled and preferences.launchAtLoginEnabled from the live login-item status (AppStore.swift:1362-1364) but does not call persistPreferences(). Verified its callers do not persist preferences either: start() (line 218) and refresh() (line 247) call only persistSessionSnapshot() (which saves session, not preferences — AppStore.swift:1438-1440); the device-change handler (line 809) calls only persistSessionSnapshot() (line 806). Only the init path persists the reconciled value (AppStore.swift:99). persistPreferences() itself just does preferencesStore.save (AppStore.swift:1264-1267). So a mid-session reconcile reaches disk only if SettingsView happens to be open at quit (onDisappear -> persistPreferences, SettingsView.swift:40-42). Impact is limited because init always re-overwrites from OS status, so a stale on-disk value is harmless on next launch — hence low — but the code does not match the story's stated persist-on-reconcile behavior (acceptance criterion 3). | Fix: Call persistPreferences() at the end of syncOnboarding after writing preferences.launchAtLoginEnabled (guarded by a change check to avoid redundant writes on every refresh), or have the callers persist preferences when the value changed.
• [LOW/edge-case] Toggle shows stale state if the login item is changed externally while a Waves window is open (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1362) — Confirmed. Reconciliation of preferences.launchAtLoginEnabled from loginItemService.status happens only at store init (AppStore.swift:95) and inside syncOnboarding, which is invoked solely on start/refresh/device-change/snapshot events (callers at AppStore.swift:100,206,218,247,809,1188). Grep confirms no scenePhase observer and no NSApplication.didBecomeActiveNotification observer anywhere in Sources/Waves. If the user enables/disables the Waves login item in System Settings while a Waves window or popover is already open, both the Settings and menu-bar toggles keep showing the old value until the next manual refresh or device change. This satisfies the literal 'on launch' criterion but undermines the story's framing of the OS as the live source of truth. | Fix: Re-read loginItemService.status and update preferences/onboarding when the app/window becomes active — observe NSApplication.didBecomeActiveNotification or use SwiftUI .onChange(of: scenePhase) to run a lightweight reconcile of launchAtLoginEnabled.

Area: Persistence (Session / Preferences / Presets / Device volume presets)
Feature: Session state caching and cross-launch restore
User Story: As a Waves user, I want my per-app volumes, mutes, boosts, pins and pinned output routes to persist and be re-applied when I relaunch the app, so that my customized mix survives quits, crashes and restarts without me redoing everything.
Expected Behavior:
• On every meaningful change (volume commit, mute, boost, pin, device change, preset apply, route recovery) the current AudioSessionSnapshot is written to ~/Library/Application Support/Waves/session.json via SessionStore.save (AppStore persistSessionSnapshot at AppStore.swift:1438-1440).
• Saves run asynchronously on a dedicated userInitiated DispatchQueue and write atomically (SessionStore.swift:50-96).
• To save space, iconTIFFData is explicitly excluded from the persisted snapshot via a manual field-by-field AudioApp remap (SessionStore.swift:53-87).
• On init, AppStore loads the cached session (sessionStore.load() ?? emptySession) before the backend starts so the UI shows the last-known mix immediately (AppStore.swift:83, 203-207).
• On startup the cached snapshot is merged with the freshly built live snapshot by logicalID, copying desiredVolume/appliedVolume/isMuted/isPinned/compatibility/volumeBoost/muteSource/targetDeviceUID from cache onto live apps (mergedSession, AppStore.swift:1383-1415).
• reapplyRestoredAudioState() re-applies saved boost/mute/volume and pinned-device routes to the backend for any app whose state differs from defaults, so audible output matches the restored UI; apps not running this session are skipped on error (AppStore.swift:820-846).
• load() rejects a session.json larger than the 10MB maxFileSize and returns nil (SessionStore.swift:33-38).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Setting an app to 30% then quitting and relaunching shows that app at 30% in the UI and re-applies 30% to the backend once the app is running again.
• session.json exists under Application Support/Waves after the first volume/mute change and contains no iconTIFFData field.
• A session.json over 10MB causes load() to return nil and the app to start from an empty session without crashing.
• Apps present in the cache but no longer running are merged in without throwing, and re-apply errors for them are logged and swallowed (no crash, no toast spam).
• Saving never blocks the main thread (writes occur on the store's background queue).
Impl Notes: Wired end-to-end and reasonably robust. Two correctness concerns: (1) SessionStore is the ONLY store that does NOT back up a corrupt/undecodable session.json — load() just returns nil (SessionStore.swift:43-46), so the next save silently overwrites the unreadable file; the recovery affordance the other three stores have is missing here. (2) The save path manually re-constructs AudioApp field-by-field to drop iconTIFFData (SessionStore.swift:56-87); if AudioApp gains a new persisted field and a developer forgets to add it here it will be silently dropped from the session cache — the comment flags this but nothing enforces it. mergedSession trusts cached compatibility values, which could be stale if an app's tap compatibility changed between launches.
Errors Found:
• [HIGH/correctness] Pinned per-app output device route is NOT re-applied on relaunch (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:820-846) — reapplyRestoredAudioState() (AppStore.swift:820-846) treats an app with a non-nil targetDeviceUID as 'customized' (line 830) and enters the re-apply loop, but the loop body (lines 833-835) only calls backend.setVolumeBoost, setMuted, and setDesiredVolume. It never calls backend.setOutputDevice(uid:forAppID:) (which exists at WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:710 and is used by the live setOutputDevice path at AppStore.swift:658). On a fresh start the backend's internal apps all have targetDeviceUID=nil, so createController routes to the system default (WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:436-444 only honors targetDeviceUID when the backend's own app carries it). The pinned route is merged into the AppStore UI (mergedSession line 1404) but never re-established in the audio engine, so an app pinned to e.g. headphones plays to the system default device after relaunch. | Fix: Inside the loop, when app.targetDeviceUID != nil, also call `try await backend.setOutputDevice(uid: app.targetDeviceUID, forAppID: app.logicalID)` within the existing do/catch so apps not running this session are skipped on error.
• [LOW/error-handling] Corrupt or newer-version session.json is silently discarded and overwritten (no recovery copy) (Sources/Waves/Services/Persistence/SessionStore.swift:43-46) — SessionStore.load() (SessionStore.swift:43-46) returns nil on any decode error, including the dataCorrupted thrown by PersistedSchema for a session.json written by a newer build (schemaVersion > current). Unlike PreferencesStore, PresetStore, and DeviceVolumePresetsStore — all of which call backupCorruptFile() before falling back — SessionStore has no backup step; on the next persistSessionSnapshot() the unreadable file is overwritten via the atomic write at SessionStore.swift:91. A user who downgrades, or whose session.json is partially corrupted, loses the saved mix with no recovery artifact. | Fix: Add a backupCorruptFile() (move session.json to session.json.corrupt) in the catch before returning nil, matching the other three stores, so a newer-build or corrupted session is preserved rather than overwritten.

Area: Persistence (Session / Preferences / Presets / Device volume presets)
Feature: User preferences persistence with forward/backward-compatible decoding
User Story: As a Waves user, I want my settings (sort mode, recent/system app visibility, auto-restore device, auto-pause music, keyboard shortcuts, per-device presets, URL scheme, excluded apps, manual order) to be remembered across launches and to survive app updates, so that I configure Waves once and it stays the way I left it.
Expected Behavior:
• All settings toggles in SettingsView write through to preferences and call store.persistPreferences(), which saves preferences.json atomically on a background queue (SettingsView.swift:41-168, AppStore.swift:1264-1267, PreferencesStore.swift:69-79).
• First launch with no preferences.json returns default UserPreferences() without writing a file (PreferencesStore.swift:33-35).
• UserPreferences decodes each field independently with per-field fallbacks, so a file written by an older build that is missing newly-added keys still loads cleanly instead of resetting every setting (UserPreferences.swift:40-61).
• On init, a manual sortMode with an empty customAppOrder is downgraded to .name, and an unacknowledged URL scheme is force-disabled then marked acknowledged, and launchAtLoginEnabled is reconciled with the actual login-item status (AppStore.swift:88-99).
• excludedAppIDs are persisted and used to stop Waves tapping DAW/VoIP apps; excluding an app sorts and saves the list (AppStore.swift:679-694).
• Manual reordering of apps writes customAppOrder and flips sortMode to .manual, persisting both (AppStore.swift:1339-1354).
• A preferences file exceeding 10MB, or one that fails to decode, is backed up to preferences.json.corrupt and defaults are used instead of overwriting (PreferencesStore.swift:38-67).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Changing any settings toggle and relaunching restores that toggle's value.
• Deleting preferences.json and launching yields all default settings and no error.
• A preferences.json missing a newly-added key (e.g. enablePerDeviceVolumePresets) loads with that key defaulted and all other saved keys intact.
• A corrupt preferences.json is renamed to preferences.json.corrupt on next load and the app starts with defaults.
• Reordering apps in the list persists the order and a later relaunch shows the same manual order.
• Launching with manual sort but no saved order falls back to Name sort.
Impl Notes: Strong implementation: per-field tolerant decoding plus corrupt-file backup makes preferences resilient to schema drift and corruption. Minor concern: launchAtLoginEnabled is persisted into preferences.json but is also overwritten from loginItemService.status on every init and in syncOnboarding (AppStore.swift:95, 1364), so the persisted value is effectively advisory and the OS login-item state is the source of truth — harmless but the stored field is redundant.
Errors Found: • [LOW/edge-case] Manual reorder drops saved order for apps not currently visible/running (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1339-1354) — reorderApps() (AppStore.swift:1339-1354) rebuilds customAppOrder from `visibleApps.map { $0.logicalID }` (line 1345). visibleApps is built from session.apps filtered by showSystemProcesses (lines 103-107) and only contains apps present in the current session. Any logicalID previously stored in customAppOrder for an app not running this session — or for a hidden system process when showSystemProcesses is off — is discarded on the next manual reorder. When that app later relaunches, manualOrderComparator's `firstIndex(of:) ?? Int.max` (line 1330) sorts it to the end instead of its saved slot. | Fix: Merge the new visible ordering into the existing customAppOrder instead of replacing it: keep entries for currently-absent IDs and splice the reordered visible IDs around them.

Area: Persistence (Session / Preferences / Presets / Device volume presets)
Feature: Volume preset library (create/apply/delete, seeded defaults)
User Story: As a Waves user, I want to save the current per-app mix as a named preset and re-apply it later, with sensible built-in Focus/Meeting presets out of the box, so that I can switch between known audio setups (e.g. focus vs. meeting) in one click.
Expected Behavior:
• On first launch presets.json is missing, so PresetStore seeds the built-in defaults (Focus, Meeting) to disk and returns them (PresetStore.swift:30-36; Preset.defaults at AudioSessionSnapshot+Preview.swift:121-144).
• savePreset(named:) captures the current backend state as a Preset, strips any excludedAppIDs entries, then either replaces an existing preset matched case-insensitively by name (preserving its id/createdAt) or appends a new one, and persists the array (AppStore.swift:1031-1064).
• applyPreset filters out excluded apps, applies the preset through the backend, persists the resulting session, and re-checks auto-pause; on failure it falls back to the current backend snapshot and still persists (AppStore.swift:1001-1029).
• deletePresets(at:) removes presets at the given offsets and saves the updated array (AppStore.swift:1066-1077).
• PresetEntry clamps appID length to 256 chars, desiredVolume to [0,1], and volumeBoost to [1,4] on decode/init, defending against malformed persisted data (Preset.swift:31-58).
• A presets file over 10MB or that fails to decode is backed up to presets.json.corrupt and the built-in defaults are returned rather than losing the user's library silently (PresetStore.swift:38-68).
Acceptance Criteria:
• A clean install shows Focus and Meeting presets and writes presets.json.
• Saving a preset with the name of an existing one (any case) updates it in place rather than creating a duplicate.
• Saving a new-named preset appends it and it survives relaunch.
• Applying a preset changes app volumes/mutes accordingly and persists the new session.
• Deleting a preset removes it from disk and it does not reappear on relaunch.
• An excluded app never appears in a newly saved preset and is never re-tapped by applying a preset.
• A corrupt presets.json is renamed to presets.json.corrupt and the defaults are restored.
Impl Notes: Complete and defensive (clamping, exclusion filtering, corrupt-file backup, default seeding). UX note: there is no explicit rename-preset function and no way to restore the built-in defaults once a user has deleted them (defaults are only seeded when presets.json is absent, PresetStore.swift:33-36) — deleting Focus/Meeting and relaunching will NOT bring them back because the file now exists. Replacing a same-named preset silently overwrites without confirmation.
Errors Found:
• [LOW/ux] Deleted built-in Focus/Meeting presets can never be restored (Sources/Waves/Services/Persistence/PresetStore.swift:30-36) — PresetStore.load(defaults:) (PresetStore.swift:30-36) seeds Preset.defaults only when presets.json is absent. Once any presets.json exists, deleting the built-in Focus/Meeting presets and relaunching will not bring them back because the file is present and loaded as-is (lines 46-48). There is no 'restore defaults' affordance anywhere in the UI. | Fix: Add a 'Restore built-in presets' action in SettingsView's preset section that re-adds any missing default-named presets, or re-seed missing defaults on load.
• [LOW/ux] Saving a preset with an existing name overwrites it with no confirmation (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1041-1052) — savePreset() (AppStore.swift:1041-1052) matches an existing preset case-insensitively by trimmed name and replaces it in place without any confirmation. The success toast says 'Preset saved' (line 1055) with no indication that an existing preset's contents were destroyed. A user typing the name of an existing preset silently overwrites it. | Fix: When a same-name match is found, surface a confirmation or at least a distinct 'Preset updated' toast so the overwrite is intentional and visible.
• [LOW/ux] Over-length preset name silently fails to save with no feedback (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MainWindowView.swift:137-147) — In the save sheet handler MainWindowView.savePreset() (MainWindowView.swift:137-147), a trimmed name longer than maxLength=100 hits `guard trimmed.count <= maxLength else { return }` (line 143) and returns without calling store.savePreset and without dismissing the sheet or showing any error. The Save button is only disabled when the name is empty (MainWindowView.swift:612), so for an over-length name the user clicks Save and nothing happens — no toast, no message, sheet stays open. The import path has no such limit at all (see PERSIST-04), so the 100-char rule is both inconsistent and silent. | Fix: Either clamp/truncate the name to 100 chars before saving, or surface an inline validation message / disable Save when the trimmed name exceeds the limit.

Area: Persistence (Session / Preferences / Presets / Device volume presets)
Feature: Preset export/import as JSON files
User Story: As a Waves user, I want to export a preset to a JSON file and import presets shared by others, so that I can back up and share my audio configurations across machines or with teammates.
Expected Behavior:
• exportPreset encodes a single Preset as pretty-printed JSON and writes it via an NSSavePanel, defaulting the filename to <preset name>.json (AppStore.swift:1079-1110).
• importPreset opens an NSOpenPanel limited to .json, single selection, files only (AppStore.swift:1112-1126).
• Import validates the file is <=10MB, the decoded preset name is non-empty, and entries count <=1000 before accepting it; each failure shows an error toast and aborts (AppStore.swift:1128-1148).
• On import, a preset whose name case-insensitively matches an existing one replaces it (keeping the existing id/createdAt); otherwise the imported preset is given a fresh UUID and timestamps to avoid SwiftUI identity collisions, then appended (AppStore.swift:1150-1165).
• After a successful import the presets array is persisted via presetStore.save (AppStore.swift:1167).
• If no main window is available, export/import abort with an error toast (AppStore.swift:1091-1094, 1120-1123).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Exporting a preset produces a readable, pretty-printed JSON file named after the preset.
• Importing that JSON file recreates the preset and persists it.
• Importing a file over 10MB, with an empty name, or with more than 1000 entries is rejected with an error toast and no change to the library.
• Importing a preset whose name matches an existing one updates the existing entry instead of creating a duplicate.
• Importing a uniquely-named preset assigns it a new UUID so list identity does not collide with an existing preset.
Impl Notes: Solid with good input validation (size, name, entry-count, fresh UUID on append). Minor gap: imported PresetEntry values are clamped by PresetEntry.init on decode, but there is no validation/sanitization of the preset's own name length on import, and a malformed JSON that decodes to a Preset with a 256-char-trimmed appID is accepted silently. Export/import operate on one preset at a time only (no bulk export of the whole library).
Errors Found: • [LOW/edge-case] Imported preset name length is not validated or clamped (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1128-1165) — importPreset() (AppStore.swift:1128-1165) validates file size (<=10MB, line 1130), non-empty name (line 1140), and entry count (<=1000, line 1145), but never bounds the name length. PresetEntry.init clamps appID to 256 chars and volume/boost ranges, but Preset.name has no clamp on decode or import. A crafted JSON with a multi-megabyte name (under the 10MB file cap) is accepted, persisted, rendered in the preset list, and used as the default export filename. This is also inconsistent with the save sheet, which enforces a 100-char limit (MainWindowView.swift:142-143). | Fix: After decode, truncate (or reject) names over a sane limit (e.g. 100 chars to match the save sheet) before matching/appending and persisting.

Area: Persistence (Session / Preferences / Presets / Device volume presets)
Feature: Per-device volume presets (remember per-app volumes per output device)
User Story: As a Waves user with multiple output devices, I want each app's volume/mute/boost to be remembered separately per output device and re-applied automatically when I switch devices, so that, e.g., music can be quiet on speakers but loud on headphones without me re-adjusting on every switch.
Expected Behavior:
• When enablePerDeviceVolumePresets is on, every volume/mute/boost change records an AppVolumeSettings under the current device's id in DeviceVolumePresets and saves to deviceVolumePresets.json (AppStore.swift:371-381, 508-519, 555-565; DeviceVolumePresetsStore.save writes pretty-printed atomic JSON, DeviceVolumePresetsStore.swift:69-80).
• Volume changes update the in-memory preset on each tick but defer the disk write to commit so a slider drag does not rewrite the file per frame (AppStore.swift:378-381, 389-392).
• On a device change, if the new device differs from the previous one and the feature is enabled, restoreDeviceVolumePresets re-applies each saved app's desiredVolume/isMuted/volumeBoost to both the session and the backend, then persists the session (AppStore.swift:802-804, 848-867).
• Settings exposes a toggle for the feature; turning it off stops recording and restoring per-device presets (SettingsView.swift:105-114).
• First launch with no file returns an empty DeviceVolumePresets; a file >10MB or failing to decode is backed up to .corrupt and empty presets are used (DeviceVolumePresetsStore.swift:29-67).
• AppVolumeSettings clamps volumeBoost to [1,4] and decodes missing fields with defaults (UserPreferences.swift:64-95).
Acceptance Criteria:
• With the feature on, setting an app to 20% on Speakers and 80% on Headphones, then switching devices, re-applies 20%/80% respectively to that app.
• Per-device settings persist across relaunch (deviceVolumePresets.json present and reloaded).
• Disabling the toggle prevents new per-device recording and prevents restore on device switch.
• A slider drag results in a single file write at commit, not one per intermediate value.
• A corrupt deviceVolumePresets.json is renamed to .corrupt and the feature starts from empty without crashing.
Impl Notes: Functionally wired and persisted, but has real gaps. (1) Unbounded growth / no cleanup: DeviceVolumePresets.removeDevice exists (UserPreferences.swift:111-113) but is NEVER called anywhere in production — entries for unplugged/forgotten devices accumulate forever, and every app the user ever touches on every device is retained indefinitely with no pruning against currently-known apps/devices. (2) Inconsistent save scheduling: setMuted/setVolumeBoost fire deviceVolumePresetsStore.save inside an unstructured Task (AppStore.swift:516-518, 562-564) while commitDesiredVolume saves synchronously through the store's own queue; both are safe (the store serializes on its queue) but the extra Task is redundant. (3) restoreDeviceVolumePresets only restores apps already present in the current session; saved settings for apps not yet running this device-switch are not pre-applied (acceptable but worth noting). (4) Device identity is keyed by device id string, so a device that returns under a different id (some aggregate/Bluetooth cases) will not match its saved settings.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/correctness] Device-switch restore re-taps EXCLUDED apps (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:848-863) — restoreDeviceVolumePresets() (AppStore.swift:848-867) iterates all session apps and applies any saved per-device settings to the backend (setDesiredVolume/setMuted/setVolumeBoost, lines 856-859) with NO isExcluded guard — unlike reapplyRestoredAudioState which guards at line 826. While new recordings are blocked for excluded apps (the setDesiredVolume/setMuted/setVolumeBoost entry points all guard on !isExcluded), settings recorded BEFORE an app was excluded remain in deviceVolumePresets: setExcluded() (AppStore.swift:686-713) does NOT prune that app's per-device entries. On a later output-device switch, those stale settings cause Waves to re-tap an app the user explicitly excluded — exactly the DAW/VoIP escape-hatch that must never be touched. | Fix: Add `guard !isExcluded(app) else { continue }` at the top of the loop body in restoreDeviceVolumePresets, and prune the app's per-device entries in setExcluded().
• [LOW/dead-code] DeviceVolumePresets.removeDevice is dead code; presets grow unbounded with no pruning (Sources/Waves/Stores/UserPreferences.swift:111-113) — DeviceVolumePresets.removeDevice(_:) (UserPreferences.swift:111-113) is never called anywhere in production (grep confirms the only 'removeDevice…' hit in source is the unrelated removeDeviceChangeListener). Every device the user has ever used and every app touched on it is retained forever in deviceVolumes; entries for permanently-unplugged devices and uninstalled apps accumulate with no cleanup. Growth is bounded only by the 10MB load cap, beyond which the whole file is backed up to .corrupt and the feature resets to empty. | Fix: Either remove removeDevice as dead code, or wire periodic pruning (drop device entries not seen in availableDevices and app entries whose logicalID no longer appears) and call it on startup/device-change.
• [LOW/correctness] Device-switch restore leaves appliedVolume stale and persists it (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:852-866) — restoreDeviceVolumePresets() updates session.apps[index].desiredVolume/isMuted/volumeBoost (lines 852-854) but never recomputes appliedVolume, and never re-merges the backend snapshot afterward (unlike reapplyRestoredAudioState which sets appliedVolume = isMuted ? 0 : desiredVolume at line 837). It then persistSessionSnapshot() at line 866, writing the stale appliedVolume to disk. After a device switch the row's effective/applied level can be inconsistent with the restored desiredVolume/mute until the next backend refresh. | Fix: After updating the three fields, set `session.apps[index].appliedVolume = settings.isMuted ? 0 : settings.desiredVolume` (and routingState as appropriate), or re-merge from the backend snapshot before persisting.
• [LOW/correctness] Applying a preset does not update per-device presets, so a later device switch reverts the preset (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1001-1029) — applyPreset() (AppStore.swift:1001-1029) changes session volumes/mutes via the backend and persists the session, but never records the resulting per-app values into deviceVolumePresets for the current device. The per-device recording only happens in setDesiredVolume/setMuted/setVolumeBoost (AppStore.swift:371-381, 508-519, 555-565). With enablePerDeviceVolumePresets on, after applying a preset the deviceVolumePresets for the current device still hold the OLD values, so the next time the user switches output devices and back, restoreDeviceVolumePresets (lines 848-867) re-applies the stale pre-preset values, silently undoing the applied preset. | Fix: After a successful applyPreset, when enablePerDeviceVolumePresets is on, write each affected app's resulting desiredVolume/isMuted/volumeBoost into deviceVolumePresets for currentDeviceID and save.
• [LOW/concurrency] Each device-change event spawns an independent Task; rapid switches can interleave restore (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:792-818) — observeDeviceChanges()'s `for await` loop (AppStore.swift:784-789) calls handleDeviceChange() synchronously, which itself launches an unstructured Task (lines 795-817) and returns immediately, so the loop proceeds to the next event without awaiting completion. There is no stored handle, cancellation, or serialization. previousDeviceID is captured from `session` at each Task's start (line 798); under rapid device changes two Tasks can run concurrently, each awaiting backend snapshots and per-device restore, leading to interleaved writes to session.apps and a possible restore against the wrong/previous device or a double-restore. | Fix: Serialize device-change handling (store the Task and cancel/await the prior one, or confine to a single queue) so only one restore runs at a time and stale ones are cancelled.
• [LOW/correctness] AppVolumeSettings does not clamp desiredVolume to [0,1] on decode (Sources/Waves/Stores/UserPreferences.swift:69-73) — AppVolumeSettings.init clamps volumeBoost to [1,4] (UserPreferences.swift:72) but applies no clamp to desiredVolume (lines 69-73), and the decode init passes desiredVolume straight through (lines 83-87). A malformed/edited deviceVolumePresets.json with desiredVolume outside [0,1] is loaded as-is and, on the next device switch, restoreDeviceVolumePresets passes it straight to backend.setDesiredVolume and into session.apps[index].desiredVolume. PresetEntry and AudioApp both clamp volume to [0,1], making this an inconsistent defensive gap. | Fix: Clamp desiredVolume to [0,1] in AppVolumeSettings.init alongside the existing volumeBoost clamp.

Area: Persistence (Session / Preferences / Presets / Device volume presets)
Feature: Versioned on-disk schema with legacy and future-version handling
User Story: As a Waves user, I want my saved data to keep loading correctly after app updates and to never be wiped just because the file format evolved, so that updates and downgrades do not silently destroy my presets, settings, or saved mix.
Expected Behavior:
• All four stores encode their payload inside a VersionedPayload envelope carrying schemaVersion = PersistedSchema.current (1) via PersistedSchema.encode (PersistedSchema.swift:16-19; used in every store's save).
• decode accepts both the new versioned envelope and a legacy bare/unversioned payload written before envelopes existed (treated as schema 0), so old files still load (PersistedSchema.swift:23-44).
• A file whose schemaVersion is greater than the build's current is rejected with a dataCorrupted error, so a newer build's file is not loaded by an older build (PersistedSchema.swift:32-39); the calling store then backs the file up (except SessionStore) and uses defaults.
• migrate is a hook keyed on version for future non-additive format changes; v1 needs no migration and returns the payload unchanged (PersistedSchema.swift:46-50).
• All stores resolve their file under Application Support/Waves, falling back to ~/.Waves if the support directory is unavailable, creating intermediate directories (e.g. SessionStore.swift:13-28).
Acceptance Criteria:
• A freshly written file contains top-level keys schemaVersion and payload (verified by PersistedSchemaTests.encodedPayloadCarriesSchemaVersion).
• A legacy unversioned object or array still decodes (verified by decodeAcceptsLegacyUnversionedFile / decodeAcceptsLegacyUnversionedArray).
• A file with schemaVersion 2 fails to decode under a build whose current is 1, triggering the store's corrupt-backup-and-default path (preferences/presets/device presets) rather than a load of unknown data.
• If Application Support is unavailable, stores create and use ~/.Waves without crashing.
• Round-tripping preferences through encode/decode preserves field values (verified by versionedEnvelopeRoundTrips).
Impl Notes: Well-designed and unit-tested envelope with deliberate future-version rejection and legacy acceptance. Concern: the future-version rejection only protects the user when the calling store backs up the rejected file; SessionStore does not back up on decode failure (SessionStore.swift:43-46), so a session.json written by a newer build is silently discarded and overwritten by the older build on next save. The migrate() function is currently an identity no-op (correct for v1) but is untested for any actual migration path since none exist yet.
Errors Found: • [LOW/error-handling] Future-version/corrupt session.json bypasses the documented corrupt-backup path and is silently overwritten (Sources/Waves/Services/Persistence/SessionStore.swift:43-46) — PERSIST-06's design rejects any file whose schemaVersion exceeds the build's current with a dataCorrupted error (PersistedSchema), and the story's acceptance criterion states the calling store then takes 'the store's corrupt-backup-and-default path'. This holds for PreferencesStore, PresetStore, and DeviceVolumePresetsStore (each calls backupCorruptFile in its catch), but NOT for SessionStore: load() (SessionStore.swift:43-46) just logs and returns nil with no backup, so a session.json written by a newer build (downgrade) — or any corrupt session — is silently discarded and overwritten by the next save (SessionStore.swift:91). The versioned-schema safety net the story promises is absent for the session store specifically. (Same root cause as the PERSIST-01 backup finding, recorded here because PERSIST-06 owns the future-version/downgrade contract for all four stores.) | Fix: Add backupCorruptFile() to SessionStore.load()'s catch (and the size-limit branch) so the session store matches the other three stores' future-version/corruption handling.

Feature: Transient toast banners for action feedback
User Story: As a Waves user, I want a brief on-screen banner whenever I change something in the mixer, so that I get immediate confirmation that my action took effect (or didn't) without leaving the window.
Expected Behavior:
• showToast(title:detail:kind:duration:) constructs an AppToast and appends it to store.toasts, which the AppToastStack renders (AppStore.swift:1442-1456, AppToasts.swift:6-15).
• Nearly every store action emits a success/info/error toast: volume commit ("Managed route active", AppStore.swift:433), mute/unmute ("App muted"/"App unmuted", 528-533), boost ("Boost updated", 574-579), pin/unpin ("Pinned"/"Unpinned", 599-604), output routing ("Output set", 662-667), preset apply/save/delete/import/export (1015-1110), device switch ("Output switched", 767), diagnostics copy ("Diagnostics copied", 778), route recovery (1189-1199), and startup/refresh (220, 249).
• Each banner shows the toast.title (bold, 1 line) and optional toast.detail (secondary, up to 2 lines) (AppToasts.swift:32-44).
• Banners use a thick Material background, rounded 14pt corners, hairline border, drop shadow, and slide-in-from-top + fade transition (AppToasts.swift:63-77).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Performing any mixer action that calls showToast adds exactly one banner to the stack with the supplied title/detail.
• A toast with a nil or empty detail renders only the title row (no empty secondary line).
• Detail text longer than two lines is truncated to two lines; title longer than one line is truncated to one.
• The banner animates in from the top edge with a spring transition.
Impl Notes: Core feedback path is complete and wired across essentially all store mutations. One UX concern: the success toast for every committed volume change ("Managed route active") fires per app on each commit, so dragging multiple sliders quickly produces a rapid burst of low-value success toasts that can crowd out more important messages (see TOAST-04).
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/correctness] Keyboard volume/mute nudge on an EXCLUDED frontmost app shows a false 'Volume increased/Muted' toast though nothing changed (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1218-1226) — increaseVolumeForFrontmostApp/decreaseVolumeForFrontmostApp/toggleMuteForFrontmostApp (AppStore.swift:1210-1262) call setDesiredVolume/setMuted and then emit the confirmation toast UNCONDITIONALLY. But setDesiredVolume (AppStore.swift:357) and setMuted (AppStore.swift:492) early-return for excluded apps. There is no isExcluded() guard in the keyboard handlers (verified: no guard between 1212-1226 / 1232-1245 / 1250-1261). So pressing the global volume hotkey while an excluded app is frontmost shows e.g. 'Volume increased — App: 60%' (a value computed locally at 1217/1223 from app.desiredVolume, which never updated) while the backend was never touched. This is the same false-success class as TOAST-07 but via the keyboard path, and it directly violates the TOAST-01 user goal ('confirmation that my action took effect'). Note checkAutoPauseMusic correctly skips excluded apps and emits no toast, so only the manual keyboard path is affected. | Fix: Add `guard !isExcluded(app) else { return }` (or surface a warning toast) at the top of increaseVolumeForFrontmostApp/decreaseVolumeForFrontmostApp/toggleMuteForFrontmostApp before showing the toast, mirroring the guards already in setDesiredVolume/setMuted.
• [LOW/dead-code] Per-banner .animation(value: toast.id) never fires; insertion is driven only by the stack (Sources/Waves/Shared/UI/AppToasts.swift:77) — AppToastBanner applies .animation(.spring(response: 0.24, dampingFraction: 0.8), value: toast.id) (AppToasts.swift:77). toast.id is a `let id = UUID()` set once at construction (AppStore.swift:16) and never mutates for a live banner, so a value-keyed animation modifier on the same view instance can never trigger. The visible slide-in comes from the .transition (AppToasts.swift:73-76) plus the parent AppToastStack's .animation(value: store.toasts) (AppToasts.swift:14). Re-read confirms the per-banner modifier is unreachable/no-op. Acceptance criterion 'animates in from the top edge with a spring transition' is satisfied by the stack-level animation, so behavior is correct; the per-banner modifier is dead. | Fix: Remove the .animation(..., value: toast.id) from AppToastBanner (line 77) and rely on the stack-level animation, or key on a value that actually changes if a per-banner spring is desired.

Feature: Kind-based styling (success / warning / error / info)
User Story: As a Waves user, I want toasts to look different depending on whether they report success, a warning, an error, or neutral info, so that I can tell at a glance how urgent a message is.
Expected Behavior:
• AppToast.Kind has four cases: success, warning, error, info (AppStore.swift:8-14).
• Each kind maps to an SF Symbol: success=checkmark.circle.fill, warning=exclamationmark.triangle.fill, error=xmark.octagon.fill, info=info.circle.fill (AppToasts.swift:97-108).
• Each kind maps to an icon color: success=green, warning=orange, error=red, info=WavesDesign.accent (AppToasts.swift:110-121).
• The icon sits in a tinted circle (iconColor at 16% opacity) at the leading edge (AppToasts.swift:25-30).
Acceptance Criteria:
• A .error toast renders a red xmark.octagon.fill icon; a .success toast renders a green checkmark.circle.fill icon.
• The icon background circle tint matches the icon color at reduced opacity for all four kinds.
• Changing the kind passed to showToast changes both the symbol and the color accordingly.
Impl Notes: Styling is fully wired and distinct per kind. Color is the only differentiator between success/warning/error beyond the symbol; users with color-vision deficiency still get distinct symbol shapes, which is good. No text-level severity prefix in the accessibility label, so screen-reader users don't hear the kind (e.g. an error vs. success is announced with identical phrasing) — minor a11y gap.
Errors Found: • [LOW/accessibility] Kind (success/warning/error/info) is conveyed by color + symbol only; never exposed to VoiceOver (Sources/Waves/Shared/UI/AppToasts.swift:90-95) — Re-read confirms toast.kind drives only the SF Symbol (AppToasts.swift:97-108) and the icon color (AppToasts.swift:110-121). accessibilityMessage (AppToasts.swift:90-95) is title + optional detail with no severity word, and .accessibilityElement(children: .combine) (AppToasts.swift:78) absorbs the icon so its symbol carries no accessibility text. A VoiceOver user hears an .error and a .success toast with identical phrasing, defeating the story's 'tell at a glance how urgent a message is'. This matches the doc's own implNotes for TOAST-02 ('No text-level severity prefix in the accessibility label'). | Fix: Prefix accessibilityMessage / the announcement with a kind word for .error/.warning (e.g. 'Error. ' / 'Warning. ') so spoken output encodes severity.

Feature: Auto-dismiss after a per-toast duration
User Story: As a Waves user, I want toasts to disappear on their own after a short time, so that confirmation messages don't pile up or block the mixer.
Expected Behavior:
• Each toast carries a Duration; showToast defaults to defaultToastDuration = 2.0s when no duration is passed (AppStore.swift:59, 1452).
• On creation a per-toast Task sleeps for toast.duration then calls dismissToast(id:) (AppStore.swift:1458-1468).
• Callers override the duration for specific messages, e.g. volume commit 1.2s (AppStore.swift:437), mute 1.1s (532), keyboard-shortcut nudges 1.0s (1226), startup failure 3.2s (223), preset export/import 2.0s (1103/1172).
• dismissToast cancels the pending dismissal Task and removes the toast from the array (AppStore.swift:1471-1475).
Acceptance Criteria:
• A toast created with the default duration disappears ~2.0s after appearing.
• A toast created with an explicit duration disappears after that duration, not the default.
• Manually dismissing a toast before its timer fires cancels the timer (no double-removal / no crash).
• If the same toast id is somehow shown again, the prior dismissal task is cancelled first (toastDismissals[id]?.cancel() at 1458).
Impl Notes: Timer lifecycle is correct and cancellation-safe. UX concern: error toasts surfacing error.localizedDescription mostly use the 2.0s default (e.g. mute/boost/route/preset failures), which is short for reading a full error string; only startup failure gets 3.2s. Errors are also not visually persistent — they auto-dismiss like any success, so a user who looks away can miss a failure entirely.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/ux] Error toasts auto-dismiss on the same short ~2.0s timer as routine successes; failures can vanish before being read (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1452) — Re-read confirms only 'Startup failed' passes an explicit longer duration (3.2s, AppStore.swift:223). Every other error path falls back to defaultToastDuration = 2.0s (AppStore.swift:59, 1452): 'Refresh failed' (251), 'Volume change failed' (446-450, default), 'Mute toggle failed' (539), 'Boost update failed' (585), "Couldn't route" (671), "Couldn't switch output" (769), 'Preset apply failed' (1026), 'Pinning failed' (606), 'Login item update failed' (183), and the import/export failures. Many of these surface error.localizedDescription. 2.0s is too short to read a full error string, and error toasts are not visually persistent, so a user who glances away misses the failure entirely. This undermines the TOAST-01/03 'or didn't' feedback guarantee and matches the doc's TOAST-03 implNotes. | Fix: In showToast, branch on kind: give .error (and ideally .warning) a longer default (4-5s) or make them sticky-until-dismissed.
• [LOW/dead-code] toastDismissals[toast.id]?.cancel() before scheduling is unreachable (ids are always unique) (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1458) — Re-read confirms showToast constructs a brand-new AppToast each call (AppStore.swift:1448-1453) whose id is a fresh UUID (AppStore.swift:16), then calls toastDismissals[toast.id]?.cancel() at 1458 before inserting the dismissal task. Because the id is guaranteed unique and not yet a key in toastDismissals, the cancel is always a no-op. Acceptance criterion #4 ('if the same toast id is somehow shown again, the prior dismissal task is cancelled first') describes a path that cannot occur given the construction. Harmless defensive code, but dead. | Fix: Drop line 1458 as unreachable, or make the toast id caller-supplied/stable so the guard becomes meaningful, or document it as an intentional defensive no-op.

Feature: Stack cap of 3 with oldest-first eviction
User Story: As a Waves user, I want only a few toasts visible at once so a burst of changes doesn't bury the window, so that the mixer stays usable during rapid edits.
Expected Behavior:
• maxToasts = 3; after each append, trimToasts() removes from the front while toasts.count > 3 (AppStore.swift:58, 1456, 1477-1483).
• Evicted toasts have their pending dismissal Task cancelled and removed from toastDismissals (AppStore.swift:1479-1481).
• The stack renders toasts top-to-bottom in a VStack with 10pt spacing and a spring layout animation keyed on store.toasts (AppToasts.swift:7-14).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Emitting 5 toasts in quick succession leaves exactly 3 banners visible.
• The 3 visible banners are the 3 most recent; the 2 oldest are gone.
• Evicting a toast does not leave an orphaned dismissal task (no later removal of an id that is already gone).
• The stack width is capped (maxWidth 420 in stack / 360-ish at mount sites) and aligned to the top.
Impl Notes: Cap and timer cleanup are correct, but eviction is purely chronological (removeFirst) with no priority for kind. A burst of success toasts (e.g. committing volume across several apps, or auto-pause/auto-resume cycles) can silently evict a pending .error toast before the user reads it. Since errors and successes share the same queue and the same short auto-dismiss, important failures can be pushed out by routine confirmations. Worth considering: never evict an error in favor of a success, or give errors a longer/sticky lifetime.
Errors Found: • [MEDIUM/logic] Oldest-first eviction is kind-blind: a pending .error can be evicted by routine .success/.info toasts (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:1478-1479) — Re-read confirms trimToasts removes strictly from the front while count > maxToasts(3), ignoring kind (AppStore.swift:1477-1483). Real burst sources verified: per-app volume commits each emit 'Managed route active' (433-438) on every commitDesiredVolume, and rapid keyboard nudges emit 'Volume increased/decreased' (1221/1240) — three of these in quick succession push an earlier .error (e.g. 'Volume change failed', 446) out of the 3-slot stack before its ~2s timer fires and before the user reads it. (Note: applyPreset emits only ONE 'Preset applied' toast at 1016, not a burst — so the first tester's mention of preset apply as a burst source is inaccurate, but the volume-commit and keyboard-nudge sources are correct and sufficient.) Errors and successes share one queue with the same short lifetime, so important failures are silently displaced. Matches the doc's TOAST-04 implNotes (implStatus 'partial'). | Fix: Make trimToasts prefer evicting non-error toasts: when over cap, remove the oldest toast whose kind != .error first; evict an error only if no lower-priority toast remains. Optionally give errors a longer/sticky lifetime.

Feature: Manual dismissal (close button + accessibility action)
User Story: As a Waves user, I want to dismiss a toast immediately, so that I can clear a message I've already read without waiting for it to time out.
Expected Behavior:
• Each banner has an xmark button that calls store.dismissToast(id: toast.id) (AppToasts.swift:48-58).
• The button has help text "Dismiss" and accessibilityLabel "Dismiss notification" (AppToasts.swift:57-58).
• Because the banner combines its children for accessibility, dismissal is also exposed as a named VoiceOver custom action "Dismiss" (AppToasts.swift:81-83).
Acceptance Criteria:
• Clicking the xmark removes that specific toast and cancels its auto-dismiss timer.
• The VoiceOver "Dismiss" custom action removes the focused toast.
• Dismissing one toast leaves the others in the stack untouched and correctly re-laid-out.
• Removal uses an opacity transition (asymmetric removal) rather than the slide-in used for insertion.
Impl Notes: Close button and VoiceOver action both route to dismissToast and work. Minor UX gap: contentShape(Rectangle()) is set on the whole banner (AppToasts.swift:72) implying tap-to-dismiss was intended, but no tap gesture is attached to the body — only the small xmark is interactive, which is a tiny hit target. Tapping the banner body does nothing.
Errors Found: • [LOW/ux] contentShape(Rectangle()) on the banner implies tap-to-dismiss but no tap gesture is attached (Sources/Waves/Shared/UI/AppToasts.swift:72) — Re-read confirms the banner sets .contentShape(Rectangle()) (AppToasts.swift:72) but no .onTapGesture / gesture is attached to the banner body (checked the full AppToastBanner and AppToastStack). Only the xmark Button (AppToasts.swift:48-58, padding 4) is interactive. So tapping the banner body does nothing and the contentShape is a no-op for dismissal; the only manual-dismiss hit target is the tiny xmark glyph, small for a transient element. Matches the doc's TOAST-05 implNotes. | Fix: Either remove the orphan .contentShape(Rectangle()), or add .onTapGesture { store.dismissToast(id: toast.id) } to the banner body to make tap-anywhere-to-dismiss real and enlarge the hit target.

Feature: VoiceOver announcement of transient toasts
User Story: As a VoiceOver user, I want toasts to be spoken when they appear, so that I get the same audio/visual feedback sighted users get even though banners are transient.
Expected Behavior:
• On banner onAppear, an AccessibilityNotification.Announcement is posted with the combined message (AppToasts.swift:84-87).
• accessibilityMessage joins title and detail as "<title>. <detail>" when detail is non-empty, otherwise just the title (AppToasts.swift:90-95).
• The banner is an accessibility element with children combined and the same message as its label (AppToasts.swift:78-79).
Acceptance Criteria:
• When a toast appears, VoiceOver announces its title (and detail, if present).
• A toast with no detail announces only the title.
• The combined banner exposes a single accessibility element labeled with the toast message, not its individual subviews.
Impl Notes: Announcement is correctly posted because VoiceOver does not auto-announce transient banners (comment at AppToasts.swift:85). Gaps: (1) the announcement has no priority/severity, so an error and a success are announced identically; (2) up to 3 toasts can appear near-simultaneously (e.g. auto-pause muting several music apps, or a preset apply), and each onAppear posts its own Announcement — later announcements can interrupt earlier ones, so a VoiceOver user may not hear all of them; (3) eviction (TOAST-04) can remove a toast before its announcement is fully spoken.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/accessibility] Simultaneous toast announcements have no priority and can interrupt/replace each other (Sources/Waves/Shared/UI/AppToasts.swift:86) — Re-read confirms each banner onAppear fires AccessibilityNotification.Announcement(accessibilityMessage).post() (AppToasts.swift:84-87) with no priority attribute. When multiple toasts insert near-simultaneously, each posts its own unprioritized announcement and VoiceOver coalesces/interrupts them, so earlier ones (which may be an error) can be cut off.
