Combined with TOAST-04 eviction, a toast can be removed before its announcement completes. CORRECTION to the first tester: the cited 'auto-pause muting several music apps' is NOT a real burst source — checkAutoPauseMusic (AppStore.swift:940-998) mutes/unmutes silently and emits NO toast. Likewise applyPreset emits only one toast. The genuine near-simultaneous sources are per-app volume commits ('Managed route active', 433) and rapid keyboard nudges (1221/1240). The criterion 'each appearing toast is announced' is not reliably met under those bursts. | Fix: Attach an announcement priority for higher-severity kinds (AttributedString with .accessibilitySpeechAnnouncementPriority(.high) for .error/.warning) and/or serialize announcements so they queue rather than interrupt; ensure a toast isn't evicted before its announcement is delivered.
• [MEDIUM/accessibility] Each toast is announced (and rendered) twice when the main window and menu-bar panel are both open (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MenuBarMixerView.swift:105) — AppToastStack is mounted in BOTH MainWindowView (line 59) and MenuBarMixerView (line 105), and both read the same shared store.toasts via @Environment(AppStore.self). When both surfaces are on screen at once (the menu-bar popover can be open while the main window is visible), a single showToast appends one AppToast that renders in TWO stacks. Each stack's AppToastBanner.onAppear independently posts AccessibilityNotification.Announcement (AppToasts.swift:84-87), so a VoiceOver user hears the same message announced twice; sighted users see the banner duplicated in both surfaces. This is a menu-bar/main-window state-desync the first tester missed. (Auto-dismiss/eviction stay consistent because there is a single source-of-truth array, so it is purely a duplicate-presentation/double-speak issue, not a crash.) | Fix: Post the VoiceOver announcement from a single owner (e.g. in showToast itself, once, rather than per-banner onAppear), and/or render only one AppToastStack for the key window / gate the menu-bar stack so toasts aren't duplicated across simultaneously-visible surfaces.

Feature: Toasts as the result channel for URL-scheme automation commands
User Story: As a power user driving Waves via waves:// URLs, I want a toast when a command is rejected or malformed, so that I can tell whether my automation actually did anything.
Expected Behavior:
• Invalid set-volume, mute, and apply-preset commands surface a .warning toast titled "URL command blocked" with a reason (AppStore.swift:299,304,318,323,333,338).
• Unknown app/preset names produce "URL command blocked" with the (truncated) identifier (AppStore.swift:304,338).
• Valid commands fall through to the normal action handlers, which then emit their own success/error toasts (e.g. set-volume -> commit -> "Managed route active").
• Note: when URL schemes are disabled or the rate limit is exceeded, the request is only logged (logger.warning) and produces NO toast (AppStore.swift:256-260, 270-273).
Acceptance Criteria:
• A waves://set-volume with a missing/out-of-range volume shows a "URL command blocked" warning toast and changes nothing.
• A waves://mute targeting a non-running app shows "App not found: <id>" as a warning toast.
• A valid waves://apply-preset?name=... shows the normal "Preset applied" success toast.
• A command received while URL schemes are disabled or rate-limited produces no toast (silent, log-only).
Impl Notes: Malformed/unknown-target commands give clear warning toasts, which is good. The gap is the silent paths: a command dropped because URL schemes are disabled or because the per-minute rate limit (10/60s) is exceeded gives the user zero in-app feedback — only an os_log entry the user won't see. From the automation author's perspective these failures are indistinguishable from success. A muted/info toast on rate-limit or disabled-scheme rejection would close the loop.
Errors Found: • [MEDIUM/correctness] URL set-volume on an excluded app emits a misleading 'Managed route active' success toast (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:308-309) — Re-read confirms handleURLSetVolume (AppStore.swift:291-310) calls setDesiredVolume(volume, for: app) then commitDesiredVolume(for: app) with no isExcluded check. setDesiredVolume guards on exclusion and returns early (357), BUT commitDesiredVolume (384-393) and scheduleVolumeApply (395-462) have NO exclusion guard (verified: the only isExcluded guards are at 357/492/545/650/826). So for an excluded app, commitDesiredVolume still seeds pendingVolumeTargets[appKey] = app.desiredVolume (the unchanged value, since setDesiredVolume no-op'd), calls backend.setDesiredVolume on success, and emits the 'Managed route active' success toast (433-438). The automation author gets a false success on an app that is on the don't-tap escape hatch, and the backend is asked to act on it. | Fix: Guard handleURLSetVolume on isExcluded(app) (emit a warning toast and return), or add an isExcluded early-return to commitDesiredVolume/scheduleVolumeApply so excluded apps never commit, never call the backend, and never toast.

Area: Accessibility (VoiceOver, contrast, reduced motion, label scaling)
Feature: VoiceOver labels, values, and hints on controls
User Story: As a VoiceOver user, I want every mixer control and icon-only button to announce a clear label, current value, and hint, so that I can operate the per-app mixer without seeing the screen.
Expected Behavior:
• Each per-app volume slider announces 'Volume for <app>', a value of '<n>%', and the hint 'Adjusts the per-app volume target' (full and compact rows).
• The mute button announces 'Mute <app>' or 'Unmute <app>' depending on state; compact row adds a hint ('Silences this app' / 'Restores audio for this app').
• The boost control announces 'Boost for <app>' with value '<n>x'.
• Toolbar icon buttons (Save preset / Refresh app list / Recover managed routes) each expose an accessibilityLabel plus a descriptive hint.
• Menu-bar icon buttons announce labels: 'Refresh app list', 'Open Waves main window', 'Open Settings', 'Launch at login', and the output-device menu announces label 'Output device' with the current device name as its value.
• Decorative elements (app icon, % readout text, level-meter capsule, status dot circle) are marked accessibilityHidden so VoiceOver does not read redundant or non-actionable content.
• Pinned pin glyph and 'Excluded' chip carry labels ('Pinned', 'Excluded from Waves').
• Route-health badge and diagnostics check rows expose combined labels including their status word (e.g. 'Passed: <title>').
Acceptance Criteria:
• With VoiceOver on, focusing any volume slider speaks an app-specific label and a percentage value.
• Focusing the mute button speaks Mute/Unmute matching the app's current muted state.
• Every icon-only button in the toolbar and menu bar is reachable and named (no 'button' with empty name).
• The app icon, numeric % label, and meter are skipped by VoiceOver navigation.
• The output-device picker speaks 'Output device' and the active device name.
Impl Notes: Coverage is thorough and consistent: icon-only controls all have labels, and decorative content is correctly hidden. Minor gap: the full MixerRowView mute button has no hint (the compact row does), and the PresetQuickPicker menu-bar label ('Presets') has no custom a11y label but is text-based so still readable. Boost is a Menu so its accessibilityValue may not be announced as an adjustable value — it reads as a popup, which is acceptable.
Errors Found: • [LOW/correctness] Volume percentage truncates instead of rounds, so VoiceOver value and the visible % readout can read one point low (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MixerRowView.swift:76) — Both the full and compact slider expose accessibilityValue as "\(Int(app.desiredVolume * 100))%" (lines 76 and 303), and the full row also renders a visible Text("\(Int(app.desiredVolume * 100))%") at line 83. desiredVolume is a Float that accumulates rounding error from 0.05 steps, so values like 0.39999998 or 0.79999995 stringify to '39%' / '79%' instead of '40%' / '80%'. I reproduced this in Swift: after stepping, Int(0.39999998*100)=39 while .rounded() gives 40. The defect is genuine but intermittent (only non-exact accumulated values are affected; clean values like 0.95, 0.90 read correctly), so it is low severity, not medium. The visible label is affected the same way the VoiceOver value is. | Fix: Use Int((app.desiredVolume * 100).rounded()) for the accessibilityValue at lines 76 and 303 and the visible label at line 83.

Area: Accessibility (VoiceOver, contrast, reduced motion, label scaling)
Feature: Adjustable sliders via VoiceOver increment/decrement
User Story: As a VoiceOver user, I want to change an app's volume with the up/down rotor gestures, so that I can set levels precisely without dragging a slider I cannot see.
Expected Behavior:
• Each volume slider declares an accessibilityAdjustableAction.
• Increment raises desiredVolume by 0.05 clamped to a max of 1.0; decrement lowers it by 0.05 clamped to a min of 0.0.
• Each adjustment calls store.setDesiredVolume and immediately store.commitDesiredVolume so the change is applied and persisted, not left pending.
• Unknown adjustment directions are ignored (no crash, no change).
• Both the full MixerRowView and the CompactMixerRow implement identical 5% step behavior.
Acceptance Criteria:
• Swiping up on a focused slider increases the announced percentage by 5 points until 100%.
• Swiping down decreases it by 5 points until 0%.
• After an adjust gesture the value is committed (survives a refresh / is reflected in the backend), not just visually nudged.
• Adjusting an excluded app's slider has no effect because the slider is disabled.
Impl Notes: Clean and correct: 5% steps, clamped, committed on each step. The disabled() on excluded rows correctly blocks the adjustable action too. Step size (5%) matches the keyboard nudge in MainWindowView, so behavior is consistent across input methods.
Errors Found: • [LOW/logic] VoiceOver decrement announces occasional off-by-one percentages because the percent is truncated, not rounded (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MixerRowView.swift:303) — adjustVolume() steps desiredVolume by exactly 0.05 and clamps correctly (the step logic is right). The defect is in displaying the value: Int(desiredVolume*100) truncates. I verified the actual Float32 sequences in Swift and the first tester's reproduction is partly INACCURATE. Decrement from 0.5 announces 50, 45, 39, 34, 29, 24 (NOT '50 -> 44 -> 39' — the first step is a clean 45, then 39 appears where 40 is expected). Decrement from 1.0 announces 100, 95, 90, 85, 79 (NOT '100 -> 94 -> 89 -> 84' — the first four are clean, the error first shows at 79 instead of 80). So the bug is real but it is an occasional 1-point-low announcement, not a systematic 6-point-then-1-low pattern, and not every step is wrong. Same root cause as A11Y-01; low severity. | Fix: Round in the integer conversion: Int((app.desiredVolume * 100).rounded()) at lines 76, 83, and 303. Optionally quantize desiredVolume in adjustVolume() (e.g. ((value*20).rounded())/20) to keep the stored value on exact 5% marks.

Area: Accessibility (VoiceOver, contrast, reduced motion, label scaling)
Feature: Custom VoiceOver actions for combined/hidden controls
User Story: As a VoiceOver user, I want pin, exclude, and toast-dismiss available as VoiceOver actions, so that I can reach controls that live in context menus or are absorbed by element grouping.
Expected Behavior:
• Each mixer row exposes accessibilityAction 'Pin'/'Unpin' (state-dependent) that toggles pinned state via store.togglePinned.
• Each mixer row exposes accessibilityAction 'Exclude from Waves'/'Manage with Waves' (state-dependent) that calls store.setExcluded.
• Toast banners combine children into one element and re-expose the dismiss control as an accessibilityAction named 'Dismiss' (because .combine absorbs the dismiss button), wired to store.dismissToast.
• These actions mirror the same operations available via the row context menu, so mouse and VoiceOver users have parity.
Acceptance Criteria:
• With VoiceOver on a mixer row, the actions rotor lists Pin/Unpin and Exclude/Manage, and invoking them changes the app's state.
• On a toast, the actions rotor lists 'Dismiss' and invoking it removes the toast.
• Action names update to reflect current state (e.g. shows 'Unpin' once an app is pinned).
Impl Notes: Good pattern — the comment in AppToasts explicitly notes that .combine absorbs the dismiss button, so the action is the correct workaround. The row's Output Device submenu from the context menu is NOT re-exposed as a VoiceOver action, so device routing per app is unreachable via VoiceOver custom actions (only pin/exclude are). That is a real parity gap worth noting.
Errors Found: • [LOW/inconsistency] Per-app Output Device submenu has no VoiceOver custom-action parity (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MixerRowView.swift:140) — Verified: the row context menu (lines 135-162) offers Pin/Unpin, an Output Device submenu, and Exclude/Manage, but only Pin/Unpin and Exclude/Manage are re-exposed via .accessibilityAction (lines 163-168). The Output Device submenu is not exposed as a VoiceOver action, so a VoiceOver user relying on the actions rotor cannot reach per-app output routing — a flagship feature that mouse users get from the same menu. The story's enumerated acceptance criteria only list pin/exclude/dismiss (all satisfied), so this is a parity gap against the story's stated intent rather than a hard criterion failure; the story's own implNotes already calls it out. Low severity. | Fix: Add accessibilityAction(s) for output routing, e.g. a 'Use System Default Output' action plus per-device actions, mirroring the context-menu submenu.

Area: Accessibility (VoiceOver, contrast, reduced motion, label scaling)
Feature: VoiceOver rotors for playing apps and apps needing attention
User Story: As a VoiceOver user, I want rotors that jump straight to apps currently playing audio or apps in an error state, so that I can navigate a long app list efficiently.
Expected Behavior:
• The main window source list declares an accessibilityRotor 'Playing apps' populated from the apps that are also in store.liveApps.
• It declares a second accessibilityRotor 'Needs attention' populated from apps whose routingState == .error.
• Each rotor entry uses the app's displayName and the app id, so selecting an entry moves focus to that row.
• Rotors are scoped to the currently filtered/visible apps list.
Acceptance Criteria:
• With VoiceOver, the rotor control lists a 'Playing apps' category containing only live apps.
• A 'Needs attention' category lists only apps with a failed route.
• Selecting a rotor entry moves VoiceOver focus to the corresponding mixer row.
• When no apps match (none playing / none errored) the rotor category is empty rather than incorrect.
Impl Notes: Wired and scoped correctly to the visible list. Rotors are only present in the main window's non-empty list branch (not in the menu-bar panel), which is reasonable. Empty rotors are valid SwiftUI. No correctness concern; matches the README claim of 'VoiceOver rotors'.

Area: Accessibility (VoiceOver, contrast, reduced motion, label scaling)
Feature: VoiceOver announcements for transient toast banners
User Story: As a VoiceOver user, I want status toasts to be spoken when they appear, so that I learn about route changes, errors, and confirmations that I would otherwise miss because banners are transient.
Expected Behavior:
• When a toast banner appears, it posts an AccessibilityNotification.Announcement with the toast's accessible message.
• The announced message is the title, or 'title. detail' when a non-empty detail is present.
• The same combined message is used as the banner's accessibilityLabel for users who navigate to it manually.
Acceptance Criteria:
• With VoiceOver on, triggering an action that raises a toast causes the toast text to be spoken without the user navigating to it.
• A toast with detail speaks both title and detail; a title-only toast speaks just the title.
• Navigating to the banner element reads the same combined message.
Impl Notes: Correctly addresses the known issue that VoiceOver does not auto-announce transient banners (comment says so). Edge case: the announcement fires in .onAppear; if multiple toasts insert at once each posts its own announcement which may queue/overlap, and there is no .screenChanged vs .announcement priority tuning, but for occasional status messages this is acceptable.
Errors Found: • [LOW/edge-case] A toast is announced twice when both the main window and menu-bar panel are open (Sources/Waves/Shared/UI/AppToasts.swift:86) — Confirmed: AppToastStack is mounted in exactly two places — MainWindowView.swift:59 and MenuBarMixerView.swift:105 — and both bind to the same store.toasts. Each AppToastBanner posts AccessibilityNotification.Announcement in its own .onAppear (AppToasts.swift:86). When both surfaces are visible at once (menu-bar panel open while the main window is also open), a newly appended toast instantiates a banner in both stacks, so the announcement fires twice and VoiceOver speaks (or queues) the message twice. The single-surface case is correct. Low severity (only manifests with both surfaces simultaneously open). | Fix: Drive the announcement once from the store when a toast is appended (post in showToast on the main actor) instead of from each banner's onAppear; or guard so only the key window's stack owns announcements.

Area: Accessibility (VoiceOver, contrast, reduced motion, label scaling)
Feature: Reduced-motion honoring for animations
User Story: As a user with the Reduce Motion accessibility setting enabled, I want Waves to suppress spring and bounce animations, so that level meters and value changes do not cause motion discomfort.
Expected Behavior:
• Both MixerRowView and CompactMixerRow read the accessibilityReduceMotion environment value.
• The animated numeric % readout uses a spring animation only when reduceMotion is false, otherwise nil (no animation).
• The live level-meter capsule uses a linear 0.18s animation only when reduceMotion is false.
• The mute-button bounce symbol effect (animateMuteChange toggle) is only triggered when reduceMotion is false, so toggling mute does not bounce under Reduce Motion.
Acceptance Criteria:
• With Reduce Motion on, changing a volume value updates the % label without an animated transition.
• With Reduce Motion on, the level meter resizes without an animated tween.
• With Reduce Motion on, toggling mute does not play the bounce symbol effect.
• With Reduce Motion off, all three animations play.
Impl Notes: Honored where it matters most (mixer rows). However reduceMotion is NOT consulted elsewhere: the toast stack spring animations (AppToasts.swift:14, 77), the toast insertion .move transition, and the main-window 'Refreshing' indicator are unconditional. The numeric % still uses .contentTransition(.numericText()) even under reduce motion (only the .animation is gated), so a subtle text morph may persist. So motion reduction is real but incomplete across the app.
Errors Found: • [LOW/accessibility] Toast spring animation and slide-in transition are not gated by Reduce Motion (Sources/Waves/Shared/UI/AppToasts.swift:77) — Confirmed: the three mixer-row animations named in the acceptance criteria ARE correctly gated on reduceMotion (numeric % spring line 90, level-meter linear line 129, mute bounce via animateMuteChange at lines 98/315) and pass. But I grepped the whole Sources tree and reduceMotion / accessibilityReduceMotion is read ONLY in MixerRowView. The toast stack spring (AppToasts.swift:14), the per-banner spring (AppToasts.swift:77), and the .move(edge:.top) insertion transition (AppToasts.swift:74) never consult accessibilityReduceMotion, so a Reduce-Motion user still sees springy banners sliding in from the top. This is a real but narrow gap against the story's stated goal of suppressing spring/move motion app-wide. Low severity. | Fix: Read @Environment(\.accessibilityReduceMotion) in AppToastStack/AppToastBanner; use reduceMotion ? nil : .spring(...) for the value animations (lines 14, 77) and swap the .move insertion for a plain .opacity transition when reduceMotion is true.

Area: Accessibility (VoiceOver, contrast, reduced motion, label scaling)
Feature: Increase Contrast honoring for low-contrast surfaces
User Story: As a user with macOS Increase Contrast enabled, I want faint chips and hairline borders to become clearly visible, so that route-state indicators and toast edges are not invisible to me.
Expected Behavior:
• RoutingStateIndicator reads colorSchemeContrast; when increased, it adds a solid Capsule stroke outline in the state color and raises the chip background opacity to 0.28 (vs 0.08/0.12 normally).
• Toast banners read colorSchemeContrast and switch their border from the 9%-white stroke to WavesDesign.hairline, which returns 45%-white under increased contrast.
• Route-state chips always pair an SF Symbol and a text label (and an accessibilityLabel) with the color, so meaning is conveyed without relying on color alone (managed/live/recent/error each have distinct symbols and words).
Acceptance Criteria:
• With Increase Contrast on, each route-state chip shows a visible outline and a stronger fill.
• With Increase Contrast on, toast banners show a distinctly brighter border than the default hairline.
• Route state is distinguishable by symbol + text even for a user who cannot perceive the color difference.
• With Increase Contrast off, the original subtle styling is preserved.
Impl Notes: The chip and toast treatments are correct and the symbol+text pairing satisfies 'understandable without color alone' (PRODUCT.md). But coverage is narrow: many other faint surfaces (WavesDesign.rail/stroke panels, the 'Refreshing' overlay border at MainWindowView.swift:77, OutputSummary divider, RouteHealthBadge 0.12 fill) do NOT consult contrast and stay low-contrast. Larger concern: the app is hard-pinned to dark mode via NSApp.appearance = .darkAqua (WavesApp.swift:88), so an Increase-Contrast user who also runs Light Appearance never gets a light high-contrast surface — contrast adaptation is dark-only. PRODUCT.md targets WCAG AA broadly, which this only partially meets.
Errors Found: • [LOW/accessibility] Compact menu-bar route indicator conveys state with icon + color only, no visible text label (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MixerRowView.swift:486) — Verified: the full row's RoutingStateIndicator pairs an SF Symbol with a visible Text(state.displayName) (lines 415-423), satisfying 'distinguishable without color'. The compact menu-bar row uses RoutingStateDot (lines 483-493), which renders only a state-colored SF Symbol with no adjacent visible text; the text label exists only as an accessibilityLabel for VoiceOver. Mitigating factor (which keeps this low / partial): the dot's glyphs DO differ per state (checkmark.circle.fill / waveform / checkmark.circle / clock.fill / exclamationmark.triangle.fill), so color is supplementary, not the sole channel, for a user who can perceive shape. It is a partial gap in the compact surface, not a full color-only failure. | Fix: Optionally add a short visible label/abbreviation beside the compact dot, or document that the distinct glyph shapes carry the meaning and color is supplementary.

Area: Accessibility (VoiceOver, contrast, reduced motion, label scaling)
Feature: Numeric label scaling to avoid clipping at large text sizes
User Story: As a user who increases system text size, I want the fixed-width percentage and boost labels to shrink-to-fit rather than truncate, so that I can still read the exact volume and boost values.
Expected Behavior:
• The volume % readout uses lineLimit(1) plus minimumScaleFactor(0.7) inside its fixed 40pt-wide frame, so larger glyphs scale down instead of clipping.
• The boost label uses lineLimit(1) plus minimumScaleFactor(0.7) inside its fixed 34/38pt frame.
• The same minimumScaleFactor(0.7) protects the boost menu label text in both compact and full layouts.
Acceptance Criteria:
• At large accessibility text sizes the '100%' label remains fully readable within its frame (not cut off).
• The boost 'x' label (e.g. '4x') remains readable at large text sizes.
• At default text size the labels render at full size.
Impl Notes: This is a clipping mitigation, not true large-text support. The labels are still in fixed-width frames (40/38/34pt) with a 0.7 floor, so beyond ~1.4x they will scale-floor and could still clip; and the frames themselves do not grow. Critically, macOS does not surface iOS-style Dynamic Type, and no view here reads dynamicTypeSize/sizeCategory or uses @ScaledMetric, so the whole UI does not actually reflow for the system 'larger text' accessibility setting — only these two labels shrink to fit. The commit message calls this 'scale numeric labels at large Dynamic Type sizes,' which overstates the actual effect on macOS.

Feature: Per-device volume-control mode (hardware / software / automatic)
Code Refs:
• Sources/WavesAudioCore/Backend/AudioControlBackend.swift:11 (func setVolumeControlMode(_:forDeviceID:))
• Sources/WavesAudioCore/Models/AudioDevice.swift:55-83 (enum VolumeControlMode hardware/software/automatic with displayName + description)
• Sources/WavesAudioCore/Models/AudioDevice.swift:9 (AudioDevice.volumeControlMode)
• Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:142 (setVolumeControlMode implementation)
• README.md:16 ('Smart Volume Backend: Intelligently switches between hardware and software volume control')
• README.md:163 (Audio Settings: 'Choose the preferred route-control mode for Waves-managed sessions')
Impl Notes: [critic gap] It is a backend/model capability with no remaining UI surface, so a UI-walk of menus/buttons/toggles never reaches it, and the 171 stories were derived from the visible control set. The picker that exposed it was deliberately removed (CHANGELOG: 'Removed — Decorative volume-control-mode picker (it was a no-op)'), but the protocol method, enum, model field, and the README text that promises it were all left behind, so it looks like a feature when scanning code/docs but isn't wired to anything users can do.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/dead-code] Per-device volume-control mode is a no-op: written but never read by any routing/volume path (Sources/Waves/Services/Audio/WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:142) — Verified by reading WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.setVolumeControlMode (line 142-146): it only mutates snapshot.currentDevice?.volumeControlMode when deviceID == current device, performs no Core Audio call, and changes no behavior. A full grep of Sources confirms AudioDevice.volumeControlMode is only ever WRITTEN (the two backend setters at WorkspaceAudioControlBackend.swift:144 and PreviewAudioControlBackend.swift:80, plus the preview initializer in AudioSessionSnapshot+Preview.swift:94 and the AudioDevice init) and is NEVER READ by any routing/volume code. The deciding UI picker was removed as a no-op (CHANGELOG), so no user can invoke setVolumeControlMode at all. The protocol method (AudioControlBackend.swift:11), VolumeControlMode enum with displayName/description (AudioDevice.swift:55-83), and AudioDevice.volumeControlMode field are stranded surface area. Minor inaccuracy in the original report: the field IS technically serialized as part of the Codable AudioDevice in the session snapshot, but the write is transient (the device-change handler reloads currentSnapshot, resetting it to .software) and nothing reads it, so the dead-code conclusion stands. | Fix: Either fully remove the dead surface (delete setVolumeControlMode from AudioControlBackend and both backends, remove AudioDevice.volumeControlMode and the VolumeControlMode enum, drop the README promises), or re-add a UI control, persist the chosen mode, and make the routing layer actually read volumeControlMode.
• [MEDIUM/inconsistency] README advertises a 'Smart Volume Backend' / route-control mode that does not exist for users (README.md:16) — Verified README.md:16 states 'Smart Volume Backend: Intelligently switches between hardware and software volume control' and README.md:163 (Audio Settings section) states 'Choose the preferred route-control mode for Waves-managed sessions'. Both describe the removed/no-op volume-control-mode capability. Confirmed by reading AudioSettingsView (SettingsView.swift:181-208): it contains only the current-output-device label, a managed-routing description, a Recover Routes button, and a boost tip — there is NO route-control-mode control, so README:163 references absent UI. | Fix: Remove the 'Smart Volume Backend' bullet (README:16) and the 'Choose the preferred route-control mode' line (README:163), or re-add them only once the feature is wired to a working UI and routing path.

Feature: Show / hide system audio processes in the app list
Code Refs:
• Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:103-107 (visibleApps filters out .system unless showSystemProcesses)
• Sources/Waves/Stores/UserPreferences.swift:6 (showSystemProcesses default false)
• Sources/Waves/Settings/SettingsView.swift:68-77 (Toggle 'Show system processes')
• Sources/WavesAudioCore/Models/AudioApp.swift:228 (.system category)
Impl Notes: [critic gap] The captured discovery stories cover helper filtering (DISC-03), category inference (DISC-04), and filtered-list states (DISC-06), but none captures the user-controllable inclusion/exclusion of the entire `.system` category from the visible list. It is a distinct list-population behavior driven by a dedicated preference and toggle, only loosely subsumed under the generic 'General preferences toggles' (SET-02).
Errors Found: • [MEDIUM/ux] Empty-state copy is wrong when the only audio apps are hidden system processes (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MainWindowView.swift:234) — Verified: visibleApps filters out .system unless showSystemProcesses (AppStore.swift:104-106), which defaults to false (UserPreferences.swift:6). AppDiscoveryPolicy.inferCategory returns .system for any unmatched com.apple.* bundle ID (AppDiscoveryPolicy.swift:56-57). So if every detected source is .system, the Running scope's scopedApps is empty and SourceListView renders ContentUnavailableView with emptyMessage 'Waves hasn't found any running apps yet.' (MainWindowView.swift:234). That copy is wrong: apps WERE found but filtered out, and nothing tells the user a filter is hiding them. The empty state does include a Settings button (MainWindowView.swift:352-357), which mitigates discoverability slightly, but the message still misattributes the cause. | Fix: When scopedApps is empty but session.apps contains .system entries and showSystemProcesses is false, show a distinct message such as 'System processes are hidden — enable Show system processes in Settings to see them.' Optionally surface a hidden-count badge.

Feature: Recent apps grouping and the 'Show recent apps' toggle
Code Refs:
• Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:124-128 (recentApps computed, gated by showRecentApps)
• Sources/Waves/Stores/UserPreferences.swift:5 (showRecentApps default true)
• Sources/Waves/Settings/SettingsView.swift:58-66 (Toggle 'Show recent apps')
• Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MenuBarMixerView.swift:48-50 (Recent CompactSection)
• Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MainWindowView.swift:110-112,234-241 (Recent sidebar scope + empty message)
Impl Notes: [critic gap] The inventory references 'recent' only obliquely (LoginItem/persistence and menu-bar sections), but there is no story for the Recent grouping itself — the computed set of non-live visible apps surfaced as a dedicated sidebar scope and a menu-bar section, gated by a user preference. It is a separate organizational/list-population behavior from live/pinned grouping (ROUTE-04, PIN-04).
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/inconsistency] Main-window 'Recent' sidebar scope stays visible (and shows a misleading empty state) when 'Show recent apps' is OFF (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MainWindowView.swift:252) — Verified: the menu bar hides the Recent section when showRecentApps is false (MenuBarMixerView.swift:48 guards 'if store.preferences.showRecentApps'). The main window does NOT: SidebarView renders ForEach(SourceFilter.allCases) which always includes .recent (MainWindowView.swift:252), and recentApps returns [] when showRecentApps is false (AppStore.swift:124-128). With the toggle off, the sidebar still shows a 'Recent' row reading '0 recent apps', and selecting it shows 'No Recent Apps' with 'Apps appear here when they're not actively playing audio.' (MainWindowView.swift:240) — attributing emptiness to no recent apps rather than the disabled preference. Inconsistent with the menu bar and misleading. | Fix: Filter SourceFilter.allCases to drop .recent when !store.preferences.showRecentApps (and reset selection to .running if it was .recent), or special-case the Recent empty message to say recent apps are turned off and link to Settings.
• [MEDIUM/ux] 'Recent' scope is a misnomer — it is 'all non-live apps' with no recency ordering (Sources/Waves/Stores/AppStore.swift:124) — Verified: recentApps is visibleApps minus the live set (AppStore.swift:124-128), returned in the user's sortMode order via visibleApps (sortedApps, AppStore.swift:1278-1301). There is no last-active timestamp or recency sort anywhere — confirmed AudioApp/sort code has no recency concept. Yet the scope is labelled 'Recent' with a clock.fill icon (MainWindowView.swift:167,210) and the menu-bar section title is 'Recent' (MenuBarMixerView.swift:49). The label/icon imply time-ordered recently-used apps; the implementation is every visible app not currently playing, including pinned and never-played apps. The feature-status implNotes itself describes it as 'the computed set of non-live visible apps.' | Fix: Either implement real recency (track a last-active timestamp per app and sort/limit by it), or rename the scope to 'Inactive' / 'Not Playing', change the clock icon, and adjust the empty/section copy to match.

Area: Main Window Mixer UI & Empty State
Feature: Last-used sidebar source scope restored across launches
Impl Notes: [critic gap] The main-window stories cover sidebar scopes (WIN-01) and search (WIN-02) but not the persistence of the selected scope. The selected source filter (Running / Pinned / Live / Recent) is saved via @SceneStorage and restored on relaunch so the user returns to the scope they left — a distinct UX-continuity behavior separate from preference/session persistence (PERSIST-01/02).
Errors Found: • [MEDIUM/edge-case] Persisted sidebar scope can be restored into a permanently-empty, misleading state (Recent scope with 'Show recent apps' off) (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MainWindowView.swift:8) — Completeness-pass finding the first tester missed (they marked WIN-08 pass). selection is @SceneStorage("waves.selectedScope") and any SourceFilter — including .recent and .pinned — is persisted and restored across launches (MainWindowView.swift:8). There is no validation that the restored scope is still populatable. If the user leaves the window on the Recent scope and then disables 'Show recent apps', recentApps becomes [] forever (AppStore.swift:124-128) and scopedApps for .recent is always empty (MainWindowView.swift:110-112), so on this and every subsequent launch the window opens to a Recent scope that can never show anything, with the misleading 'No Recent Apps' copy (MainWindowView.swift:240). Unlike the menu bar, nothing drops/hides the .recent scope or resets selection to .running, so the persisted scope is a stuck dead-end. This is the WIN-08 persistence/desync angle distinct from DISC-08's general inconsistency. | Fix: On window appear, validate the restored selection against currently-available scopes and fall back to .running when the scope is disabled (e.g. .recent while !showRecentApps); and drop .recent from SourceFilter.allCases when showRecentApps is off so it can't be selected or restored.

Area: Main Window Mixer UI & Empty State
Feature: Keyboard 'b' to cycle boost and 'p' to pin on the selected mixer row
Impl Notes: [critic gap] WIN-07 captures 'keyboard-driven control and drag-to-reorder' generically and KEY-01..03 cover the global frontmost-app hotkeys, but the in-list row-level key handlers go beyond volume/mute: 'b' cycles boost 1x->4x->1x and 'p' toggles pin on the currently selected row. Pin-via-keyboard and the in-window (non-global) boost-cycle key are not separately captured, and a UI-walk focused on buttons/shortcut-modifiers can miss bare-character onKeyPress handlers.
Errors Found:
• [MEDIUM/ux] In-list 'b' (cycle boost) and 'p' (pin) shortcuts are completely undocumented (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MainWindowView.swift:385) — Verified MainWindowView.swift:381-386 attaches bare-character onKeyPress handlers to the source list: space/m (mute), =/- (nudge volume by 0.05), b (cycleBoost, MainWindowView.swift:422-425), p (togglePinned). Confirmed none appear in the Settings 'Keyboard shortcuts' section (SettingsView.swift:127-160, lists only ⌘⌥↑/↓/M) nor in HelpView's Keyboard Shortcuts list (HelpView.swift:64-103, lists only ⌘⌥↑/↓/M, ⌘R, ⌘S, ⌘,). No accessibility hint advertises them either. A user cannot discover that 'b' cycles boost or 'p' pins the selected row. | Fix: List the in-row keys (space/m mute, =/- volume, b boost, p pin) in HelpView and/or the Settings keyboard-shortcuts section, or surface them via per-row accessibility hints / a help overlay.
• [MEDIUM/inconsistency] In-list keys ignore the 'Enable keyboard shortcuts' preference that gates the global hotkeys (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MainWindowView.swift:408) — Verified the global hotkey handlers all guard on preferences.enableKeyboardShortcuts (increaseVolumeForFrontmostApp AppStore.swift:1211, decreaseVolumeForFrontmostApp AppStore.swift:1230, toggleMuteForFrontmostApp AppStore.swift:1249). The in-list handleKey helper (MainWindowView.swift:408-414) and its onKeyPress callers (MainWindowView.swift:381-386) call store.setMuted / nudgeVolume / cycleBoost / store.togglePinned with NO enableKeyboardShortcuts guard. So disabling 'Enable keyboard shortcuts' in Settings does not disable space/m/=/-/b/p on the selected row, contradicting the toggle's implied meaning. | Fix: Gate handleKey on store.preferences.enableKeyboardShortcuts (return .ignored when disabled), or clarify in copy that the toggle only governs the global ⌘⌥ hotkeys while in-list keys are always on.
• [LOW/ux] onKeyPress intercepts b/p/m/-/=, defeating List type-select for the selected-row case (Sources/Waves/Features/Mixer/MainWindowView.swift:386) — Verified MainWindowView.swift:381-386 consume 'm', 'b', 'p', '-', '=' as command keys, returning .handled whenever a row is selected (handleKey, MainWindowView.swift:408-414). AppKit/SwiftUI lists support type-select (typing a letter jumps to the next matching row). Because these letters are swallowed when a row is selected, a user cannot type m/b/p to jump to apps starting with M/B/P; the keystroke mutes/cycles-boosts/pins the selected row instead. Note handleKey returns .ignored when no row is selected, so type-select still works before any selection — limiting the impact, hence low severity and 'likely'. | Fix: Require a modifier for the action keys, or scope these handlers so they don't shadow list type-select, or document the tradeoff clearly.
